emails.subject: CE3K, AI-SF book
emails.body:
06-DEC-78 18:11:37,1314
Net mail from site USC-ISI rcvd at 06-DEC-78 18:11:35
Date: 6 DEC 1978 1454-PST
Sender: GEOFF at USC-ISI
Subject: [Mike at Rand-Unix: CE3K, continued]
From: Geoff at SRI-KA (Geoffrey S. Goodfellow)
To: mrc at SAIL,
To: dee at CCA
Message-ID: <[USC-ISI] 6-DEC-78 14:54:12.GEOFF>


Begin forwarded message
===========================
Mail from SRI-KA rcvd at 6-DEC-78 1435-PST
Mail from RAND-UNIX rcvd at 6-Dec-78 1425-PST
From: Mike at Rand-Unix
Date:  6 Dec 1978 at 1423-PST
To: mike at ucla-security,lauren at ucla-security, mclure at kl, geoff at ka
cc:     mike at rand-unix
Subject: CE3K, continued

According to the NY Times, Stephen Spielberg has been given approx.  1
megabuck to shoot 15 minutes worth of CE3K to be included in the re-
release of the movie next year.  These scenes were in the original
script and cut when the money ran out.  At least $250K of it will be
spent on the sequence after Dreyfuss enters the mothership, showing
the interior of the alien spacecraft.  Several sequences in the
current version will be excised to make room for the new material,
including the famous mashed potato scene in which Dreyfuss makes a
mountain out of his spuds.

Trumbull will be doing the special effects.
===========================
End forwarded message

FYI -
08-DEC-78 08:09:12,778
Net mail from site BBN-TENEXD rcvd at 08-DEC-78 08:09:10
Date: 7 Dec 1978 1731-EST
Sender: KALIKOW at BBN-TENEXD
Subject: Amusing 'AI-Sci-Fi' book
From: KALIKOW at BBN-TENEXD
To: AI-Folks:
Message-ID: <[BBN-TENEXD] 7-Dec-78 17:31:59.KALIKOW>

Try "The Adolescence of P-1," by Thomas J. Ryan; MacMillan, 1977.
280 pp.

The story of a Computer Science major and the program he writes
while an undergraduate.  Seems that the program, designed to work
its way into the operating system of time-shared computers and to
access others via any available network connections, is amazingly
successful, gobbling up huge amounts of core, learning and
conversing, adapting and hiding, and defending itself ...

If SciFi is an avocation for you, you might like it.

/Dan
-------

emails.subject: LEAK@MIT-AI 01/05/79 23:41:23 Re: Hardcopy letter ...
emails.body:
LEAK@MIT-AI 01/05/79 23:41:23 Re: Hardcopy letter from Moses to several people
The following letter was sent in hardcopy to JPG, MRG, HIC, RWK
and others via interdepartmental mail:

"	As manager of the MC machine I find it necessary to allow no
new tourist accounts on this machine.

	The primary reasons for this decision are:  (1) My analysis
of the usage statistics of MC indicate that tourists are using far too
much of the machine in detriment to our paying MACSYMA users.
(2) They are taking too much of the time of and placing too much of a
strain on my key personnel in maintaining accounts and in communication
with them.  (3) The sponsors who pay for the machine have made it clear
to me that they do not want us to grant tourist accounts.

	MIT students who desire to use MACSYMA in their courses will
continue to receive accounts with the same hour restrictions
that tourists currently enjoy."

Simultaneously, a similar letter was sent by Martin about the ML machine.

Recent account refusals indicate that this policy has been in
effect for some time.  This suggests that Moses and Martin were
hoping to sneak this by without anyone's noticing.

The MC accounts people are said to be of mixed feelings toward the
policy.  This casts doubt on the reality of (1) and (2).

emails.to: KMP at MIT-MC, KEN at MIT-AI
emails.subject: iota and let-files
emails.body:
KEN@MIT-AI 01/04/79 17:08:45 Re: iota and let-files

    we aught [sic] to get together and talk about it (maybe with a Lisp
    hacker like HIC or GLS) I'm beginning to beleive that the right way is
    like mine but that you require that every variable be bound to a file
    object.  This way the only reason one would type
      (let-files ... (let-files ((foo foo))...)) would be
    that you intended foo to be closed in the inner one.

    also there are other minor differences:
    nointerupt for setqs and (and (filep v) (close v))
    rather than your         (and v (close v))

Hmm. Well, your implementation has a bug. If you aren't in the unwind
protect then if there is an error in a form after the first one, then
a return from the error can't be helped by the no-interrupt. The file
still doesn't get closed. Bug-free code has equivalent effect, but mine
is cleaner since it does the 'right' thing. Perhaps I should do
(AND (FILEP V) (CLOSE V)) tho' since obviously this will avoid screws
of people setting things they oughtn't.

I've considered making IOTA bind a variable implicitly (EOF to a gensym)
for finding read-end-of-file's ... dunno about that. I think that's
un-needed mostly so probably won't. Do you use the third arg to READ ever?
Just curious.

emails.subject: KRD@MIT-AI 12/28/78 11:13:51 Re: hotels in Japan f...
emails.body:
KRD@MIT-AI 12/28/78 11:13:51 Re: hotels in Japan for IJCAI
Date: 27 Dec 1978 1648-PST
Subject: HOTEL ACCOMODATIONS


HOTEL ACCOMODATIONS

There are several possibilities for moderately priced
rooms in Tokyo, in the following categories.  For further details,
sndmsg to ijcai79@sumex-aim.

1.  DOUBLE OCCUPANCY
    A single room in a major hotel can be quite expensive, however
    several of these hotels offer double rooms for Y 8,700- Y 13,000/night.

2.  BUSINESS HOTELS
    Around 20 "business hotels,"  simple hotels lacking restaurants
    and lounges with compact but clean rooms, are located near the
    conference  hall.  These hotels have mostly single rooms ranging from
    Y 3,500 - Y 6,000/night.

3.  LOWER-PRICED, LESS CONVENIENT ROOMS
    For students, there are two youth hostels in Tokyo, charging Y 600 - î
    Y 1050/night.  There is also a special hotel in Tokyo for foreign
    visitors with room  charges from Y 2,800 - Y 4,200/night for singles,
    Y 3,900 - Y 6,400/night for doubles.  For those interested in seeing
    more of Tokyo and staying at least a week, please write for information
    regarding an exchange program.

4.  YMCA
    The Tokyo YMCA is within 15 min. walking distance of the conference
    hall.  A single room is Y 3,600/night.

emails.subject: PRATT@MIT-AI 12/20/78 11:56:44 Re: Programs are Da...
emails.body:
PRATT@MIT-AI 12/20/78 11:56:44 Re: Programs are Data
I want to collect data on the extent to which people take advantage of tLISP's
"programs are data" philosophy, so as to be able to say exactly how useful
the philosophy is and to have examples that I can present in a discussion
of the philosophy.  If you make use of the philosophy, let me know how often,
in what ways, and point me at specific examples in your programs.  If you
don't make use of it, let me know that also, otherwise it will be easy for
people to tell me my data is meaningless because it only samples the
proponents.  I'd also be interested in hearing from anyone with opinions
on the matter, but please put opinions and data in separate messages so I
can keep things reasonably straight.

emails.subject: An old problem has been fixed.
emails.body:
If you have had problems because the XGP inserted unecessary
space between lines (in particular, when trying to use .ls 0),
then things should now work properly.  The problem was that R
used the ";VSP 0" command instead of ";LSP 0".  The difference
between these two commands is that the VSP command forces the
XGP program to assume a line height, and it assumes the height
of font 0.  In effect, the XGP was assuming that every line
contains font 0 characters.  The result was that lines less
tall than font 0 were being spread apart to the font 0 spacing.
The necessary change has been patched into R32 on the ITS machines
to make it use the LSP command instead of the VSP command.

emails.subject: MacLISP's Floating point
emails.body:
MMiller at BBNA, ben at MIT-ML, BUG-LISP at MIT-MC,
    BRoberts at BBNC

Please see my comments on the **'d item below ...
    Date:  9 SEP 1980 0225-EDT
    From: JONL at MIT-MC (Jon L White)

        Date: 8 September 1980 14:03-EDT
        From: Benjamin Kuipers <BEN at MIT-ML>
        TOPS-10 MACLISP still has the problem of using KA-10
        floating point instructions, which must be software
        simulated on the Tufts KL-10.  Are there any plans to
        fix this?
    Not that I know of.   Several other people have complained about
    this -- especially Ken Kahn who is trying to use MacLISP on a
    Swedish TOPS-10 that apparently doesn't support much in hte way of
**  compatibility -- but there's just not a lot of "steam" here on our
**  side to maintain the TOPS-10 version.  Hopefully, some combination
**  of TOPS-10 MacLISP users could delve into these things? (the
    CMUA crew, and maybe someone from Tufts?).

I hate to criticize, since I know everyone there as friends and know that
they do the best that they can, given human time limitations.  Moreover,
the result of years of software development work has been freely
distributed, and for this we should all be grateful.  But this situation,
which I will describe as "The Inferiority of MacLisp Support on non-I.T.S.
Systems" is a very serious problem for MacLisp AND THUS (if you believe, as
I do, that it represents the future whereas InterLisp represents the past)
FOR THE AI COMMUNITY AT LARGE.

The documentation situation is equally embarrassing for MacLisp advocates.

Byron and I, here at TI, have tried hard to "sell" MacLisp, but due to
better documentation and 20X support, INTERLISP may win here in the end.
("Hopefully you users will do it" is, unrealistic outside of the university
setting -- although we ARE TRYING to bootleg some Tops-20 improvements to
MacLisp, like cursorpos and such.)  This is particularly sad since we are
trying to buy (indeed may be within days of ordering) LISPM's.  Those of
you who have only lived in the I.T.S. world cannot appreciate just how
inferior MacLisp is on other operating systems, relative to its state on
I.T.S.  Given the number of sites running I.T.S. (!) this seems a bit
parochial, even if understandable from the MIT point of view.

I don't know what the right answer is, or how to improve the situation.
But ignoring it doesn't help.

One possible solution might be to propagate I.T.S. along with Emacs and
MacLisp.  What is the legal status of this?  Can any site with a 10/20
choose to run that instead (not just universities or just MIT)?  Who would
I ask, to get a tape?  What special hardware configurations, if any, would
be required?  What about updates?  Would it mean no further support from
either DEC or maintenance houses when things crash?  SUPPOSE WE BOUGHT ALL
FUTURE 10/20-class MACHINES FROM FOONLY, INC., INSTEAD OF DEC?  Could THEY
supply I.T.S., and will they help out when an I.T.S.-based machine crashes?

Or, is there any chance that M.I.T. will move over to Tops-20 release N,
after most of the good features of I.T.S. get stolen by DEC?  The version
from CMU, with multi-forking and PCL commands, release 4, is pretty nice;
in some ways nicer (certainly friendlier to learn) than I.T.S.  Mostly what
it lacks is operating system level display support -- each application
(like Emacs) must reinvent the wheel.

Please accept these comments as constructive feedback.  For now, we will
continue to try to improve the Tops-20 user environment; we may even
attempt a manual on the scale of the recent UCI document.  On the other
hand, this task seems so ominous for a new group that we may well have to
go over to INTERLISP in order to get some things running to show off to
management.  At the end of the year, we can't just say "we wrote a manual
for LISP and fixed some bugs specific to the Tops-20 version of our
favorite dialect".

If you have other suggestions, they'd be welcome.
    Regards, Mark

emails.subject: Your long note of last week
emails.body:
Date: 23 September 1980 23:27-EDT
    From: Mark L. Miller <MILLER at MIT-AI>
    Subject: MacLISP's Floating point
    To: JONL at MIT-MC
    cc: KEN at MIT-AI, GJS at MIT-AI, PHW at MIT-AI, TBlocker at BBNA,
	MMiller at BBNA, ben at MIT-ML, BUG-LISP at MIT-MC,
	BRoberts at BBNC
I was hoping to reply more to your note, Mark, but Scott Fahlman's reply
(which is still in my mail file) makes essentially the main point --
that maintaining Maclisp, while useful, is not a forward-looking project
(as NIL is).  However, two things are on the horizon which may help you
and other non-MIT Maclisp users:
1) Kent Pitman has been working on a full Maclisp manual, to be done in
   TEX, and there is some hope that this will be done "shortly".
2) Mike Travers has done a virtual-terminal service for TOPS-20 (on the
   XX machine) which essentially implements the ITS-style control-P
   codes -- thus it should be possible to use the ITS-specific code
   for Maclisp's CURSORPOS (written in MIDAS), and thus achieve on TOPS-20
   the same level of "rubout-ability" and CRT-usabililty  as on ITS.

emails.to: MILLER at MIT-AI, JONL at MIT-MC
emails.subject: Your long note of last week
emails.body:
PHW at MIT-MC, BUG-LISP at MIT-MC, BRoberts at BBNC

Jonl,
   Those two points are VERY encouraging!  Please sign TI up for an
order of 2 dozen manuals (tell me where to send the check!) and
please tell me how to get a Tops-20 Dumper tape of Mike Travers'
20X CRT support goody.  Meanwhile, I'm still a "fan" you know and
the last shot has not been fired.                         Regards, Mark

emails.subject: KL-10 (especially TOPS-10) incompatibilities for MacLISP
emails.body:
I think I've finally fixed that old "snag", the lack of compatible
double-precision floating-point instructions on most KL-10's.  I've
CC'd this note to several people, since  Mark Miller made a rather
long comment about this problem last month:
    Date: 23 September 1980 23:27-EDT
    From: Mark L. Miller <MILLER at MIT-AI>
    Subject: MacLISP's Floating point
    cc: KEN at MIT-AI, GJS at MIT-AI, PHW at MIT-AI, TBlocker at BBNA,
	MMiller at BBNA, ben at MIT-ML, BUG-LISP at MIT-MC,
	BRoberts at BBNC
    .  .  .
So in reply to Bob Meacham's note:
    Date: 16 Oct 1980 0542-PDT
    From: Cjohnson at SUMEX-AIM
    Subject: MAClisp installation at ORNL
    .  .  .
    I almost immediately got the message "?KA10 floating point instruction at
    user PC 441351"  DDT examination revealed the command "FADL  7,10".
    Our 3 CPUs are KL-10s, and we are currently running version 7.01 of the
    TOP-10 monitor.  Under this version, the monitor command "set float on"
    will translate the FADL instruction to something acceptable, but under
    the 7.02 monitor whose pre-release version we will receive in January
    even this temporary fix will disappear.  So that's snag #1, I guess - I
    need to get the FADL (which I guess MIDAS puts in there?) replaced by
    something legal.
    .  .  .
Finally! This is just the information I needed to find out what was
wrong -- its only the lack of a true FADL, FDVL, etc on most KL-10s which
cause thhe problem.  So I've just assembled a new lisp here at MIT-MC which
has a run-time test for being on a KL-10, and uses appropriate versions of
double-precision instructions.  Likely, by tomorrow this time, I'll have
the TOPS-10 and TOPS-20 versions updated, and hoopefully some redistribution
can begin.  As for your other complaint:
    Now snag #2 is more mysterious to me.  When I did "set float on" and then
    .  .  .
    ?Illegal UUO at user PC 436207
    DDT revealed 436206 was HRRZ  12,0(12)  and 436207 was just 0,,436211
    .  .  .
	    Cordially, Robert Meacham
Most likely this is due to an administrative limit on the maximum size of
"core" (really, "address space") used by a job on your TOPS-10 system --
many system managers set a ridiculously low limit, which makes it impossible
to load up any significant application into MacLISP; but it shouldn't matter
since you are no doubt running the VM system.  True, it is a bug that MacLISP
doesn't die more gracefully, but there are so many more important things
to work on first ....

emails.subject: delegation vs. referral
emails.body:
After kicking around the terminology
with people for a while, I have not detected
any great groundswell of support for
using "refer" instead of "delegate".

However we should emphasize more that the first
delegation is special because the client remains
the same by default through successive delegations.

Do any of you fell strongly about the question?

Cheers,

Carl

I've been travelling around the world and just got back and got your message

    Date: 25 Sep 1979 2131-PDT
    From: HPP.GENESERETH at SU-SCORE


    I'm teaching the AI course at Stanford this quarter, and I need
    a good paper on the representation of time.  I thought of your
    masters thesis, but it's pretty long.  Isthere anything shorter?
    Other good references  Larry Fagan has just completed a PHD on
    the subject here.  I don't know anything about it yet though.
    What about a reference (paper) on your aesthetics stuff?  I'm more
    interested in technique at the monet than philosophy.

    How are things going?
    -------

I'll send a longer message later.
Gorry and I wrote a AI Journal paper on time August 1977 that I would recommend.
As to aesthetics stuff my thesis (tr should be out now or soon) is the best bet.

The trip was great!

emails.from: EBM at MIT-DMS (J. Eliot B. Moss)
emails.subject: R bug fix, and Macro package change
emails.body:
R bug fixed:  .tr and .tc did not reclaim all the storage they
	should, which caused an R that used them a lot to grow to
	immense sizes.  This has been fixed, and there is no change
	in functionality, only in memory usage.

Macro package change:
	The figure stuff has been changed yet again, this time to
reduce the amount of white space appearing before the line separating
figures and footnotes.  (This white space grew as a side-effect of a
recent bug fix.)  The difference will probably not be visible on the XGP,
but will make LPT output look somewhat better.  Also, the previous fixes
to figures have been carried over to footnotes.  Unless I receive bug
reports, this stuff should be stable for a long time.

NOTE: This new R has been installed as NR20 on XX and NR on ITS.
Unless I hear objections, I will promote both on Mon.

emails.subject: a precedent broken
emails.body:
Yesterday I was on the phone in the computer center when four kids
got into a fight about who would get to use the terminal, and after
wrestling for a while they managed to crash the system, and then
as soon as I got off the phone they started in on how it was the
other guy's fault, and I had about a thousand things to do that day,
so instead of massaging their backs and listening to their tales
of woe I just threw all of them out for the rest of the day, amid
a chorus of "that's not fair!"  Everyone was amazed, because I've
put up with much more annoying activities without doing anything
to them and they're not used to having me act like a teacher.
Perhaps they'll learn to be able to figure out when I'm too busy
to be bothered with them.

Yesterday was also the last day of school for seniors.  That led
to a good deal of rowdyism all over the school.  (Earlier in the
week the seniors let the air out of the tires of all the school
buses one morning; apparently there is a tradition that there must
be a Senior Prank.  I haven't heard the word "prank" in a long
time--been hanging out too much with people who say "hack"!)

Yesterday was also the day we had a meeting with Dan Curtin of
Digital Leasing Corp.  If you want to buy a computer from DEC
and pay it off over time, they send you to Digital Leasing,
which is in fact not a part of DEC although DEC set it up in the
first place.  And they have a close relationship in practice.
Anyway, this guy had been in before, with documents for a
five-year lease agreement, and we arranged a meeting to draw up
the final documents now that the configuration is settled.
Last time around he had left off a copy of the agreement which
was missing page two, so we didn't know exactly what it said.
Turns out that it said that after the five years, after we've
paid off the cost of the system and then some, we still didn't
own it!  We would have the option of paying an extra 10% to
buy it at that point.  They hadn't mentioned that before.  So
we said well in that case we'll borrow the money from our
bank, see you around, and he said "Oh, you want a Conditional
Sales Agreement!"  It seems they have this other kind of deal
where you do end up owning the machine, and the interest is
cheaper to boot.  Naturally it hadn't occurred to the turkey
to mention it before.  Grrrr.  So we're starting all over on
filling out forms and sending them to the lawyers.  This guy
is a real turkey.  Not only that, he is the only person I ever
met who has an HP calculator (the business kind) and can't
figure out how to use a non-HP (i.e. non-RPN) one!  Not only
that, he smokes.  Turkey Of The Month.

I was even wearing my "thank you for not smoking near me" t-shirt.
Which was also ignored by David Levington, my boss, who smokes a
pipe.  After the meeting I went back to the computer center and
the first thing that happened was that one of the kids said to
me "I can tell by the smell that they didn't pay any attention
to your t-shirt."  So I've written a memo on the subject of how
it will make my job harder if the kids don't want to be near me
because I'm carrying around his stink.

Oh, speaking of meetings, Tuesday I went to a School Committee
meeting to talk about the computer system.  It was a farce.  First
they talked about the Executive Intern program, and spent an hour
and a half listening to students who had been in the program talk
about how wonderful it was.  Then they spent two hours talking
about Discipline: they got a report from the building superintendent
about how many thousand dollars we spend each month on repairing
vandalism, with pretty color graphs on transparencies, then they
heard from the Assistant Principal and the Hall Directors about
how many suspensions there were this year in each of 40 different
categories.  (They had ordered the school to tighten up on
discipline, and specifically to suspend kids more.  To their
credit, the A.P. and hall directors told them politely but firmly
that they thought it was all a bad idea.)  By the time that was
over it was after 11 pm so nobody wanted to spend another two
hours talking about the computer.  Instead they talked about the
paperwork.  It seems we have to let people bid on supplying the
computer, even though we know nobody will beat DEC's offer, and
we had already advertised the bid.  The committee passed a
resolution authorizing us to advertise it, which we had already
done.  They didn't want to authorize us to sign the lease (this
was before we found out from the Turkey about the conditional
sales agreement) until the bidding was over--something about it
not being legal or something.  So they are going to have a special
meeting in a week, after the bids are in, to sign the papers.
It was all pretty obnoxious because we had invited a couple of
people from the town who had been working with us on planning
the configuration and all that to come talk to the committee
with me, so we all had to sit around all evening waiting to not
have the discussion after all.  But in any case, they have
in principle approved spending the money, even though they didn't
sign anything this time around, so it's all settled, it says here.

Oh, another contributing factor to yesterday is that one kid
came to my 10 am algebra class smelling of alcohol.  It says in
books that kids don't drink in the morning unless there is
something very wrong going on in their lives, so that got me
worried too.  Quite a day, yesterday.

But afterwards I took a kid home because he wanted to hear
all my wonderful 1960s records.  So I think I've created
another Kinks fan.

emails.to: PRATT at MIT-MC, MACRAK at MIT-MC
emails.subject: Replies to ` query
emails.body:
Soon there will be a new BACKQ package for maclisp, which willl
have the option of generating "macroified" code, with the name
|`-expander/|| as a primary eval-time macro.  This option will
be exercised if BACKQUOTE-EXPAND-WHEN is "EVAL" (or in general
anything except "READ), and otherwise the expansion will occur
at read-in time just as at present.  Three other internal markers:
|-,-/||  |-,@-/||  and |-,.-/||    The latter one is for NCONC
instead of APPEND, and thus we can have `(A ,@B)  generate
(APPEND A B ()), thus copying B, but `(A ,.B)  generate
(APPEND A B).  Note that `(,.A B) would generate (NCONC A B).
[this idea, for ",." comes from RBR, I think].
GRINDEF would print out a form just like it was read-in, using
the READMACROINVERSE feature;  however, there is a flaw in this
feature in that readmacros won't appear in the CDR part of
a list;  thus you never see '(A . 'B)  grindef'd out that way.
For the same reason, it would be somewhat of a pain to get
`(A . ,B) to grindef out that way, but `(A ,@B) and `(A ,.B)
both win.  Note the weird commutation:  `(A . ,B) works the
same as `(A ,. B)  ??
All the aforesaid "markers" and macros would be on the obarray,
so that any PRINTing or GRINDEFing (regardless of the success of
the READMACROINVERSE attempts)  would be readable back in with
no loss of functionality.
Any further comments?

emails.subject: series 8 cold load
emails.body:
the series 8 cold load is making its appearance on
various machines.
--------------------------
[1]  Meters work differently.  **incompatible change**
[2]  LMIO1;XGP is an initial part of the system.  This
     means ESC-Q always available.
[3]  PKG-LOAD, LOAD-FILE-ALIST, and COMPILE-FILE-ALIST
     are available in a form which takes a functional argument.
[4]  The usual accumulation of bug-fixes, etc, installed.
[5]  A tool exists to help track down the source of
     "symbol appears more than once in the cold load" errors.
     Look in LISPM;COLDF if you ever need to use this.
[6]  The initial facility which allows you to augment the microcode
     incrementally is operational.  Brave pioneers (who expect
     to get arrows in the back) should see RG.

[1] Meters  (ie counts of various events maintained by the
microcode) work differently.  They used to be available
as variables, ONE-Q-FORWARDed to A-Memory.
  Problem was, 24 bits wasnt enough.  Carries into the
data-type field happened,  eventually
crashing the system when the GC looked at the corrupted
data.  In the new scheme, the meters are no longer directly
accessible.  Instead there exists (READ-METER <meter-name>)
and (WRITE-METER <meter-name>).  The meter names are the
same as before, but note they are in the SI: package.
Meters are now a full 32 bits, thus, READ-METER can return
a BIGNUM.  MOON;LMETER has been updated to use the new scheme.

[2] YEA.

[3] SI:PKG-LOAD-MAP, SI:LOAD-FILE-ALIST-MAP, and SI:COMPILE-FILE-ALIST-MAP
exist (as of now, they're not in GLOBAL, in the future they will be).
These all take a functional argument in the first argument position,
followed by arguments as in the base form.  Anytime it is decided to "do"
anything (compile or load a file), the functional argument is called,
with arguments indicating what is to be done.  Its arranged so that if
the functional argument is (FUNCTION FUNCALL), the entire effect is exactly
like the base form.

emails.subject: R bug fixed
emails.body:
A bug which caused a blank line and .sp to have different effects
when causing a trap has been fixed.  It manifested itself in the
form of blank pages after figures whose insertion was caused by
a trap set off by a blank line.  A blank line and .sp are now
entirely equivalent.  The old NR was flushed, and this fixed R
put in as NR in its place.  If I don't get bug reports, I will
promote it to R in a few days.
-------

emails.subject: plans
emails.body:
How are your plans shaping up for next year?  If you're going to be in
Stockholm, you would be most welcome to come visit in Edinburgh.  (Actually,
you can visit even if you don't go to Sweden ...)  We already have arrangements
for a flat, and it has a spare guest bedroom.  Will be leaving in mid-August ...
it's the cheap budget fare, which doesn't allow any stopovers, so unfortunately I
won't be able to stop in Boston on the way.

The conference in La Jolla sounds good - I was thinking about going to it, but
decided instead to leave for Edinburgh in time to be there during the Festival.

Haven't been doing anything with ThingLab lately.  For the past several weeks
I've been hacking on a decompiler that takes Smalltalk byte codes and
reconstitutes the source text.  The immediate application is on the Notetaker,
where there isn't any room to store the sources.  Some other applications might
involve trying an alternative syntax for Smalltalk, e.g. a graphical or
template-oriented one.  When the decompiler is done, however, I have to go back
and crank out a tech report.  The results of my comparative study are that
hacking is more fun than doing theses or writing reports.
	Alan

emails.subject: ELLEN@MIT-MC 05/29/79 13:57:55 Re: P.S. about pean...
emails.body:
ELLEN@MIT-MC 05/29/79 13:57:55 Re: P.S. about peanut butter (long (but funny) message)
Steve Lammert from CMU recounts the following story which happened to the
MIT student who transported his peanut butter to CMU:

The scene -- Logan International Airport

Our hero, not wishing to trust UPS or the TWA baggage men with his treasure,
has successfully made his way through the X-ray machine with a large white
tub.  Briefcase in one hand and handle of tub in the other, he is hustling
toward his gate for a plane that is about to leave him stranded in Boston.

Suddenly, from behind him, he hears the sound of running feet and a voice
shouting, "HEY!  STOP!  You, with the tub!  STOP!"

Our hero stops, amidst a mixed feeling of curiosity, impatience, and quiet
fear.  He is approached by a man in a dark suit and dark glasses, who flashed
a badge from somewhere in his hand.

"Airport security.  What's in the tub?"

Somewhat exasperatedly, our hero replys "Twenty-five pounds of peanut butter."

"What?"

"Twenty-five pounds of peanut butter.  You want a taste?  Or maybe you'd
like to probe inside it with your forearm.  Here, I'll open it for you..."

"Uh, no thanks.  Really, we were just....ah, doing our job.  You a student
here?"

"Yup."  Our hero picks up the peanut butter and dashes for his plane,
but as he leaves the scene, he hears the security man mutter:
"...must be one of those kids from M.I.T....peanut butter.  Sheesh!"

emails.subject: JKESS@MIT-MC 05/31/79 15:23:03 Re:  GLP paper mess...
emails.body:
JKESS@MIT-MC 05/31/79 15:23:03 Re:  GLP paper message from someone calling himself fucked

    fucked@MIT-MC (Sent by ___032@MIT-MC) 05/31/79 04:07:28
    you fuckingbastard, that HUMOUR printout you did used up the
    last paper in the GLPT! and I wanted my pritout before going home! now!!!!

I don't know who to blame for this -- sorry to all other users, but
it requires a reply.

1) Protocol states that all unsigned messages shall be ignored. If you
have something to say, then feel free to say it, but give the recipient
a chance to respond.

2) Such language is non-optimal; it conveys no useful data.

3) Paper in transfinite quantities is stored under the desks next to our
IMLAC. Replacement is trivial; if you can't figure it out, ask someone.

4) As yet, no remote low-paper warning is available, else I would have
come over to the office and replaced the paper. I was working from the
stud center, and intended to pick up my output today.

If you wish to discuss this further, please contact me -- but sign your
mail. This kind of broadcast note is an annoyance to all users -- I resent
being forced to use it to reach you.

					Joseph Jay Kesselman Jr.
					Keshlam the Seer
					Knight of Randomness
					Hacker of Plasma
					And slightly ticked off.

zvona@ai (Sent by ___006) 04/30/79 19:48:42 Re: heuristics for drawing (long message)
To: ken at MIT-AI
CC: aesthetics at MIT-AI
i recently got a copy of The Big Yeloow Drawing Book by Dan, Marian,
& Hugh O'Neill. I am rather excited by it, because it appeas
to be essentially a set of explanations of the basic rules
that are used by artists to acheive a given effect. In the words of the book:

This is a different drwing book...It presents the concept that
	drawing is the basis of fine art and that the basic principles
	of good drawing have been defined.Because these principles
	have been defined, they must be learned in order to  present a three
	dimentional illusion on a two dimentional piece of paper.
	In writining the book the suspicion arose thsat the
	undefinable mysticism of art should be rejected. The tired old
	concept that discovery is the only approach to art is considered
	inefficient.

the book goes on the present techniques, starting from the very basics,
for drawing, concentrating on conveying emotions.
(dan o'neill is a cartoonist.) the neat thing is that he shows how easily
emotions are conveyed by very simple, symbolic pictures:

	All cartoons are signals, exacvtly the way stop signs, red lights,
	green lights are signals. signals give us information: stop, go,
	slow down.
	The cartoon character has a signal on its face: an expression!
 This expression
	tells us the character is feeling an emotion. the way the character
	looks tells us what it fels, and that is much more than the words
	the character will speak.
	The signals are simple. get your self a soft lead pencil and turn
	the page...

because the techniques he presents are so simple, i think it would
be relatively easy (ie a merely herculean labor)
to extend ANNI to producing o'neills expressive faces, given the pre-
existing emotional machinery.

emails.subject: Journal Entry
emails.body:
I have flushed the TEMACS macro library.  Instead there are now the libraries:
SCRLIN - macros for moving over screen lines instead of text lines.
         Automatically sets up ^A, ^E, ^N, ^P.  On non-display consoles,
         these macros revert to text line movers.
DELIM  - macros for moving over balanced pairs of []{}()<>"'.
         Sets up C-[, C-], C-(, C-), etc. to move over pairs (forward or back).
JLKMAC - Mostly defines setup hooks for JLK.

Anybody currently using TEMACS probably wants to simply load SCRLIN or DELIM.
Other people who wanted to try out screen line moving or delimiter moving,
but didn't want to get screwed by JLK's hacks, now have a chance.

emails.subject: Gazillions of tiny ARC files
emails.body:
I wrote some EMACS code to copy directly from the arc file to disk files
without going thru the system ARC device handler.  In a test using a link
from my data directory to MC:LLOGO;AR2 INTRO, attempting to barf out ALL
of the 190 files, it took 27 secs of runtime to get thru 170 of them
before it lost with directory full.  If/when you are ready to re-R them,
in your EMACS do MM Run Lib$LLOGO;ARCDSK which will lead you by the
hand to specify (1) what arc file, and (2) selection of which files
you don't want by killing in a recursive ^R.

As I'm sure you are aware, ITS directories don't really have enough room
to store a moby archiv file, as well as hundreds of DSK duplicates of
the contents.  Probably the way to win is to use a (nearly) empty
directory and put a link there to the real arc file.

The ARC device handler locks the arc file lots, so if any job has a
channel open to that archive, the EMACS code can't open the arc file
to read it directly.  It waits after reading the usual directory from
the ARC device before it can get the file open for direct reading.
Directory Full is not recoverable in my code, so beware.

emails.from: EBM at MIT-DMS (J. Eliot B. Moss)
emails.subject: Macro arguments
emails.body:
After considerable thought, and discussion with several people,
I have come up with a new proposal for inline and regular macro
invocation.  See R;INLINE INVOKE.  This form would be incompatible
wherever a macro argument started with a '{'.  ('{' and '}' are
a new form of quotes for macro arguments.)  In addition, that file
discusses a new form of insertion of macro arguments and string
registers that puts backslashes back on control characters (its
merits are discussed there, too).

Note: as of now there is a bug in the ^A(expr) macro argument
insertion form, which will be corrected soon.

Get comments on the proposal back to me soon, since I would
like to fix the bug and install the argument parsing stuff
at the same time, within a few days.

emails.subject: ZWEI initialization functions
emails.body:
I got annoyed with the habit of ZWEI of starting up with an initial buffer
with package USER and a random directory in the filename.  Thus, my init
file DOYLE;LISPM > contains two functions, SET-INITIAL-FILENAME-DEFAULT,
which takes a filename (e.g. "FOO > DOYLE; AI:") and makes it the initial
default filename, and SET-INITIAL-PACKAGE-DEFAULT, which takes a package
name and makes that the package initially seen in ZWEI.  The latter function
hacks a slot in an obscure ZWEI data structure, so I hope that it can be
made a standard function to avoid lossage in the future if the implementation
of the editor changes.  I wish that LOGIN automatically called the first
function, since it only depends on the user's login name, typically.

letters.recipient:
American Scientist
345 Whitney Ave.
New Haven, CT 06511
letters.body:
I found Dr. Bennett's article, "How Artificial is Intelligence?" to be very
entertaining.  There were, however, a few glaring technical errors and unjustified
claims were made at the conclusion.  On several occasions Dr. Bennett alludes to the limitation
that memory imposes on high-order correlation studies.  He reasons that a fourth-order
correlation matrix would require 28 5  or about 17 million words of memory.  If one
takes advantage of the fact that a vast majority of the entries are zero by using
hash tables the memory requirements would be much less.  Hash tables require memory
only for those array elements that are needed, in this case non-zero elements.
Given the fact that English strongly restricts the number of possible letters after
a sequence of text the memory requirements would not grow very fast with an increase in
the order of matrices.
Dr. Bennett seems to imply that what is needed for creativity is high-order
correlation matrices.  It is interesting to note that as the order gets high, to say
ten or twenty then the originality of the response of a monkey simulation is very very i low. r
If the previous string of letters was "to be or " then there will be a very small
probability of the simulation continuing with anything other than "not to be that is the
question" regardless of the amount of text the matrices were generated from.
The possibility of simulating human intelligence on a computer is an exciting idea.
The notion that Dr. Bennett presents at the conclusion that correlation matrices of string
data such as words, sentences, shapes, concepts and so on might lead to some success is a
step in the right direction but doomed to failure.  If one contemplates the size of the
correlation matrices one would need and the large number of possible sentences or concepts
and the difficulty of ever filling them with much data, the suggestion seems ludicrous.
The fundamental flaw of article is to suppose that intelligence and creativity have any
basis in random numbers.  On the contrary, there is every reason to suppose that people
make choices not by simulating coin tosses but by applying many complex rules to a very
large amount of knowledge.   You can not ask a simulation of the sort described in the
article why it choose one word over another, what the sentence it just typed meant,
what its purpose was in writing what it did.  Great works of literature and art are not
merely rare statistical fluctuations i nor r are they simply the products of correlation
matrices, but are the product of many careful, thoughtful, and knowledgable decisions.

letters.recipient:
Professor Douglas Hofstadter
Computer Science Department
101 Lindley Hall
Bloomington, Indiana 47401
letters.body:
I finally finished your book.  I apologize for both the wear and tear it took and the
length of time it took me to return it.  While I liked it very much I feel funny praising it
since I was there when you talked about Guy's letter and I'm afraid mine won't match up.
Maybe I should start with little picky things just to get them out of the way.  I
took notes about such things though some I can't find the part that the note refers to.  A
minor gripe is the lack of page numbers makes this a little bit difficult.  For example, I
had a note that you had an extra ~ in the prudence vs. imprudence section, yet I just spent
10-15 minutes and couldn't find it.   Oh well, I feel funny about this (complaining about
little things when I liked it and have some higher-level comments (later)).  At one point in
Chapter 5 you say that infinite recursion couldn't even run much less be useful.  Well I write
infinitely recursive graphic procedures frequently and they don't stop but they eventially
draw such small vectors (or so far from the origin) that the stops changing.  Its because they
do a side-effect before recursing that it makes sense.
At one point you talked about messages with respect to communication with
extra-terestrials.  I enjoyed it but you seemed have missed the idea of what can be taught.
For example, Freudenthal's book on LINCOS which describes how to communicate a mathematical
language with a minimum of assumptions about the recepient is pretty convincing.  Marvin and I
have talked a bit (and once planned to write a paper) about how to communicate by transmitting
programs and how the programming language is transmitted and boot strapped.
Fred Hoyle's novel "A for Andromeda" inspired us.
I wasn't completely convinced about the Crab's inability to build a perfect record
player.  The analogy with Godel, etc. is very good but what bothers me is that part of your
arguement hinges on the assumption that there will always be an "unchanged part" that the
record can destroy.  I fail to see why the player couldn't inspect the record and build
a record-player builder that could just as well self-destruct after building a player suited
for the about-to-be-played record and that such a record player could know enough to build
another record-player builder.  The "unchanged part" can be in the "software" sort of.
At the end of Chapter 17, you bring up Epimenides paradox in a neural connection.  One
possibility that I wonder if you've considered is that the meaning of a sentence is the
procedures that it evokes (and their effects) (ala SHRDLU).  Well the interpretation for
this paradox would be that the sentence means a trace of it being evaluated (ie the thoughts
it evokes when one hears it and tries to determine its validity -- exploring
alternatives, etc.).
What I took to be a typo in "The Magnificrab, Indeed" was where you have
n   n n
a + b  =   c for n = 0
I assume you meant n = 2.  Another probable typo is when you refer to concept network #47
I think you meant 49.
I liked your discussion of Samuel's statement that machines can't be original.
As I remember it though you seemed to have only half of the arguement against it.  Not only is
the lowest level inviolet since it is just physics so too does the very highest-level have a
special status that is very important for understanding all this.  Bugs aside, the top level
criteria for making choices cannot be overridden.  They may be changed but since the change is
evaluated with regard to these criteria the new decision procedure must also reflect the
original criteria.  This assumes that there are no strange loops in the criteria themselves
which there need not be.  This relatively immutable top-level is relevant to discussions of
originality and free-will.  Speaking of this I was surprised to find Minsky's paper
on Minds, Models and Machines neither referenced nor the ideas in it brought into the
discussion.
What I liked most about your book was how well you explained so many
strangely-convuluted ideas.  Many times I had the feeling that I had been thinking those ideas
for quite a while but you managed to articulate them so well.  Along the same lines I would
highly recommend the book to lay people interested in the philosophical side of AI.  The
dialogs it goes without saying were great, both in and of themselves and the way they
illustrated the text.  I liked the last dialog and it reminded me (and Julie and Henry Minsky
who I showed it too) of a story I wrote a few years ago.  I just got it off the data computer
and thought you might enjoy it.
Thanks for loaning me the book.  Looking forward to get my own copy soon.  Feel free
to write (I have more thoughts about the book but would rather discuss them than write them).

letters.recipient:
Luciana Migliavacca
The iconographic research
Edizioni Scientifiche e Techniche
Mondadori
Segrate (Milano)
Casella postale 1824
20100 Milano
Italy
letters.body:
I received your letter today.
Fortunately, I received your telegram before I had a chance to make another set of copies.
.New_paragraph
You asked for some information as to how the slides were made.
The computer (a DEC PDP10) controls a color television set.
It splits the screen into about a quarter of a million points, where each point can be one of
two billion colors (though the computers memory limits me to only sixteen different colors at
any one time).
Rather than program at the level of setting the color of the points, I programmed in Lisp
and sometimes Director.
Both allow me to describe objects and how they should be transformed at a much more conceptual
level.
.New_paragraph
The symmetrical spiral-like ones made use of what is called a "turtle".
Turtles respond to messages asking them to go forward or turn right or to change the
color of the trail they leave behind as they move.
The basic idea of this set is to have the turtle go forward, turn an angle that is very close
to,
but not exactly, a divisor of the circle, and then repeat but each time going forward a little
bit more than before.
The color of the "pen" is changed frequently.
.New_paragraph
Another large group were made by describing two objects to the computer and then instructing
it to draw a smooth transition between them.
Others were made by drawing simple figures (e.g. circles) that were very much larger than the
dimensions of the screen.
The computer was instructed to continue lines that reached the edge starting on the opposite
edge.
.New_paragraph
If you have any more questions feel free to write and ask.
I hope this is understandable and of help.
When the book in published I would appreciate being able to see it, at least any section that
may include my slides.
Thank you.

The University of Southwestern Louisiana

letters.body:
I am sorry I have taken so long to respond to your last letter.  I have been thinking
about your suggestion that we collaborate on a computer drawn sound track.  For the next half
year, at least, I will be too busy with my thesis to consider it, after that who knows?  I
did find out that Information International, Inc. did build a very sophisticated system to do
just that.  The quality of the sound was limitted only by the optical reader of the
projector.  I haven't been able to find out if any such films were made though I do have very
vague memories of such films.
You asked me if I have any thoughts as to why so few artists and animators use
computers.  I just finished writing a short article for the University Film Study Center
Newsletter that deals with just that question.  It should be out soon and if you have any
difficulty getting a copy I will send you one.  Briefly the reasons
are mostly historical --- until very recently computer systems were too expensive and
inaccessible and none were designed very well for naive users.  Collaborations are difficult
to form and make work and artists, like most of the population, are computer illiterates.
From what I see of the educational system this is in the process of changing.  Even this
week's Time magazine seems to be saying this.
Thanks for the offer and if we are both still interested when I finish my
dissertation we should look into it carefully.

letters.recipient:
Robert Russett
Box 4 - 1097
The University of Southwestern Louisiana
Lafayette, Louisiana 70504
letters.body:
Thank you for your letter in August regarding a film
I had in the Sinking Creek Festival.
In the postscript of that letter you asked me to clarify my comment about the discussion of
the future of animation in your book.  While I enjoyed very much
the sections on Knowlton, VanDerBeek, Whitney, Foldes, and Emshwiller, I found lacking any
discussion of what I and many others see as the future of animation especially with regard to
computers.  Two very important trends seemed to be neglected.  One is the view of people such
as Alan Kay (see his September Scientific American article for example), Nicholas Negroponte
and Seymour Papert that soon (less than five years) very sophisticated computer systems capable
of producing animation will be very common and inexpensive.  Negroponte's article "The return
of the Sunday painter" in which he writes of computer systems that will be responsive and easy
to use resulting in many people producing computer animation as a hobby.  Another interesting
aspect of this view is that computer animation will become an integrated part of everyday life.
A simple example is Negroponte's idea of "video wallpaper" that can display images ranging
from very realistic
scenic views to complex dynamic abstractions in addition to being able to replicate the
appearance of any wallpaper currently available.  Seymour Papert has written much about how
to make these systems so accessible, so easy to program, that an average elementary school
student can quickly produce very impressive results.
The other trend is that computer systems are becoming more competent,
more knowledgable, thereby allowing one to interact with them at a much high level of
description.  As computer system became more powerful, they advanced from being a tool of the
animator to being a medium.   As the computers' abilities continue to increase, it becomes
more sensible to view computers as collaborators with the animators rather than as a medium.
To help make these ideas a little more concrete, I have included an article that I
recently completed about computer animation and a paper about how I think one should go about
teaching the computer about animation.  This latter subject is the topic of my doctoral
dissertation which I am currently pursuing.
Looking forward to hearing your comments.

letters.body:
I am sorry I took so long to respond to your letter.  Regarding your question about
computer animation facilities in this area, you are probably familiar with them all.  At MIT
there is the Architecture Machine Group of Negroponte and the Artificial Intelligence lab
here.  The Center for Advanced Visual Studies has very little going on in animation (the only
thing I know of is a project involving the animation of leaves).
Harvard has very little going on other than a group interested in maps.   The
Carpenter Center is very good place for personal animation but they know nothing about
computers.  Currently George Griffen is teaching there, when I took it Suzan Pitt-Kranning
and Mary Beams were there, next year who knows?
While I am interested in computer music I know very little.  There are at least
three groups: Barry Vercoe (who I don't know though I
know well a couple of people that work with him) Jeanne Bamberger who I see from time to time
but have troubles understanding her, and there is a spin-off group of a language processing
group that has a system that Greg Gargarian (Logo's secretary who is also a composer) uses.
I too thought that Smoliar's work on labanotation would be useful for what I'm doing
but it didn't turn out to be.  As to my own work, if you didn't get any of my working papers
last October then I'll send them along.  Three papers are in the works right now, but it will
be a month or two before they are complete and printed.  I'll send them to you when they are.
By the way did you see the Technology Review article Henry and I wrote on computer animation?
It was the October-November 1977 issue and should be in most libraries.
Also you might want to get in touch with Richard Steiger who is a graduate student
here doing his thesis on a computational theory of music.  He is also very interested in
computer animation.  His office is 939, the same building my address.
One last thing,  I heard once about someone (whose name I forgot) who was "artist in
residence" at SAIL and did some interesting work in computer animation.  Do you know anything
about that?

would like to be paid.
I would be more than happy to put together and teach a course on AI.
Probably an introductory survey course would be most appropriate.
My current research interests include many inter-related topics.
I am working on (and hope to continue) applying ideas and techniques in AI to computer
graphics and animation.
I am most interested in the creative aesthetic aspects of this.
Closely related to this is my work on Director which is
a computer language for both AI and graphics designed to be used by people of all levels
of programming experience.

and I will generate a more detailed proposal.

letters.recipient:
Professor Ron Baecker
Department of Computer Science
University of Toronto
Toronto, Ontario m5s1a4
letters.body:
Thank you for an interesting time in Toronto.
Nothing much new at my end about job offers.
The Papert-Kay-Holt panel discussion was quite interesting.
The consensus was schools should go away (or at least become much more passive)
and that stirred up quite a lively debate.
.New_paragraph
I have a few comments about your paper "Towards an effective characterization of graphical
interaction".
I enjoyed it and thought it clear and convincing.
However, part of it seemed to be addressed at some stupidities in the Core report that seem
so obvious.
I guess it had to be said but personally I find it so obvious that for reasonable
efficiency you can't be graphic device independent that I find it hard to believe that people
are seriously arguing for that.
Another kind of minor complaint concerns the examples about "x15=xa+50".
While at one point there is a footnote saying it is the semantics that counts not the syntax
it did seem to be terrible syntax.
Also from my point of view it seems not too fair to compare demonstrative pointing with
cartesian since there are better symbolic specifications (e.g. turtle geometry, constraints).
.New_paragraph
While I agreed with your list of characteristics of good interactive techniques, I found
the elements of the list to be many different types and level of abstraction.
For example, number 8 and 9 need alot more elaboration and seem to be of a different type than
say 6.
I guess my biggest complaint is that I had hopes of some very general principles or at least
an explication of what it means for a techniques to be "natural, easy to learn , not
cumbersome..."
I agree that the problem is how to let the user "focus on the application, not the
communication" and you give many clues and examples how to do it but somehow I think that
something deeper can be said about this.
.New_paragraph
I hope this might be some help.
I basically like the paper its just I thought you could benefit more from criticism than
praise.
.New_paragraph
Enclosed are the receipts from my trip.
The plane ticket costs $141.10 and the hotel was $50.25 (US dollars) totalling
191.35.

Stanford, California 94305
letters.body:
Enclosed is my IJCAI paper.
Its id number is k-089.
.New_paragraph
Regarding the incorporation of reviewers' comments.
One of the reviewers thought the paper was acceptable as it was originally submitted
and had no suggestions for revisions.
The other had vague complaints which have been taken into account.
For example, despite the need to cut the paper's length in half, several places in the
text I have tried to make the connection to " italics(aesthetic) choice-making" more
explicit.
One complaint was that I wrote of how my system makes decisions rather than describing
what it does.
Since making decisions is precisely what my system does, I fail to see how one could
comply with such a suggestion.
Finally, he complains that I do not address the questions I raise in the beginning:
"how knowledge is represented, organized, and used".
On the contrary, most of the paper is about how the knowledge is used in making
choices and how it is organized.
.New_paragraph
I hope you find everything satisfactory.
Thank you.

letters.recipient:
Dr. Nomi Feldman
Conference Coordinator
2321 Morena Blvd.
San Diego, California, 92110
letters.body:
I am planning on attending your conference on Cognitive Science.
I have enclosed the $25 registration fee.
Accommodations will not be necessary, as I plan to stay with friends.
Thank you.

letters.recipient:
Professor Sten-Ake Tarnlund
Institutionen for ADB
Kungl. Tekniska Hogskolan
Stockholms Universitet
S-106 91 Stockholm, Sweden
letters.body:
Hi, I received your letter.
I have decided to turn down some offers elsewhere and am betting on either
you or MIT to come through.
Since MIT will take two weeks minimum and more likely a month to come up with an
offer, I probably can wait until late July or early August.
One other constraint is that I am planning on going around the world in August.
I'll leave Boston around August 6th, go to the cognitive science conference
in La Jolla, California a week later and then to IJCAI.
(Will you be at IJCAI?)
From there I plan to travel around Asia and India and will be in Europe in mid-September.
If all works out between us, I could then go to Stockholm directly.
In any case, I will need to have made plans for next year before I leave Boston.
.New_paragraph
Regarding your pending budget decision, when precisely do you expect it to be made?
Is there a chance it will be made before August?
What do you guess the odds are that it will work out in our favor?
.New_paragraph
Hope to hear from you soon.

letters.recipient:
Howard Austin
c/o Schlumberger - Doll Research Center
Old Quarry Road
Ridgefield, Conn. 06877
letters.body:
Good talking to you on the phone earlier today.
Enclosed is my resume.
If you have any questions or want copies of any of my papers, just ask.
For references contact Carl Hewitt (my thesis advisor),
Marvin Minsky and Seymour Papert.
.New_paragraph
Thanks for your interest and looking forward to visiting you and your lab.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.body:
.sec Introduction
	Suppose you have Logo draw two images and you are interested in the
series of drawings that gradually transform the first drawing into the second.  Suppose
you have a circle and a square then the following might be close to what you have in
mind:
.begin group
.skip 9
.end once indent 0,0
You might be doing this as an exercise in turtle geometry
with varying
degrees of sophistication or you just want to produce interesting drawings.
Or you may be animating and desire smooth transitions between parts of drawings, for
example, you have a mouth that smiles and one that frowns and you want the face to
gradually transform from one to the other.
	There are, however, very many interpolations between two drawings.  Suppose in
the previous example the circle and square were created by calling a procedure called
"Poly" whose first argument is the size of each side and the second the angle turned.
So the square might have been "[fl]POLY 200 90[fr]" and the circle "[fl]POLY 20 10[fr]".
Now one simple way to interpolate the two would be to gradually change 200 to 20 and
90 to 10.  This would produce the following series of drawings however:
.begin group
.skip 12
.end
	What follows is a description of various attempts I made over a two week period
to refine the definition of this problem and
to produce a very general solution to it.  Interesting discoveries made along the way
will also be described.
.sec Versions Zero and One
	Before I actually tried implementing anything I thought of a simple, though
limited solution.  It was based on the observation that any drawing can rather simply
be transformed to a straight line.  All that is necessary is to reexecute the original
procedure with repeatedly smaller inputs to "Right" and "Left".  To transform a line
into an arbitrary turtle program, one needs only use the procedure for transforming
drawings into lines, and reverse the result.  This scheme is not very good, since the
series of drawings will seldom be a natural transformation, nor will they be
very pretty.  Glenn Iba suggested a better version of this same approach, namely that
you always transform into a circle and then from a circle to the second drawing.
	My first thought on how to do this without using one
of these middle man
was that the total distance and turning were important.  Somehow knowing the
totals for both drawings one could interpolate between them.  As will be shown later,
these totals are useful, however, they abstract too much from the drawings.  The
triangle "[fl]POLY 200 120[fr]" has the same totals as the hexagon "[fl]POLY 100 60[fr]"
yet they have very different appearances.
	At that point I realized you need to manipulate the display lists
that correspond to drawings, in addition to statistics of their execution.
Comparing any two display lists is difficult, since there is so much variety possible.
The way around this that I pursued was to [fi]canonicalize[fr] the display lists.
As a first approximation, I implemented a version that produced display lists that
consisted of only "Forward" some amount, followed by "Right" some amount, then "Forward"
again, and so on.  Two forwards in a row were reduced to a forward of their sum,
left some amount was recorded as
right the negative of that amount and so on. The handling of
"Penup" and "Pendown" was put off until later.  There was a bug in the original version
in that "[fl]FORWARD 100[fr]" followed by "[fl]BACK 50[fr]" was represented in the
display list as "[fl]FORWARD 50[fr]". The patch was to represent them as the list
[fl]((FORWARD 100) (RIGHT 180) (FORWARD 50) (RIGHT 180))[fr]. For example, the
display list of "[fl]POLY 200 90[fr]" was:
.begin nofill skip
(RIGHT 90)
(FORWARD 200)
(RIGHT 90)
(FORWARD 200)
(RIGHT 90)
(FORWARD 200)
(RIGHT 90)
(FORWARD 200)
.end
	Now if we want to interpolate between that display list the one resulting
from the call "[fl]POLY 100 120[fr]" then we face the problem of different
lengths of the display list.  If only they were the same length then the interpolation
would be very easy, just interpolate between the first line of the first display list
and the first line of the second display list, and then for the second line of each
and so on.  The easiest way to make two display lists equal is to fatten up the
shorter one by adding dummy statements like "[fl]FORWARD 0[fr]" and "[fl]RIGHT 0[fr]".
This would work, however, the interpolation would not be symmetrical since the dummy
vectors are interspersed with full vectors.
	The way that I first implemented fattening up display lists was based on the
observation that
.begin nofill choose lisp
FORWARD 100 [fn]is equivalent to[fl] FORWARD 50, RIGHT 0, FORWARD 50 [fn]and that[fl]
RIGHT 60 [fn]is equivalent to[fl] RIGHT 30, FORWARD 0, RIGHT 30.
.end
So any line of a display list could by this method become three lines.  The algorithm
that I first tried
substituted the equivalent three lines into the shorter display list a number of times
equal to
the difference in length between the two display lists divided by two.  The decision as
to which lines in the shorter display list would be expanded was to distribute them as
equally as possible to try to preserve any symmetry.  So applying this algorithm to
the display list of a square and triangle the triangle display list was transformed as
follows:
.begin nofill choose lisp skip
[fn]Original				Fattened Up Display List[fl]

(RIGHT 120)			(RIGHT 120)
(FORWARD 100)			(FORWARD 100)
				(RIGHT 60)
(RIGHT 120)			(FORWARD 0)
				(RIGHT 60)
(FORWARD 100)			(FORWARD 100)
(RIGHT 120)			(RIGHT 120)
(FORWARD 100)			(FORWARD 100)
.end
	When the shorter list was less than a third of the length of the other then this
simple scheme breaks down.  I needed the ability of expand a line by any factor, not
just three with this scheme.  The algorithm was easily extended to multiple any line
by an odd factor by splitting the amount up by half of the
factor plus one and inserting dummy
operations in between.  For example, the result of expanding
"[fl]RIGHT 90[fr]" by a factor of five is
[fl]((RIGHT 30) (FORWARD 0) (RIGHT 30) (FORWARD 0) (RIGHT 30))[fr].  The initial version
did not succeed when an even factor was called for.
	Once the display lists are the same length and corresponding lines have
corresponding operations then interpolation was simple.  At first I created a list
of intermediate display lists by making a linear interpolation between the corresponding
amounts.  Later I realized that creating the display lists was both inflexible and
inefficient so I changed it to produce an "interpolation function" which expected as
an input a function to apply to the difference.  For example,
the interpolation function between
a square and triangle was:
.begin nofill single space skip indent 0,0 preface 0
.choose lisp
(LAMBDA (INTERPOLATION-FUNCTION)
	(RIGHT (+ 90 (FUNCALL INTERPOLATION-FUNCTION -30)))
	(FORWARD 200 (FUNCALL INTERPOLATION-FUNCTION -100))
	(RIGHT (+ 90 (FUNCALL INTERPOLATION-FUNCTION 30)))
	(FORWARD 200 (FUNCALL INTERPOLATION-FUNCTION -200))
	(RIGHT (+ 90 (FUNCALL INTERPOLATION-FUNCTION 30)))
	(FORWARD 200 (FUNCALL INTERPOLATION-FUNCTION -100))
	(RIGHT (+ 90 (FUNCALL INTERPOLATION-FUNCTION -30)))
	(FORWARD 200 (FUNCALL INTERPOLATION-FUNCTION -100)))
.end
	It was simple to write a few functions that repeated called this with different
interpolation functions, producing a straight line interpolation between the square and
triangle.
	Next, I worried about how to handle programs with "Penup" and "Pendown" commands
in them.  My first thought was to just insert them into the display lists and then
into the corresponding position in the interpolation function.  This will tend to
produce strange interpolations where two dashed lines on different dash lengths will
interpolate suddenly to an even more dashed line.  A dashed line should
interpolate with a solid line of the same length by gradually removing the "blank"
spaces in the dashed line.  With this in mind I changed the display list generator
to treat pen commands specially.  Penup was defined to remember the turtle state and
and then prevent the other commands from adding
 to the display list until a pendown comes along.  The
"Pendown" command will then generate a new command that I called "Invisibly" and add
it to the display list.  "Invisibly" expects three commands as inputs, the first and
third are
"Right" commands and the middle one is a "Forward" command.
These three commands when done
with the penup behave identically to whatever happened while the drawing program was
running with the pen up.  As an example of hidden dependencies, this change caused a
bug that did not manifest itself for days.  The problem was that the assumption that
between any two "Rights" is a "Forward" and between any two "Forwards" is a "Right"
was no longer valid.  It makes sense to go forward then go somewhere with the pen up and
then go forward again.  This was simple to patch by inserting a "Right 0" before the
last "Forward", however, it was a little harder to track down.
	The next problem was how to modify the interpolator of equivalent programs
if they contain "Invisibly" commands.  The initial solution to this was both cludgy
and buggy.  I was misled by the example of the dashed lines mentioned above.  The
problem I thought was how to make "Invisiblies" disappear since if there were more
"Invisiblies" in the destination drawing then I could do the interpolation between
the destination and source and then "reverse" the result.  Making "Invisibly" statements
gradually disappear was easy, I just interpolated each command in the "Invisibly" with
a dummy "Invisibly" that looked like
"[fl](INVISIBLY (RIGHT 0) (FORWARD 0) (RIGHT 0))[fr]".  Since "Invisibly" commands had
been standardized as right, forward then right this part was easy.
.skip
	Thus ended the first night of hacking.
.sec Deficiencies Discovered and Improvements Implemented
	Playing with the program and showing it to various people resulted in the
discovery of many deficiencies.  One problem was that in interpolating a circle and
a star [fl](POLY 200 144)[fr] resulted in the following:
.begin group skip 12
.end once indent 0
The program was "correct" in some sense, however, as Hal Abelson pointed out, one would
like the interpolation between two closed figures to be closed.  The program produced
a much better interpolation when asked to interpolate as follows:
.begin nofill choose lisp
.skip
(MAKE.INTERPOLATION 'STAR.TO.CIRCLE
		    '(POLY 200 144)
		    '(PROGN (POLY 20 10) (POLY 20 10)))
.end
The resulting interpolation looked like:
.begin group skip 12
.end once indent 0
The correspondences were much more reasonable when the two display lists had the
same total turnage.  With this in mind I changed the program to check the total
turnage and if the display lists were state transparent then each could be repeated
until equal to the least common multiple of total turnage of each divided by 360.
For example, a display list that had a total turnage of 720 when interpolated with
one with a 1080 turnage would be tripled while the other doubled.  If they were not
state transparent then the following hack was incorporated.  The display
list is doubled by turning right 180 and then running the display list backwards.  This
was motivated by the desire to produce a nice interpolation between a line and a
circle such as:
.begin group skip 12
.end once indent 0
while the program was producing this:
.begin group skip 12
.end once indent 0
This hack caused more problems than it was worth and was later eliminated.  So presently
only state transparent programs keeps things closed.  To interpolate to or from a line,
it needs to be entered as something like "[fl](POLY 200 180)[fr]"
	One exciting possibility for my program that I overlooked at first was that the
interpolation functions it writes are also [fi]extrapolation[fr] functions.
For example, the "Circle.to.star" function could be run from -3 to 4 where 0 is the
circle and 1 is the star (double circle actually) and the result is:
.begin group skip 24
.end once indent 0
	One embarrassing aspect of the program's performance was when interpolating
between a triangle and a square.  The interpolation looked bad, even worse than
the present performance of the system which looks like:
.begin group skip 12
.end once indent 0
The interpolation was only "almost" closed and not very symmetrical.  This problem
while still not solved, led to some improvements in the system.
	One idea was that the lack of symmetry was due to the way in which display
lists were expanded.  After talking with Hal Abelson about this, we decided that
maybe if we expanded both display list to the least common multiple of the number of
instructions in each that maybe smoother, more symmetrical interpolations would result.
So by this scheme the triangle display list would get fattened by a factor of four
and the square by three.  At this point I realized that the old way of multiplying by
a factor lost for even factors.  The solution that I implemented was to break up
pairs of instructions at the same time rather than one at a time.  This way if an
even factor was called for the expansion one less could be generated and a dummy forward
and right could be inserted in the center of the expanded list.
This simplified the code
and produced better interpolations but did not solve the square to triangle problem.
	Also though this implementation was more elegant and accurate sometimes it
results in rather poor use of resources.  One problem that I gave the system was:
.begin choose lisp nofill
MAKE.INTERPOLATION 'CIRCLE.TO.STAR156 '(POLY 10 10) '(POLY 200 156)
.end
This problem had been presented to an earlier version of the program (before total
turnage was equalized) by Seymour Papert
and it lost very badly.  The new version it did the "right" thing
which looked like:
.begin group skip 12
.end once indent 0
The problem was that it took it forever to generate the interpolation function
and to draw the drawings.  The problem was that it took the circle and "ran" it
13 times producing a display list 13 times 72 or 936 instructions long.  It then
found the least common multiple of that and the thirty pointed star whose length is
60 coming up with 4680.  The interpolation
function was 40 blocks long and the compiler used up over
a 100K of list space trying to compile it before it gave up.  So I cheated and
changed the circle to [fl]"(POLY 10 12)"[fr] whose length is also 60 instructions,
so the resulting function was a fifth the size of the previous one and looked as
good.
	The triangle to square problem bothered me, and I noticed that the
interpolation between a triangle and a twelve sided polygon and the interpolation
between that polygon and a square are very symmetrical and nice. They look like this:
.begin group skip 12
.end once indent 0
So I thought how could one interpolation function interpolate between these three
display lists.  I worked out the algorithm for making them equivalent and then
fitting a quadratic to the three points corresponding to the amounts in corresponding
statements.  I started to implement this but lost interest because it was too tedious
changing all the code to handle two [fi]or three[fr] drawings.  The reader is invited
to implement the generalization of all this, namely, fitting a curve to an
arbitrary number of display lists.
	Next I returned to the problem of handling the "Invisibly" commands.  I realized
that a better way to handle them is to insert at the corresponding place in the other
function a dummy "Invisibly" command.  Interpolating between "Invisibly" commands is
simply the interpolation between their components.  This behaves reasonably for the few
examples I tested it out on and again made the code simpler.
	The last problem I worked on was to make interpolations on top of each other
look better.  For example, the interpolation between a circle and square looked like
this:
.begin group skip 12
.end once indent 0
While I wanted it to look like this:
.begin group skip 12
.end once indent 0
The problem was how to center the two display lists over the same point.  This was
solved by keeping a "position list" in addition to the display list.  A position list
is a list of pairs, the first element is the state of the turtle, the second element is
the amount of the forward that took the turtle to that point or the amount of the forward
for leaving that state.  The idea is that a crude approximation of the center of a
display list is the average x coordinate and y coordinate of all the vertices.  This
method favors, however, many short vectors over a few long ones.  For this reason the
position list also included the amount of the forwards and uses them as weights when
calculating the center.
	Once I had the center I constructed a series of commands with the pen up to
take the turtle from the center to the starting point and then apply those commands
to go from the starting state to a new starting state that results in the drawing
being centered around the start state.  The display list generated thus had
the appropriate "Invisibly" command at the beginning and worked fine.
.sec Things Left to Do
	There are many directions one could take this.  One could vary the system
and see if there are visible effects.  I tried a couple of variations, one where
only "Forward" statements were broken up in the process of fattening up, another
instead of breaking up
up statements equally it used ratios of the number of
vectors in each display list to break up lines more fairly, and another used the product
of their lengths
instead of using the least common multiple to fatten up to,
The first two had little affect and were flushed.  The last one
led to trying twice the product of their lengths.  I was motivated by the
triangle to square problem and the interpolations improved as the length of the
interpolation functions did.  Approximations built into the algorithm became
less apparent as can be seen in the following drawings:
.begin group skip 24
.end once indent 0
	Another possible improvement would be to break up short vectors less than
long vectors.  In all the Poly examples this would have no affect since the vectors
have the same length.  It is worth exploring the effect of this would be on more
random drawings.
	One direction I would like to take this, if I have the time, is to
interpolate other aspects of the drawings.  If they are in color then the colors
could be interpolated in the obvious way, i.e. computing the differences in the
red, green and blue parts of each drawing.  A straight line interpolation
is known not the be perceive as equal increments, but very pretty pictures may
result anyway.  Another aspect of a tv turtle drawing is its shading.  A special
purpose hack can be done for textures, by slowing changing the threshold that is
used to decide to put down a point or not.  A more common shading method is to use
windows.  Windows can also be interpolated.  If the dimensions of each are the same
(if they are not they probably could be made the same) then a difference table is
easily generated by xoring the two windows.  This difference table (or window) could
then be successively filtered so that more and more points get through and at each
stage xored with the first window.  This may look like a "dissolve" in movies, but
since it is so easy I may try it.  How to treat programs that make use of xor or
erasers is a hard problem that I have thought little about.
	Despite the ability of the system to handle to some extend sections where the
pen is up the system could not interpolate between two faces unless it was either
guided by extra information or if it was just lucky and only interpolated between
noses and never a nose of one face and a mouth of the other.  My feeling is that this
cannot be done in general using a mathematical approach such those that I have been
discussing but must be based on more semantic information of what the drawings
represent.
.sec Appendix How to Play with the System
	The following is subject to change so if anything goes wrong just send me
a message.
	To load the system on AI, just type:
.begin nofill indent 0,0
:l ken;inter
to start up the system is Lisp.  To start it up in Lisp Logo try:
:llogo
DO YOU WANT TO USE THE TV TURTLE?
yes
?readlisp inter llogo dsk ken
.end
	To make an interpolation you should use the function "Make.interpolation"
which is abbreviated as "MI".   To create centered interpolations use
"Make.centered.interpolation" abbreviated as "MCI".  "MI" and "MCI" have identical
inputs, the
first one is the name you want to give
the interpolation function, the second is the first call to a tvrtle program and
the second call comes last.  For example, to interpolate between a circle and star
and call it 'circle.to.star you type:
.begin nofill indent 0,0 skip
MI 'CIRCLE.TO.STAR '(POLY 10 10) '(POLY 200 144)
.end once indent 0
If you are in LLogo then you should preceded the name with ":", for example,
':CIRCLE.TO.STAR.
