.sec Procedural Computer Animation
	(this section describes animation done without the aid of graphical inputs,
solely be program generated images)
.subsec  Historical view
	Procedural computer animation began in the early 1960s, with Bell Labs being
among the pioneers.  Computer languages and software intended for other purposes were
extended to produce vector drawings on a cathode ray tube.  Typically objects were
defined by their coordinates in space, occasionally in a three dimensional space that
could be projected into a two dimension plane internally by the program.  Procedures
were defined to manipulate these objects by rotating, scaling, translating them
typically using linear algebra.  The most common use of this type of computer animation
was in the production of instructional films.  Early films of this type included one
that showed a communication satellite in orbit and another that displayed crystal growth.
	From the beginning computers were being used to produce films that
took advantage
of some of the unique advantages they have in animation.  These films displayed
something
that could not be filmed (being too small, far away, or whatever) and required too great
an amount of computation to be practical for conventional hand drawn animation.  These
are films that could not have been done using either analog or demonstrative mechanisms.
.subsec  Current State as exemplified by LOGO
	Procedural computer animation has evolved down a few different paths since the
early 1960s.  One pathtaken was to extend
the original conception of computer animation as the
manipulation of objects described by their coordinates or generating functions.
These systems can display projections of three dimensional objects hiding those
lines that would be obscured were the objects real solids.  Then the problems of
realistic
shading and lighting were resolved, so that today there are systems that can generate
pictures that look very much like photographs of real objects.  Static reality was
one of the major goals of these researchers and little was done to provide good control
of the motion and transformations of these objects.  Typically the motion would be
defined mathematically.
The University of Utah
pioneered much of this research.
	The other major direction that computer graphics and animation research has gone
is towards designing system that are easier to use.  Special languages were designed
that were simple, mnemonic, and yet powerful enough to do all that the previous systems
did.  Perhaps most importantly within these languages were imbedded concepts and ways
of thinking of graphics and animation that were much closer to how one originally
concieves of one's animation.  For example, instead of having to describe a triangle
in terms of its end points, these newer languages allowed one to give rather intuitive
instructions to a mechanical "pen".  The two best examples of such languages is
SmallTalk developed at Xerox Parc and Logo developed at MIT.  One reason for this
stress on simplicity and intuitiveness is because both language were originally intended
to be used by young school children.  Both projects are still active in teaching
children to use the computer to create music, drawings, animation, etc. however, it
soon became apparent that these languages were often the most convient way for computer
scientists to do computer graphics and animation.
Since I am more familiar
with Logo I will describe only it.
.subsubsec Turtles or how you draw things
	In Logo one draws by giving instructions to a computational entity called a
turtle.  One can tell the turtle to go forward any specified amount, to turn left or
right any amount.  These turtles will if they have their "pen down", leave a trail
as they move around the screen.  It turns out that turtles are a very good, intuitive
way to think about producing images on the screen.  Very simple, very clean, turtle
programs, often produce very impressive complex images (some of which illustrate this
article?)
.subsubsec Turtles or a good way to animate things
	Using Logo to animate one normally takes what are called "snapshots" of sections
of the display and these snapshots are subsequently displayed in rapid succession often
in different parts of the screen producing apparent motion.  However, in some more
recent extentions of Logo (and even earlier with Smalltalk)
one is able to animate in a more
intuitive way.  Multiple turtles can be created with almost any appearance and can move
around the screen in any manner.  One ultimate goal along this line is to create
an animation system where the user is like a movie director who can easily
specify what he or
she wants each "actor" to look like and how they behave.
.sec Demonstrative Computer Animation
As mentioned earlier the other major approach to computer animation is one which
one controls basically through graphical means.  One points with a light pen, or draws
on a digitized tablet which sends the current coordinates of a stylus to the computer
a large number of times a second.  Ivan Sutherland pioneered this method of communication
with a computer at MIT's Lincoln Lab in the early 1960s.  He was followed in the late
1960s by Ron Baecker, also at the Lincoln Lab, who concentrated on the problem of
[fi]animating[fr] images drawn into the computer.  A major innovation of his was a
technique for
graphical input of the path and speed of objects.
	These initial efforts lead to "key-frame" systems which added the capibility
of the computer producing "in-between" drawings between two drawings that were
graphically inputted into the machine.  This "in-betweening" is one of the most
tedious and time consuming aspects of conventional animation and it is hoped that
such systems will soon be of help to conventional (ie hand-drawing) animators.  Using
these programs in odds ways can produce quite unusual transitions such as those in
Peter Foldes' film "Hunger".)
	The lastest direction these system are going is towards what are called
"paint brush" systems.  Up until recently when the computer drew a line or displayed
a line a user drew on a tablet the line was typically a thin single-toned line.  With
the creation of color raster scan systems
foot<Barry this is what I showed you on the Advent>
sometimes called "frame
buffers", it is now possible to replace the "pen" with a "paint brush".  The user can
define the characteristics of the brush he or she wants--- its color, width, pattern
and "paints" on a color tv monitor.
New York Institute of Technology, MIT's Architecture Machine Group, and Xerox Parc
are among a few who are developing such systems.
.sec  The Future
	(this section is rather opinated and while optomistic that optomism is shared
by most computer scientists today)
.subsec  Hardware
	Computers have been getting cheaper, faster and more powerful every year.  If
current trends continue computers will within a ten years be cheap enough for nearly
anyone who wants to, to own one.  In addition, with faster computers and displays
the need to film the display in order to see one's animation will hopefully become
much rarer.  Also with faster and cheaper memory it becomes feasible to raise the
quality of the image and number of possible colors.
	Currently both computer and hand-made animation is too expensive and time
consuming to allow one to really explore.  It is rare that an animated film goes
thru many revisions as a result of producing "rough drafts" and then making improvements.
There is good reason to believe that when one is able to produce quality [fi]
interactive[fr] computer animation that the ease of modification and testing
will greatly increase the quality of these animations.
.subsec Digitized Live Action
	The computer is beginning to be used to process real images of the world.
These images get into the computer via a vidicon (tv) camera and an analog to digital
converter.  The programmer is then free to manipulate these images in a variety of ways.
This sort of animation is most analogous to roto-scoping where an animator bases each
drawing
frame by frame on live action footage.  Ken Knowlton and Lilian Schwartz both at Bell
Labs have produced computer films based on this technique.
.subsec Better Separation of each element of the Animation
	One unique property of the newer
computer animation is that each graphical element can
be manipulated independently.  A user of a paint brush or color Logo system can
draw an image and then without redrawing replace any of the colors with any others
instantaneously.  Similarly if one is happy with the color but not the shape one
can re-create the image with the same colors but with a new shape.  Similarly the
motion, change, texture, and size of any element of the drawing can be manipulated
independently of the others.  I see such systems as being very common and designed so
that it is even easier to exploite this great advantage of computer in the
development and testing of animations
.subsec Smarter systems
	Another direction one could hope that animation will go is towards systems
that know a fair amount about animation and the elements being animated.  Recent
advances in Artificial Intelligence at MIT and elsewhere provide computational
mechanisms that could be used to create a system that is knowledgable enough about
animation to allow the user to interact with the computer at a more conceptual
level.  For example, the user might simply indicate that a particular character
should walk in a bouncy energetic manner over to another character.  Or if a more
abstract film is desired one might indicate that one wants a film centered around
a bright pulsating circle and the system will make guess as to what size it should
be, how fast it aught to pulsate (and what that means) what the background color
should be, etc.
.subsec Animation by Computer
	In the longer term there is reason to believe that some day the term
"computer animation" will become an ambiguous term.  It will mean either "animation
done on a computer" or "animation done by a computer".  The today either
the latter
does not exist or consists of films where nearly all the computer decisions were
based on a simulated flip of a coin.  The day when such decisions might be based on
a real understanding of perception and aesthetics may not be that far off.

(defun put-hand (:x :hand)
         (go-square (plus :x 3.) 17.5)
	 (eraserdown)
	 (fillwindow 71. 12.)
	 (eraserup)
	 (put-hand1 :x :hand))

(defun put-hand1 (:x :hand)
	 (cond ((null :hand))
	       (t (put-letter :x 17.5 (car :hand))
		  (put-hand1  (plus :x 1.) (cdr :hand)))))

(defun make-colors nil
       (makecolor 'background 0.5 0.5 0.5)
       (makecolor 'double-word 1.0 0.5 0.5)
       (makecolor 'double-letter 0.5 0.5 1.0)
       (makecolor 'triple-word 1.0 0.0 0.0)
       (makecolor 'triple-letter 0.0 0.0 1.0))

(defun pick-color (:pattern)
       (cond ((equal :pattern 'double-word)
	      (pencolor 'dw))))

(defun  score-cross-hor (:x :y :subproduct)
	(cond ((null (letters :x :y)) :subproduct)
	      (t (score-cross-hor :x
				  (plus :y 1.)
				  (plus :subproduct (check-cross-hor :x :y))))))

(defun score-cross-vert  (:x :y :subproduct)
       (cond ((null (letters :x :y)) :subproduct)
	     (t (score-cross-vert (1+ :x)
				  :y
				  (+ :subproduct (check-cross-vert :x :y))))))

(defun score-cross-word (:x :y :direction)
       (cond ((equal :direction 'sleeping) (score-cross-vert :x :y 0.))
	     (t (score-cross-hor :x :y 0.))))

(defun check-cross-hor (:x :y)
     (cond ((eq (bonus :x :y) 'filled) 0.)
	   ((letters (compute-cross-x :x '-1.) :y)
	    (score-word (find-beg-x :x :y) :y 'sleeping))
	   ((letters (compute-cross-x :x 1.) :y)
	    (score-word :x :y 'sleeping))
	   (t  0.)))

(defun check-cross-vert (:x :y)
       (cond ((eq (bonus :x :y) 'filled) 0.)
	     ((letters :x (compute-cross-y :y -1.))
	      (score-word :x (find-beg-y :x :y) 'standing))
	     ((letters :x (compute-cross-y :y 1.))
	      (score-word :x :y 'standing))
	     (t  0.)))


(defun space nil
	 ( '| | ))

(defun replace (:old :new :thing)
      (subst :old :new :thing))

(defun take-out (:x :list)
   	(cond ((null :list) :list)
	      ((equal :x (car :list))(cdr :list))
	      (t (cons (car :list) (take-out :x (cdr :list))))))

(defun count-needle (:needle :haystack)
       (cond ((null :haystack) 0.)
	     ((equal (car :haystack) :needle)
	      (1+ (count-needle :needle (cdr :haystack))))
	zz(     (t (count-needle :needle (cdr :haystack)))))

(defun thanks nil
       (print '(your welcome)))


(defun put-score (:x :y :score)
       (erase-score (plus :x 0.5) (player-y :player))
       (go-square :x (player-y :player))
       (draw :score)))

(defun put-names (:players)
       (cond ((null :players) (stop)))
       (go-square 16.5 (player-y (car :players)))
       (mark (car :players))
       (home)

(defun game nil
       (start-game)
       (start-play)
       (play)))

(defun play nil
       (prog (:the-score)
	     (setq count-play 1.)
	     (cond ((greaterp (remainder :count-play (count :players))  0.)
		    (setq player (get 'player-no (\ :count-play (count :players)))))
		   (t (setq player (get 'player-no (count :players)))))
   25.	(type (sentence :player (space)))
   30.	(make 'play (request))
   33.	(cond ((null :play) (go 30.)))
   34.	(cond
	 ((equal (car :play) '*)
	  (cond
	   ((equal (nth 2. :play) 'trade.tiles)
	    (trade.tiles
	      (apply 'word
				(cdr (cdr :play))))
	    (go 84.)))))
   35.	(cond ((equal (car :play) '*)
	       (run (cdr :play))
	       (go 30.)))
   37.	(cond ((equal (check.notation) 'oops)
	       (go 30.)))
   40.	(cond ((numberp (car (cdr :play)))
	       (make 'direction 'sleeping))
	      (t (make 'direction 'standing)))
   42.	(cond ((numberp (car (cdr :play)))
	       (make 'y.position  (nth 2. :play))
	       (make 'x.position
		      (numequiv (nth 3. :play)))
	       (go 50.)))
   44.	(make 'x.position
	       (numequiv (nth 2. :play)))
   46.	(make 'y.position  (nth 3. :play))
   50.	(make 'the.score
	      (play.word :x.position
			   :y.position
			   :direction
			   (car :play)))
   52.	(cond ((equal :the.score 'blech)
	       (logo-print (square-brackets (try another play)))
	       (go 30.)))
   55.	(put.score 19.6 (player.y :player) :the.score)
   62.	(putprop :player
	      (plus :the.score (get :player '.score))
	      '.score)
   70.	(put.score 21.5
		     (player.y :player)
		     (get :player '.score))
   80.	(putprop :player
	      (pick.hand (get :player '.hand))
	      '.hand)
   84.	(cond
	 ((greaterp (remainder :count.play
					   (count :players))
			  0.)
	  (make
	   'x
	   (difference

	     (times
	      (user-paren
	       (difference
		(user-paren (remainder :count.play
					     (count :players)))
		1.))
	      8.)
	    3.)))
	 (t
	  (cond
	   ((equal (remainder :count.play
					  (count :players))
			 0.)
	    (make
	     'x
	     (difference

	       (times
		(user-paren
		 (difference (user-paren (count :players)) 1.))
		8.)
	      3.))))))
   85.	(put.hand :x (get :player '.hand))
   90.	(make 'count.play (plus :count.play 1.))
   95.	(saveboard)
   100.	(go 20.)
	))

	  (output
	   (score.letters1

	     :subtotal
	     (times 2. (value.letter (letters :x :y))))))))
   30.	(cond
	 ((equal (bonus :x :y) 'triple.letter)

	     :subtotal
	     (times 3. (value.letter (letters :x :y))))))))
   40.	(output
	 (score.letters1
	  (compute.x :x :direction)
	  (compute.y :y :direction)
	  (plus :subtotal (value.letter (letters :x :y)))))
	))

(defun
 check.word
  (:x :y :direction :word :temp.hand :count.word)

   10.	(cond ((null :word)
	       (cond ((equal :count.word 7.)
		      (output 'bonus))
		     (t (output :temp.hand)))))
   15.	(make 'tile (car :word))
   20.	(cond ((either (both (greaterp (count :word)
					     (difference 16.
							       :x))
			     (equal :direction
					  'sleeping))
		       (both (greaterp (count :word)
					     (difference 16.
							       :y))
			     (equal :direction
					  'standing)))
	       (logo-print (square-brackets (word is
						  too
						  long
						  it
						  wont
						  fit
						  on
						  the
						  board,
						  try
						  again)))
	       (output 'try.again)))
   30.	(cond
	 ((not (equal (letters :x :y) (car :word)))
	  (cond
	   ((not (null (letters :x :y)))
	    (logo-print
	      (sentence (square-brackets (you cant enter))
				   (car :word)
				   (square-brackets (in the
							space
							where))
				   (letters :x :y)
				   (square-brackets (is))))
	    (output 'try.again)))))
   33.	(cond ((equal :direction 'standing)
	       (check.stand :x :y)))
   35.	(cond ((equal :direction 'sleeping)
	       (check.sleep :x :y)))
   36.	(cond ((letters :x :y)
	       (make 'tile 'nothing)
	       (go 40.))
	      (t (make 'count.word
		       (plus :count.word 1.))))
   37.	(cond ( (member (car :word) :temp.hand)
	       (go 40.)))
   38.	(cond ((member (space) :temp.hand)
	       (make 'blank.word
		     (replace (car :word) (space) (car :play)))
	       (make 'blank (car :word))
	       (make 'tile (space))
	       (go 40.)))
   39.	(logo-print
	  (sentence (car :word)
			       (square-brackets (is not
						    a
						    letter
						    in
						    your
						    hand))))
	(output 'try.again)
   40.	(cond ((equal :direction 'standing)
	       (output (check.word :x
				     (plus :y 1.)
				     :direction
				     (cdr :word)
				     (take.out :tile :temp.hand)
				     :count.word)))
	      (t (output (check.word (plus :x 1.)
				       :y
				       :direction
				       (cdr :word)
				       (take.out :tile :temp.hand)
				       :count.word))))
	))

(defun
 play.word
  (:x :y :direction :word)
  (prog (:the.score)
   5.	(local 'the.score)
   7.	(make 'connected (false))
   8.	(make 'blank.word :word)
   9.	(make 'temp.hand
	      (check.word :x
			    :y
			    :direction
			    :word
			    (get :player '.hand)
			    0.))
   10.	(cond ((equal :temp.hand 'try.again)
	       (output 'blech))
	      (t (go 11.)))
   11.	(cond ((not :connected)
	       (cond ((greaterp :count.play 1.)
		      (logo-print (square-brackets (your word
							 must
							 be
							 attached
							 to
							 another
							 word
							 on
							 the
							 board)))
		      (output 'blech)))))
   13.	(enter.word :x :y :direction :blank.word)
   15.	(make 'the.score
	      (plus (score.word :x :y :direction)
		    (score.cross.word :x :y :direction)))
   18.	(cond ((equal :temp.hand 'bonus)
	       (make 'the.score (plus :the.score 50.))))
   22.	(put.word :x :y :direction :blank.word)
   30.	(zap.bonus :x :y)
   35.	(cond ((equal :temp.hand 'bonus)
	       (putprop :player nil '.hand))
	      (t (putprop :player :temp.hand '.hand)))
   40.	(output :the.score)
	))

TO FOO
10 PRINT [FOO 0 BAR]
END

TO PUT.HAND :X :HAND
10 GO.SQUARE :X + 3 17.5
20 ERASERDOWN FILLWINDOW 71 12
22 ERASERUP
30 PUT.HAND1 :X :HAND
END

TO PUT.HAND1 :X :HAND
10 IF EMPTYP :HAND THEN STOP
20 PUT.LETTER :X 17.5 FIRST :HAND
30 PUT.HAND1 (:X + 1) (BUTFIRST :HAND)
END

TO MAKE.COLORS
5 MAKECOLOR 'BACKGROUND 0.5 0.5 0.5
10 MAKECOLOR 'DOUBLE.WORD 1.0 0.5 0.5
20 MAKECOLOR 'DOUBLE.LETTER 0.5 0.5 1.0
30 MAKECOLOR 'TRIPLE.WORD 1.0 0.0 0.0
40 MAKECOLOR 'TRIPLE.LETTER 0.0 0.0 1.0
END

TO PICK.COLOR :PATTERN
10 IF :PATTERN = 'DOUBLE.WORD THEN PENCOLOR 'DW
END

TO SCORE.CROSS.HOR :X :Y :SUBPRODUCT
10 IF EMPTYP LETTERS :X :Y THEN OUTPUT :SUBPRODUCT
20 OUTPUT SCORE.CROSS.HOR :X :Y + 1 :SUBPRODUCT + CHECK.CROSS.HOR :X :Y
END

TO SCORE.CROSS.VERT :X :Y :SUBPRODUCT
10 IF EMPTYP LETTERS :X :Y THEN OUTPUT :SUBPRODUCT
20 OUTPUT SCORE.CROSS.VERT :X + 1 :Y :SUBPRODUCT + CHECK.CROSS.VERT :X :Y
END

TO SCORE.CROSS.WORD :X :Y :DIRECTION
10 IF :DIRECTION = 'SLEEPING THEN OUTPUT SCORE.CROSS.VERT :X :Y 0 ELSE OUTPUT SCORE.CROSS.HOR :X :Y 0
END

TO CHECK.CROSS.HOR :X :Y
10 IF BONUS :X :Y = 'FILLED THEN OUTPUT 0
20 IF LETTERS COMPUTE.CROSS.X :X '-1 :Y THEN OUTPUT SCORE.WORD FIND.BEG.X :X :Y :Y 'SLEEPING
30 IF LETTERS COMPUTE.CROSS.X :X 1 :Y THEN OUTPUT SCORE.WORD :X :Y 'SLEEPING ELSE OUTPUT 0
END

TO CHECK.CROSS.VERT :X :Y
10 IF BONUS :X :Y = 'FILLED THEN OUTPUT 0
20 IF LETTERS :X COMPUTE.CROSS.Y :Y (-1) THEN OUTPUT SCORE.WORD :X FIND.BEG.Y :X :Y 'STANDING
30 IF LETTERS :X COMPUTE.CROSS.Y :Y 1 THEN OUTPUT SCORE.WORD :X :Y 'STANDING ELSE OUTPUT 0
END

TO SPACE
10 OUTPUT '$ $
END

TO REPLACE :OLD :NEW :THING
10 IF EMPTYP :THING THEN OUTPUT :THING
20 IF :OLD = FIRST :THING THEN OUTPUT WORD :NEW BUTFIRST :THING ELSE OUTPUT WORD FIRST :THING REPLACE :OLD :NEW BUTFIRST :THING
END

TO TAKE.OUT :X :LIST
10 IF EMPTYP :LIST THEN OUTPUT :LIST
20 IF :X = FIRST :LIST THEN OUTPUT BUTFIRST :LIST
30 OUTPUT FPUT FIRST :LIST TAKE.OUT :X (((BUTFIRST :LIST)))
END

TO COUNT.NEEDLE :NEEDLE :HAYSTACK
10 IF EMPTYP :HAYSTACK THEN OUTPUT 0
20 IF FIRST :HAYSTACK = :NEEDLE THEN OUTPUT 1 + COUNT.NEEDLE :NEEDLE BUTFIRST :HAYSTACK ELSE OUTPUT COUNT.NEEDLE :NEEDLE BUTFIRST :HAYSTACK
END

TO THANKS
10 PRINT [YOUR WELCOME]
END

TO PUT.SCORE :X :Y :SCORE
10 ERASE.SCORE :X + 0.5 PLAYER.Y :PLAYER
20 GO.SQUARE :X PLAYER.Y :PLAYER
30 DRAW :SCORE

TO PLAY
5 LOCAL 'THE.SCORE
10 MAKE 'COUNT.PLAY 1
20 IF :COUNT.PLAY \ COUNT :PLAYERS > 0 THEN MAKE 'PLAYER GETP 'PLAYER.NO :COUNT.PLAY \ COUNT :PLAYERS ELSE MAKE 'PLAYER GETP 'PLAYER.NO COUNT :PLAYERS
25 TYPE SENTENCE :PLAYER SPACE
30 MAKE 'PLAY REQUEST
33 IF EMPTYP :PLAY THEN GO 30
34 IF FIRST :PLAY = '$*$ THEN IF NTH 2 :PLAY = 'TRADE.TILES THEN TRADE.TILES (APPLY 'WORD BUTFIRST BUTFIRST :PLAY) GO 84
35 IF FIRST :PLAY = '$*$ THEN RUN BUTFIRST :PLAY GO 30
37 IF CHECK.NOTATION = 'OOPS THEN GO 30
40 IF NUMBERP FIRST BUTFIRST :PLAY THEN MAKE 'DIRECTION 'SLEEPING ELSE MAKE 'DIRECTION 'STANDING
42 IF NUMBERP FIRST BUTFIRST :PLAY THEN MAKE 'Y.POSITION (NTH 2 :PLAY) MAKE 'X.POSITION (NUMEQUIV NTH 3 :PLAY) GO 50
44 MAKE 'X.POSITION (NUMEQUIV NTH 2 :PLAY)
46 MAKE 'Y.POSITION (NTH 3 :PLAY)
50 MAKE 'THE.SCORE PLAY.WORD :X.POSITION :Y.POSITION :DIRECTION FIRST :PLAY
52 IF :THE.SCORE = 'BLECH THEN PRINT [TRY ANOTHER PLAY] GO 30
55 PUT.SCORE 19.6 PLAYER.Y :PLAYER :THE.SCORE
62 PUTP :PLAYER PLUS :THE.SCORE GETP :PLAYER '.SCORE '.SCORE
70 PUT.SCORE 21.5 PLAYER.Y :PLAYER GETP :PLAYER '.SCORE
80 PUTP :PLAYER PICK.HAND GETP :PLAYER '.HAND '.HAND
84 IF :COUNT.PLAY \ COUNT :PLAYERS > 0 THEN MAKE 'X (((:COUNT.PLAY \ COUNT :PLAYERS) - 1) * 8) - 3 ELSE IF :COUNT.PLAY \ COUNT :PLAYERS = 0 THEN MAKE 'X (((COUNT :PLAYERS) - 1) * 8) - 3
85 PUT.HAND :X GETP :PLAYER '.HAND
90 MAKE 'COUNT.PLAY :COUNT.PLAY + 1
95 SAVEBOARD
100 GO 20
END

TO CHECK.WORD :X :Y :DIRECTION :WORD :TEMP.HAND :COUNT.WORD
10 IF EMPTYP :WORD THEN IF :COUNT.WORD = 7 THEN OUTPUT 'BONUS ELSE OUTPUT :TEMP.HAND
15 MAKE 'TILE FIRST :WORD
20 IF EITHER BOTH COUNT :WORD > 16 - :X :DIRECTION = 'SLEEPING BOTH COUNT :WORD > 16 - :Y :DIRECTION = 'STANDING THEN PRINT [WORD IS TOO LONG IT WONT FIT ON THE BOARD, TRY AGAIN] OUTPUT 'TRY.AGAIN
30 IF NOT LETTERS :X :Y = FIRST :WORD THEN IF NOT EMPTYP LETTERS :X :Y THEN PRINT (SENTENCE [YOU CANT ENTER] FIRST :WORD [IN THE SPACE WHERE] LETTERS :X :Y [IS]) OUTPUT 'TRY.AGAIN
33 IF :DIRECTION = 'STANDING THEN CHECK.STAND :X :Y
35 IF :DIRECTION = 'SLEEPING THEN CHECK.SLEEP :X :Y
36 IF LETTERS :X :Y THEN MAKE 'TILE 'NOTHING GO 40 ELSE MAKE 'COUNT.WORD :COUNT.WORD + 1
37 IF (MEMBER FIRST :WORD :TEMP.HAND) THEN GO 40
38 IF MEMBER SPACE :TEMP.HAND THEN MAKE 'BLANK.WORD REPLACE FIRST :WORD SPACE FIRST :PLAY MAKE 'BLANK FIRST :WORD MAKE 'TILE SPACE GO 40
39 PRINT (SENTENCE FIRST :WORD [IS NOT A LETTER IN YOUR HAND]) OUTPUT 'TRY.AGAIN
40 IF :DIRECTION = 'STANDING THEN OUTPUT CHECK.WORD :X :Y + 1 :DIRECTION BUTFIRST :WORD TAKE.OUT :TILE :TEMP.HAND :COUNT.WORD ELSE OUTPUT CHECK.WORD :X + 1 :Y :DIRECTION BUTFIRST :WORD TAKE.OUT :TILE :TEMP.HAND :COUNT.WORD
END

END

TO PLAY.WORD :X :Y :DIRECTION :WORD
5 LOCAL 'THE.SCORE
7 MAKE 'CONNECTED FALSE
8 MAKE 'BLANK.WORD :WORD
9 MAKE 'TEMP.HAND CHECK.WORD :X :Y :DIRECTION :WORD GETP :PLAYER '.HAND 0
10 IF :TEMP.HAND = 'TRY.AGAIN THEN OUTPUT 'BLECH ELSE GO 11
11 IF NOT :CONNECTED THEN IF :COUNT.PLAY > 1 THEN PRINT [YOUR WORD MUST BE ATTACHED TO ANOTHER WORD ON THE BOARD] OUTPUT 'BLECH
13 ENTER.WORD :X :Y :DIRECTION :BLANK.WORD
15 MAKE 'THE.SCORE PLUS SCORE.WORD :X :Y :DIRECTION SCORE.CROSS.WORD :X :Y :DIRECTION
18 IF :TEMP.HAND = 'BONUS THEN MAKE 'THE.SCORE PLUS :THE.SCORE 50
22 PUT.WORD :X :Y :DIRECTION :BLANK.WORD
30 ZAP.BONUS :X :Y
35 IF :TEMP.HAND = 'BONUS THEN PUTP :PLAYER NIL '.HAND ELSE PUTP :PLAYER :TEMP.HAND '.HAND
40 OUTPUT :THE.SCORE
END

23 PRINT [FIRST TYPE THE COORDINATE OF AXIS ALONG WHICH THE WORD LIES]
26 PRINT [THEN TYPE THE COORDINATE OF THE PERPENDICULAR AXIS WHERE THE FIRST LETTER WILL BE PLACED]
85 PRINT [$don't$ FORGET TO LEAVE SPACES AS INDICATED]
90 PRINT [IF DURING THE COURSE OF THE GAME, YOU FORGET THESE INSTRUCTIONS,]
100 PRINT [SIMPLY TYPE $*$ OOPS, AND I WILL REAPPEAR]
110 PRINT [WHEN YOU STRIKE THE RETURN BAR THE GAME WILL CONTINUE]
END

TO SAVEBOARD
10 MAKEWINDOW 'SAVE.BOARD
END

TO OOPS
10 SAVEBOARD
15 SLEEP 0.5
20 NODISPLAY
23 TVSIZE 570 200
26 SHOWWINDOW 'CHECK.NOTATION
30 NOTENOTE
40 REQUEST
50 NODISPLAY TVSIZE 570 370 (SHOWWINDOW 'SAVE.BOARD)
END

TO ZAP.BONUS :X :Y
10 IF EMPTYP LETTERS :X :Y THEN STOP
20 STORE BONUS :X :Y 'FILLED
25 RESTORE.BLANK :X :Y
30 ZAP.BONUS COMPUTE.X :X :DIRECTION COMPUTE.Y :Y :DIRECTION
END

TO RESTORE.BLANK :X :Y
10 IF LETTERS :X :Y = SPACE THEN STORE LETTERS :X :Y :BLANK
END

TO COMPUTE.CROSS.X :X :INCREMENT
10 OUTPUT PLUS :X :INCREMENT
END

TO COMPUTE.CROSS.Y :Y :INCREMENT
10 OUTPUT PLUS :Y :INCREMENT
END

TO FIND.BEG.X :X :Y
10 IF EMPTYP LETTERS :X :Y THEN OUTPUT :X + 1
20 OUTPUT FIND.BEG.X :X - 1 :Y
END

TO FIND.BEG.Y :X :Y
10 IF EMPTYP LETTERS :X :Y THEN OUTPUT :Y + 1
20 OUTPUT FIND.BEG.Y :X :Y - 1
END

TO CHECK.NOTATION
10 IF COUNT :PLAY < 3 THEN OOPS OUTPUT 'OOPS
20 IF NUMBERP FIRST :PLAY THEN PRINT [$you've$ GOOFED THE NOTATION....] OOPS OUTPUT 'OOPS
30 IF NUMBERP NTH 2 :PLAY THEN IF NUMBERP NTH 3 :PLAY THEN PRINT [$you've$ GOOFED THE NOTATION...] OOPS OUTPUT 'OOPS
40 IF NOT NUMBERP NTH 2 :PLAY THEN IF NOT NUMBERP NTH 3 :PLAY THEN PRINT [$you've$ GOOFED THE NOTATION....]
END

TO INSTRUCT
10 NODISPLAY
20 TVSIZE 570 200
30 SHOWWINDOW 'CHECK.NOTATION
40 NOTENOTE
END

TO TRADE.TILES :TILES
5 IF EMPTYP :TILES THEN STOP
10 PUTP :PLAYER TAKE.OUT FIRST :TILES GETP :PLAYER '.HAND '.HAND
20 PUTP :PLAYER PICK.HAND GETP :PLAYER '.HAND '.HAND
30 TRADE.TILES BUTFIRST :TILES
END

TO TECO
10 VALRET '$tecojp$
END

TO SHADE.SQUARE :X :Y :PzL FATTERN
5 IF (STATUS FEATURE COLOR) THEN PENCOLOR :BACKGROUND
10 GO.SQUARE :X :Y
15 IF (STATUS FEATURE COLOR) THEN PENCOLOR :PATTERN
20 SHOWWINDOW :PATTERN :XCOR :YCOR
END

MAKE 'REDEFINE 'T
MAKE 'LISPBREAK 'NIL
MAKE 'EDITMODE 'T

TO SIMLAST :L
10 IF EMPTYP BUTFIRST :L THEN OUTPUT FIRST :L
20 OUTPUT SIMLAST BUTFIRST :L
END

TO PREMIERE :LIST.FN
10 OUTPUT THING WORD :LIST.FN 'FIRST
END

TO MAKE.LIST :WORD1 :WORD2
10 MAKE 'FUNNY.WORD WORD 'FUNNY RANDOM
20 MAKE WORD :FUNNY.WORD 'FIRST :WORD1
30 MAKE (WORD :FUNNY.WORD 'SECOND) :WORD2
40 OUTPUT :FUNNY.WORD
END

TO FACT :N
5 IF :N < 0 THEN OUTPUT (SENTENCE [NO NO NO] :N [IS A NEGATIVE NUMBER AND YOU $can't$ FACT A NEGATIVE NUMBER])
10 IF :N = 0 THEN OUTPUT 1
20 OUTPUT :N * FACT :N - 1
END

TO SPOLYANA :SIZE :ANGLE :DELTASIZE :DELTANGLE
10 FORWARD :SIZE
20 RIGHT :ANGLE
30 SPOLYANA :SIZE + :DELTASIZE :ANGLE + :DELTANGLE :DELTASIZE :DELTANGLE
END

TO POLYANA :SIZE :ANGLE :DELTA
10 FORWARD :SIZE
20 RIGHT :ANGLE
30 POLYANA :SIZE :ANGLE + :DELTA :DELTA
END

TO SPOLY :SIZE :ANGLE :DELTA
10 FORWARD :SIZE
20 RIGHT :ANGLE
30 SPOLY :SIZE + :DELTA :ANGLE :DELTA
END

TO DO.IT :COMD :TIMES
10 IF :TIMES = 0 THEN STOP
20 RUN :COMD
30 DO.IT :COMD :TIMES - 1
END

TO POLYSIDE :SIZE :ANGLE
10 FORWARD :SIZE
20 RIGHT :ANGLE
END

TO WARDBACK :LIST
10 IF EMPTYP :LIST THEN OUTPUT NIL
15 IF :ZED = BUTFIRST :LIST THEN OUTPUT :LIST
20 OUTPUT SENTENCE (WORDIFY (WARDBACK (LAST :LIST))) (WARDBACK (BUTLAST :LIST))
END

TO WORDIFY :LIST
10 IF EMPTYP :LIST THEN OUTPUT :ZED
20 OUTPUT WORD FIRST :LIST WORDIFY BUTFIRST :LIST
END

TO ROAD :LENGTH
10 RIGHT 90
20 FORWARD :LENGTH / 2
30 LEFT 180
40 FORWARD :LENGTH
50 RIGHT 180
60 FORWARD :LENGTH / 2
70 LEFT 90
END

TO VEE :LENGTH :ANGLE
10 RIGHT :ANGLE / 2
20 FORWARD :LENGTH
30 BACK :LENGTH
40 LEFT :ANGLE
50 FORWARD :LENGTH
60 BACK :LENGTH
70 RIGHT :ANGLE / 2
END

TO ARROW :VEELENGTH :ROADLENGTH :ANGLE
20 LEFT 90
30 FORWARD :ROADLENGTH / 2
40 VEE :VEELENGTH :Ah!NGLE
50 RIGHT 180
60 FORWARD :ROADLENGTH
70 VEE :VEELENGTH :ANGLE
80 LEFT 180
90 FORWARD :ROADLENGTH / 2
100 RIGHT 90
END

TO DANCE :PIC1 :PIC2 :TIME1 :TIME2
10 NIL :PIC1
11 COLORSWITCH
20 SLEEP :TIME1
30 HIDEWINDOW :PIC1
40 NIL :PIC2
50 SLEEP :TIME2
60 HIDEWINDOW :PIC2
70 GO 10
END

TO D :TIME
10 DANCE 'DOG 'WARHEAD :TIME :TIME
END
