and any instantiation of squares?  Will there often be a
confusion between the actor "George", his "script", and
his image on the screen?  Identity becomes very strange
to talk about, since anything about an actor can be changed,
what it looks like, how it acts, even what it is a kind of. (Though this
programming style may be discouraged to avoid this confusion.)
Only its name is permanent.
	Explaining the ticks, plans and universes might bring up problems.
How should one talk about, or think about, many separate
processes going on in parallel?  I don't really know.
	This system lends itself very well to the "little man"
vocabulary.  Each actor is a little person,
who knows a few things,
can be told to do a few things, and can be taught new tricks.
This little person receives messages and sends them to others.
This view of programming lends itself very well to a variation of
playing "turtle."  The children can play games where they are actors
and pass the messages around.
.sec Efficiency Issues
	One may worry that such a system will run too slowly to be
useful for working with children or for building an intelligent system
on top of it.  The message passing and matching involved are much
slower than more traditional mechanisms.  The basic use of
hierarchies is slow, since each actor seldom can respond directly
to a message but needs to pass it on to the class which it is a member.
	The answer to this objection is standard.  One should let a
compiler worry about such efficiencies.  I have implemented a few
macros that when possible replace actor transmissions with the code
that they would invoke.  I also plan to "compile" patterns in the
receivers to run faster.  The price for some of these hacks is less flexibility.
If the transmission "ASK FRED '(APPEAR FORWARD 100)" is replaced by the action part
of the receiver in Object with "Fred" and "100" instantiated properly,
then telling Fred's immediate superior a new way to handle "forward"
message will not affect his behavior if he is "compiled."
        There are other efficiency hacks that may be worthwhile,
for example, in the memory system for
the actors.  I plan, however,
to follow the principle that the code should be written clearly and
simply and that efficiency hacks should be below the surface and transparent
to the user.
.sec Extensions and Improvements Planned
	One useful extension would be the addition of primitives
for joining and breaking apart "Object" actors.  For example,
one may want to join a triangle actor and a square actor to make
a house actor.  Or one may want to have a face accept messages
as well as any of it parts.  A person may get into a car, so that
temporarily any movement of the car should also change the state of
the person.  Some progress has been made here, so that simple
composite objects can be defined but more needs to be done.
	Another improvement being considered is the ability to handle
partial messages.  For example, if an object receives a forward message
without a number as the second word, then instead of the present response
of printing out an error message,  it should prompt the user with a question
like, "How much should Fred go forward?"
	Taking this idea one step further I plan to have a "help" button that
can be pushed at any time.  If one has typed only part of a message and then
the help button, then the actor may be able to finish part of all of the message
for the user.  This feature will hopefully alleviate many of the problems of typing
long names and messages that make the code more readable.

docs.title: The Problem
docs.body:
.sec The Problem
	Computer animation is very limited compared with hand drawn animation.
At the present, it is well adapted only to producing abstract, as opposed to
representational, animation.  The few exceptions are not flexible enough to be of
general usefulness.foot<Refer to Utah system and sketch kind like Ron Baecker's>
  On the other hand, conventional animation is very time
consuming and requires expensive equipment.  As such it is available as a medium
to very few adults and even fewer children.
Many skills are necessary to transform a story board into a finished cartoon,
regardless of the type of animation.
	The research I am proposing is an attempt to alleviate this problem by
equiping a computer with many of the skills necessary for
going from an idea
to a finished cartoon.  This attempt diverges from the usual computer animation
and graphics research by facing the problem of putting the required knowledge into
a computer, rather than relying on the user.
In order for the computer to be a useful apprentice in this endeavor
it must know many animation and cartooning techniques such as in-betweening,
easing in and out, anticipation, exaggeration, flow of motion, composition,
and perspective.
It must also know many things about the real world: if a person is described
as happy then probably that person is smiling, if someone says "Hi" there is
good chance that person raised his or her hand in greeting, people walk by moving
their arms and legs in a particular pattern and so on.
The computer needs to able to accept
statements about a character or object
and take those facts into account while constructing
the animated scenes.  All of these facts about the world, the characters, and
the necessary animation techniques
must interact with the intended story in such a way that
a reasonable approximation of the user's idea is converted into a cartoon.
	Clearly, this is a huge problem. Therefore we are involved in exploring
only a limited portion of this problem.  The hope is that, if this is successful, a
more complete system could be considered.  The major limitations envisioned are:
foot<More limitations will probably become necessary as this research progresses.>
.begin narrow 5,5 single space
.skip
(1) The only characters the system will know about are a few of those in Schultz's
Peanuts World,

(2) The computer will understand only simple activities.  For example, it will
understand walking, running, talking, turning and the like, but will [fi]not[fr]
understand more complex or unusual activities such as fighting, skipping rope, and
mountain climbing.

(3) The drawings will be simple line drawings,

(4) The English that the user uses to communicate with the system will need to
be simple and stylized.
.end
.skip 4
.begin center select 6
A
.end
.sec Motivation for Creating such a System
	The reasons for building such a system are many and varied.
.begin narrow 5,5
(1) Artificial Intelligence (AI):  This research is being carried out at the MIT Artificial
Intelligence Laboratory where the problems of instilling intelligence in computers
and of understanding natural intelligence are being explored.  The animation system
should be a good domain within which to explore the
problems of representing real-world knowledge and
common sense, and of handling
interactions of many different sources of knowledge, such as
animation techniques, cartooning techniques, the Peanuts characters and their world,
human emotions and how they influence people's appearance, and the story
presented by the user.

(2) Educational: The system could provide a different and stimulating environment
for children to learn to express themselves well, to learn,
about animation and film in the role of a director,
to learn how to criticize their own work
in order for it to be improved, and to get experience working on relatively long-term
projects.  These activities fit well with the activities of the
LOGO Laboratory,foot<This research is
being sponsored by the LOGO Lab.  The original idea of this project came from Prof. Ira
Goldstein of the Lab and was initially explored by a seminar led by him.>
 a part
of the Artificial Intelligence Laboratory,
which among other things involves children in learning by
programming within a specially designed computer language on well-chosen problems.
The teaching of programming to children and this intelligent animation project
can be more directly linked if the children are encouraged to
modify and add new things to the animation system, in addition to simply telling
stories and criticizing resulting cartoons.  If the system is designed and
implemented properly then there is a good chance that the children will be able
to learn enough about how the system works to consider it a "white box."  That is
they might understand how the system "thinks", thereby providing them with an example to
help them in being more explicit about their own thinking.

(3) Computer Graphics and Animation: To our knowledge, this will be the first time
that a computer animation system knew what it doing, in addition, to doing it.
This knowledge should enable the system to make reasonable guesses, rather than require
the user to explicitly describe each detail.  For example, the system will, unless
told otherwise, make assumptions as to whether a particular character is
smiling, where to place the characters and background, how fast to move things
and the like.  In addition, the system is to be built upon primitives that are very
well adapted to animation.  They are based
on the "actor" semantics of computation of Carl Hewitt foot<Hewitt, C. and Smith B.
Towards a Programming Apprentice ..> and Alan Kay foot<Kay, A. ...>
The relation of this research to this advanced programming language technology
is discussed in the appendix.

(4) Entertainment:  This may be the first time that animation would become an
[fi]active[fr] event in people's activities, as opposed to the usual passive viewing of
other's work.
.end
.begin center select 6
B
.end
.sec The Basic Idea
	The system, as presently envisioned,
 will go through the following steps in its normal mode of operation:
.begin narrow 5,5 single space
(1) translate the user's story or script into an internal representation;

(2) for each episode in the story,
generate symbolic descriptions of successive key-frames;foot<By the term,
"key-frame", I mean "film" frames
that are interpolated between.  This is not to be confused with
Minksy frames which are closer to what I call "episodes" later.>

(3) find differences between the descriptions
of successive key-frames,
thereby generating a description of the transformation between them;

(4) use the transformation or difference description to generate commands to
the appropriate "actors" which result in commands to the display;

(5) alter the appropriate
key-frames and transformations and repeat steps (3) through (5) if the user criticizes
the resulting cartoon.
.end
	If at any point in the performance of steps 1 through 3 the system is confused
or forced to make too many unfounded assumptions, then a dialog would be initiated with
the user and advice would be sought.
.skip 4
.begin center select 6
A
.end

.sec An Example
	An imaginary scenario
is perhaps appropriate here.  Suppose the system was told the
following story (based on the cartoon in Figure 1):
.begin narrow 8,8
	Charlie Brown walks up to Lucy and greets her by saying, "Happy New Year,
Lucy!"
The background is a grass field.
	Lucy asks, "Does that make it so?" Charlie
Brown has a look of puzzlement and interest.
Lucy continues, "Does your saying, 'Happy New Year'
MAKE it happy?"
Lucy's arms are upraised as sign of emphasis.
Lucy goes on, "Just because you SAY it does that
mean it WILL be?"
Lucy's arms are outstretched as sign of question.
Charlie Brown has a facial expression of confusion.
	Lucy shouts, "IS THIS A GUARANTEE?  IS THIS ..."
	Charlie Brown says, "Oh, good grief."
.end
	This may be translated into the following internal representation:foot<Note
that the details of this example are [fi]ad hoc[fr] since the system is at such an early
stage of development.>
.begin nofill
.skip
[fl] (Background grass-field)
 (key-frame 1
     (Charlie Brown (walking (towards Lucy))
	      	    (says "Happy New Year, Lucy!")
	      	    (greeting Lucy))
     (Lucy (on screen)))
 (key-frame 2
     (Lucy (asks "Does that make it so?")))
 (key-frame 3
     (Charlie Brown (expression (and interest puzzlement)))
     (Lucy (arms up-raised)
	   (expression emphatic)
	   (asks "Does ... ?")))
 (key-frame 4
     (Charlie Brown (expression confusion))
     (Lucy (asks "Just because ...")
	   (arms out-stretched)
	   (expression questioning)))
 (key-frame 5
     (Lucy (shouts "IS THIS A GUARANTEE?")))
 (key-frame 6
     (Charlie Brown (says "Oh, good grief"))
     (Lucy (continues "IS THIS ...")))
.end
	The next task of the system is to fill into these key frames
important details that are not explicitly given by the text.
This is accomplished by
the use of defaults and very simple common sense inferences.
The "complete" descriptions of the key-frames follows (the notation "<<...>>"
is used to distinguish the assumptions made):
.begin nofill skip
[fl] (key-frame 1
     (Charlie Brown (walking (towards Lucy))
	 (says "Happy New Year, Lucy!")
	 (greeting Lucy)
	 <<arm extended>>
	;[fi] based on the greeting sterotype[fr]
	 <<expression mildly-friendly>>
	;[fi]since a greeting is going on[fr]
	 <<position screen-left>>
	;[fi] this choice is arbitrary [fr]
	 <<orientation full-right-profile>>
	;[fi]based on the above position and "(walking (towards Lucy))"[fr]
	 <<clothes default-Charlie-Brown>>
	;[fi]a smarter version would know that "Happy New Year" indicates (clothes winter))[fr]
     (Lucy <<standing (facing Charlie Brown)>>
	;[fi]This is assumed since she is talking with him[fr]
	   <<orientation full-left-profile>>
	;[fi]since she is facing Charlie Brown[fr]
	   <<expression mild>>
	;[fi]either default or for contrast with future frames[fr]
	   <<arms down>>;[fi]standard default[fr]
	   <<position screen-right>>
	;[fi]since there are only 2 people and Charlie Brown already is "screen-left"[fr]
	   <<clothes default-lucy>>))
.skip 2
  (key-frame 2
     (Charlie Brown
	   <<standing (facing Lucy)>> ;[fi]since he is talking with her[fr]
	   <<arms down>>)
	;[fi] people get tired of extending their arms, so if nothing to the contrary[fr]
	;[fi] is indicated then they are lowered. The rest is "no-change"[fr]
     (Lucy (asks "Does that make it so?")))
	;[fi] the rest is "no-change" and so need not be repeated[fn]
.once indent 0
The other key-frames would also be expanded of course but will not be here.
.end
	Next the transformations are created based on the
differences between the key frames.  The transformation between the first
two key-frames follows:
.begin nofill
.skip
[fl] (Transformation key-frame1 key-frame2
 	(Charlie Brown (walking standing)
		       (arms (one extended) down)
	;[fi]note there is no position change except that implicit in "walking"[fr]
	    	       (talking listening))
	(Lucy (listening talking)))
.end
	This results in calls to the processes or actors involved. For example,
the transformation "(Charlie Brown (arms (one extended) down)),"
(which means that Charlie
Brown's arm changes from being extended in the first key-frame to where
both arms are down) becomes a call to the "actor" Charlie Brown with the message
"(arms down)".  This actor or process interprets the message,
making assumptions as necessary such as the use of his right arm.  The movement
would take into account reasonable speeds for such movements, easing in and out,
and the like.
	After the "actors" are called, a movie consisting of a list of commands
to the computer's display is produced which is shown.
At that point, the user may enter criticism or advice. Some
examples are:
.begin nofill
.skip
 "No, have Charlie Brown come in from screen right"
 "Charlie Brown should walk in front of the tree not behind it"
 "Ok, but the eyes in the last part should open wider."
 "Lucy turned her head too fast."
 "We should zoom in on Lucy when she shouts"
 "Half way through they should turn towards the 'audience'."
 "Something is wrong in the last scene but I don't know what."
 "Instead of the first cut try a fast dissolve."
.end
	It should be noted that understanding
and determining what should be done when such comments are entered
are very difficult problems.
.skip 6
.begin center select 6
B
.end

.sec Key-Frames and Transformations
	Key-frames are film frames that characterize a scene.  They are
symbolic descriptions of who and what is on the screen and in what form.
They are essentially static descriptions of the world at a point in time.
When enough things change a new key-frame is generated.  For example, when the
"camera" position changes significantly or when the characters change
position, orientation, or expression significantly then a new key-frame
describing the new situation is created.  Of course, the new
key-frame typically inherits much of its description from the previous
frames.
	Transformations are descriptions of the differences
between two successive key-frames.  Some elements will correspond to
differences such as "before his arm was up and now it is at his side," while
most will correspond to more dynamic descriptions of the change such
 as "his arm is being
lowered until it comes to his side."  Both of these examples indicate essentially
the same action on the screen; however, there are cases where only one kind of
description is appropriate.  For example, "He ran very fast up to Lucy" is best
represented as a dynamic transformation.  Presently, it is not clear if there are
cases where it is more natural or concise to represent transformations as state
changes, but there are cases where not enough is known to guess the process.
For example, if a character is described as having brown hair and then minutes
later as having blond hair, the system is unlikely to know enough to guess that
the hair was dyed and so must either change the color abruptly or slowly, but
it can not interpolate a scene where bleach is being added to the hair.
It should be noted that transformations are only partially
derived from key-frame comparisons, sometimes they will be part of the
initial story, for example, the story may have included the phrase, "he
jumped up and down."
.subsec Semantic Interpolation or Symbolic Difference
	Usually computer animation systems that can interpolate between
two scenes do so without any knowledge of what the objects are that
are being "in-betweened."  Usually a point (or a line or a parameterized curve)
in one scene is
a associated with a point (line or curve) in the other.  Then the creation of
in-between positions for that point (or line) is computationally simple.
There are at least two
problems with this method though.  First, points in the source
are easily associated with
the wrong target points.   Second,
natural motion it seldom produced.  Take as an example
a stick figure walking.  A good speed and rate of easing in and out for the
arms may not be the same for the legs.  The rest of the body is also moving
and yet not a constant rate.  Meanwhile there may be a breeze going through a tree
in the background that requires another rhythm.
	The proposed knowledgable animation system would solve these problems in
two ways.  The interpolation is done not on the points or lines in the scene
but on a [fi]symbolic[fr] description of the scene.  This use of symbolic
descriptions greatly simplifies the problem as has been demonstrated in many of the AI
programs of the last five or ten years.  Rather than comparing the points or
lines of, say, Charlie Brown's face, we can compare simple descriptions of his
eyes, mouth, ears, and face.  The description of the differences between the symbolic
descriptions of two key-frames is also more useful than a lower-level description
in terms of points or lines.  It may describe his mouth as first happy and then
angry.  In that case, the procedure that knows about mouths in general and
the procedure that knows about
any peculiarities of Charlie Brown's mouth would then be in a good position to
generate the in-betweens for his mouth.  The knowledge of how an effective
change of expression appears is then brought to bear, rather than relying on
some general interpolation mechanism.   It should be noted that if several
body parts interpolate in a similar manner then it should be easy for them to share
the same knowledge.
.subsec The Vocabulary of Key-frames and Transformations
	After it is decided that symbolic descriptions of scenes and transformations
between scenes will be used, the question arises as to what
an appropriate vocabulary for the objects, relations
and actions is.  To get a feel for the problems involved I studied the drawings
of Charlie Brown's face in a couple of Schultz's Peanuts books.  The outcome was
encouraging for it seems as if almost any expression found in the books can be
constructed selecting eyes, ears, mouths, etc. from a small "catalog" of parts of the
head.  For example, see Figure 2 < use old diagram that Karen redrew>.
	Relations between objects and actions have not been investigated very much
yet.  Hopefully, we will find that only a relatively small number of descriptors
will suffice for the simple animation we have in mind.  A small size
vocabulary of actions and objects
is desirable because knowledge must be attached to each
descriptor about how to interpolate, how to
make reasonable default judgements, and the like.
One may object that by classifying objects, actions and relations into coarse groupings
that subtleties that have important effects on the viewer are lost.  This is a
possibility, but it is premature to propose a computer AI system that would produce
cartoons of anywhere near professional
quality.  Any lack of subtleties, while unfortunate, will not significantly affect
the success of the system as a learning environment and as a prototype for subsequent
more advanced systems.  We must learn to walk before we attempt to run.
.subsec Defaults and the Role of the Episode
	An episode is a coherent piece of the cartoon, usually separated from
other episodes by a cut or dissolve.  It is within each episode that key-frames
are generated.  In order for reasonable defaults to be chosen, the system needs
to identify with each part of the story a sterotyped or generalized episode.  These
episodes are similar to what Schank calls "scripts"foot<Schank ...>
and Minksy calls "frames."foot<Minsky, M. ...>  For
example, the system will have sterotypes for episodes such as, someone greeting another,
or someone arguing with another, or people playing baseball.  These structures
contain much information, thereby lessening the demands on the user to describe the
story in tedious detail.  For example, once a story fragment is recognized as
an instance of a greeting, then
(unless there is reason to assume otherwise) the character
will raise his or her hand while saying the greeting and perhaps the other character
will nod.  In addition, the sterotyped episode would contain such defaults
as the normal duration of a greeting, the normal facial expressions and the like.
	An episode provides much of the details necessary to produce the calls to
the lower-level drawing and animation primitives.  There are, however, other sources
of defaults.   Continuing with the greeting example, suppose the sterotype
for greeting episodes indicates
that Charlie Brown should raise his arm.  The sterotype knows nothing special about
Charlie Brown and so should not decide whether Charlie
Brown should use his left hand or right hand.  This knowledge
would exist elsewhere, for example, in the description of a typical person which might
indicate that if one hand is carrying something use the other, if both are busy then
suggest that perhaps no hand should be raised, and if both hands are free then pick the
right arm.  Now it might be that Charlie Brown is left-handed and so should
act differently in the last case.  This information would be associated with Charlie
Brown and would be considered before
more general information about people.  In other words,
I envision the use of a hierarchy of knowledge and appropriate inheritance operators.
.subsec The Role of Animation Knowledge in this Process
	It is obvious that an animator, in addition to knowing about the real world,
knows much about animation techniques, many of which apply equally well to
computer animation as to hand-drawn animation.  For example, the animator knows
how to ease into and out of a motion, how to exaggeration an action and how to produce
a more dramatic effect by providing contrast to anticipated actions.  A good animator
also has a well-developed sense of timing and rhythm.  He or she knows how long
certain acts should take and how to mix different acts together to produce a
rhythm in the film.
Also, the animator
knows how to produce film effects such as dissolves, zooms, pans and cuts.  Probably
most important an animator knows when to apply these techniques.
	Clearly, I need to imbed these techniques and advice about their application
into the computer animator.  The first task I face is grouping these techniques into
categories.  For instance, dissolves, pans, zooms and cuts might all be grouped into
"camera" techniques.  Easing in and out and in-betweening perhaps should be grouped
as "motion" techniques.  Anticipation, exaggeration, and focusing
attention perhaps should be called "dramatic" techniques.
	[fi]Camera techniques[fr]
could be procedures within the computer that are suggested
by the episode sterotypes.  Perhaps the greeting episode would suggest a
pan from the person greeting towards the other at a medium "camera" distance.  The
argument episode may suggest that quick cutting and close-ups on the faces of the
debaters would be best.  Cuts would, in general, be used to separate episodes and so on.
These would only be suggestions and a computerized "aesthetics" critic may notice that,
say, too many dissolves have been going on and either veto the suggestion or propose
a cut as an alternative.
	The [fi]motion techniques[fr] could be procedures that are suggested
by the object being affected.  In that way trees would know how to react to wind
in a way that is different from how an arm would move or an eye close.  As is discussed
later all these objects are imbedded in a hierarchy which describes what class
each object is a member of, and in turn what class each class is a member of and so on.
For example,
Charlie Brown's left arm is an instance of Charlie Brown's arms, which are instances
of arms (if adult arms were different, another class could be inserted), which are a
kind of limb, which is a kind of body part, which is an object.  Any advice as to
the natural flow of an object is placed at the highest level of generality that is
appropriate.  If there is something special about arms that does not apply to legs
then that information is associated with arms.  In moving, say, Charlie Brown's left arm
advice is searched for starting with the instance of the arm, then to Charlie's arms,
then arms in general, and so on.  Any advice that contradicts,
in a straight-forward manner, advice that came from
a more specific element of the hierarchy is rejected.
	The problems of deciding when the [fi]dramatic techniques[fr] are appropriate is
similar to the problem discussed in the next section on how the cartoonist's knowledge
could be imbeded into the computer animator.
.subsec The Artist's or Cartoonist's Knowledge
	A very important aspect of animation is the drawings themselves considered as stills.
The cartoonist's understanding of
composition, balance, point of attention, contrast, and the like is important for
producing aesthetically pleasing cartoons.  These techniques along with the animator's
dramatic techniques need to be incorporated into the system; however at this point,
it is not too clear where.  Some of the knowledge can go into the "aesthetics" critics
mentioned earlier that reject or modify
suggestions based on what preceded and
on what will follow the current frames.
In the process of filling in the key-frames this knowledge is used in a more positive way.
Contrast and point of attention will be called upon to help place
the characters and elements of the background
whose position is under-specified.  The episode will also
have suggestions about the use of these techniques,
for example, the argument episode knows that, in general, the end
of an argument is the most dramatic part and perhaps should be emphasized by contrast,
exaggeration and anticipation.
.skip 6
.begin center select 6
A
.end
.sec Summary
	This research while promising is at very early stages.  The actor-based animation
system described in the appendix works but is in need of many improvements.  The nature
of key-frames, episodes, critics, and the vocabulary needed are being explored and much
remains to be done.  The educational aspect of the project is the most exciting part,
yet other than keeping it in mind, little can be done now.  Children will be using the
actor-based animation system this spring, and hopefully improvements will become obvious
and problems discovered at an early enough stage to do something about it.  But as
to whether the system will really work, and, if so, whether it will provide an new kind
of environment to learn about animation, story telling, communication and thinking,
remains to be seen.

.skip 5
.begin center select 6
A
.end

docs.title: An Actor System for Kids
docs.body:
.sec What this is
	This document assumes familiarity with Hewitt's
Actor concepts.  I will only incidentally argue it's
relative merits with LOGO, the purpose is more to present
ideas as to how to sweeten up the syntax of Plasma
to be useable by kids in a LOGO-like way.
	This document was inspired by Danny Hillis's
"Ten Things to do with a Better Computer".
My uneasiness with the syntax, that I believe Danny also
feels, is the main problem that this addresses.  It proposes
a system built upon an actor system called PLASMA.  The interface
or editor is improved for use by beginners.  At any point help
in terms of the proper inputs to procedures and the insertion
of parenthesis and pretty printing is available.
	Comments welcome.
.sec Examples based on factorial for lack of imagination
.subsec The Cases statement of the recursive version
1) Each actor knows how to prompt for it's body or datapart.
The prompting will only occur if asked for, in the following
examples that is almost always the case.  This is not meant
to be realistic, only instructive.  A reasonable way for
the user to ask for help would be to hit the help button at
any point during input (or editting). In all the examples
the help button is pushed after typing the name of an actor
or after typing in only some of the inputs to an actor (or
more accurately only part of the pattern of the receiver of
an actor).  Ideally the help button would have meaning at
all times, for instance, hitting after typing in part on an
"atom" it would show the various ways the atom could be
completed or complete it if unique.
	As an example of the normal use of the "help" button,
suppose the user was defining factorial and came to the cases
statement the following dialog might result:
.begin
.nofill
user: cases
SYSTEM: PLEASE TYPE IN THE FIRST CASE.
user: receive 0
SYSTEM: WHAT SHOULD BE DONE IF A 0 IS RECEIVED?
user: return 1
[fi];Notice that "return" is meta-syntactic, i.e. does not exist in
;the target language and is used only to indicate that the last
;clause of the action of the receiver is finished.  It has been
;suggested that this be only a single button.[fr]
SYSTEM: PLEASE TYPE IN ANOTHER CASE.
user : receive number
SYSTEM: NUMBER HAS NO MEANING HERE,
	 I ASSUME YOU MEAN "=NUMBER"
[fi];At this point the system puts "=number" into the program
;buffer and display and the user must press "rubout"
;to undo the system's rather conservative assumption.[fr]
SYSTEM: WHAT SHOULD BE DONE AFTER
	=NUMBER MATCHES WHAT IT IS SENT?
user: return number * (factorial (number - 1))
[fi];Note that this too could have been prompted.
;If this were prompted, it would go more smoothly if one were
;to stick to prefix functions.[fr]
SYSTEM: I ASSUME THE CASES STATEMENT IS FINISHED.
[fi];Again the user can undo this reasonable (since =number will catch anything) assumption.[fr]
.end
.subsec Why this looks winning
Many functions are served by such a prompter system:

.begin skip indent 10
.nofill
1) simple errors that are a pain are caught at definition time
2) the user need not remember syntax nor argument order
3) messy nesting and grinding is avoided at definition time
4) the meaning of the primitive (cases is this case) is clearer
5) parsing is simplified and is less mysterious
.end
	Notice that if the user is sophisticated with respect to some
primitives the prompter will not prompt.  The prompter askes its
questions only if a form is unfinished, e.g. "cases" is typed
followed by the "help" button. In the above example in response
to the first question by the system the user could have typed:

user: receive 0 return 1

.once indent 0
and the system would have gone on to ask about the next case,
if the "help" button were pushed.
	I envision this system only winning on a display
console, where waiting for the system to type is not a problem, and where
it is possible to have part of display show the code as it
is being defined (in addition to the last question (or two)).
The best arrangement th`zought of so far, is where the typing
of "code" is incremently pretty printed as it comes in, either
straight, or in response to a question or prompt.
.subsec The rest of the recursive factorial
	A problem is that this works best for actors
.turn off ""
that are the first word in a form.
For example, the defining distinguished symbol, "[fl][fr]" should
not be typed first.  A new name for it is needed when it is
in the first position so to define factorial a dialog like
this would occur:
.begin
.nofill
user: define (or teach or whatever)
SYSTEM: WHAT SHALL WE CALL THIS?
user: factorial
SYSTEM: WHAT IS IT'S DEFINITION?
user: cases
 ...same as above
SYSTEM: I ASSUME  THE DEFINITION OF FACTORIAL IS COMPLETE.
SYSTEM: FACTORIAL DEFINED
.end
.subsec A Typing Objection
	A fair objection is that this a lot of
typing to get out of a 5th grader.
Let's compare it to it's LOGO equivalent.
.begin
.nofill
[fl]TO FACTORIAL :NUMBER
10 IF :NUMBER = 0 OUTPUT 1
20 OUTPUT NUMBER * FACTORIAL N - 1
END
.end
In the prompter version about 70 characters are typed,
in the LOGO about 65 or practically the same (since
shorter words than "define" or "receive" could
be used or abbreviations are possible, or the help completion
mode mentioned above could be used). For meta-linguistic forms such as
"help", "return", and "rubout" they should, and hopefully
would be single buttons.
Also it seems
plausible that typing to an interactive system would be
more interesting.
.subsec Editing
	For editing let us suppose that we want to modify the
definition so that a complaint is sent to a complaint
department if a negative number is sent to factorial.
.begin
.nofill
user: edit factorial
[fi];If factorial was not mentioned the name of the procedure would be requested.[fr]
SYSTEM: DO YOU WANT TO ADD, REMOVE, OR
	CHANGE A CASE IN THE CASES STATEMENT?
[fi];Since there is only one form (the cases statement) in the
definition the buck was passed to cases.[fr]
user: add
SYSTEM: WHAT IS THE CONDITION?
user: receive (< 0)
SYSTEM: WHAT SHOULD BE DONE IF SOMETHING
	LESS THAN 0 IS RECEIVED?
user: tell complaint-department [factorial doesn't like negative numbers]
SYSTEM: SHOULD THIS BE BEFORE OR AFTER
	THE "RECEIVE 0, RETURN 1" CASE?
[fi];Cases should know that nothing can fail to match the "receive =number" condition[fr]
user: doesn't matter
SYSTEM: ARE YOU FINISHED EDITING FACTORIAL?
user: yes
SYSTEM: FACTORIAL DEFINED
.end
At this point the user may notice that this condition is checked
every time and hopefully need not be checked on all the recursive
calls.  Many different patches are possible.
.subsec An Iterative Factorial Example
	While this is not the place to argue the relative merits of
Actors verses Logo the comparison between the iterative
implementations of factorial in each is perhaps instructive.
.begin
.nofill
LOGO:
[fl]TO FACTORIAL :NUMBER
10 LOCAL "ACCUMULATION
20 MAKE "ACCUMULATION 1
30 IF :NUMBER = 0 OUTPUT :ACCUMULATION
40 MAKE "ACCUMULATION :ACCUMULATION * :NUMBER
50 MAKE "NUMBER :NUMBER - 1
60 GO 30
END[fr]

Actor Prompter:

user: define factorial
SYSTEM: WHAT IS THE DEFINITION OF FACTORIAL?
user: first receive =number
SYSTEM: WHAT SHOULD BE DONE AFTER MATCHING "=NUMBER"?
user: tell loop [number 1]
SYSTEM: LOOP IS NOT DEFINED; WHAT IS ITS DEFINITION?
[fi];Note that the user should have a "postpone" button for situations
;like this and the system would remind the user later.[fr]
user: receive [=count =accumulation]
SYSTEM: WHAT SHOULD BE DONE AFTER RECEIVING THAT?
user: rules
SYSTEM: RULES FOR WHAT?
user: count
SYSTEM: PLEASE TYPE IN A RULE, OR
	WHEN FINISHED HIT THE "COMPLETE" BUTTON.
user: receive 0, return accumulation
SYSTEM: OK, PLEASE TYPE IN ANOTHER RULE.
[fi];This might be the standard request after the first rule is in[fr]
user: otherwise tell loop [(number - 1 ) (accumulation * number)]
[fi];This too could have been prompted of course[fr]
SYSTEM: I ASSUME THE DEFINITION OF FACTORIAL IS OVER.
[fi];Some smarts are needed to do this, "rules" must know that nothing
;can follow "otherwise" (or "else"). etc.[fr]
.end
	Some people may not find the above especially
clear, I did not when first introduced to Actor
concepts but now I find it a very good way to think
about recursion versus iteratation.  I think the problem is simply getting
used to message passing and having it explained well.
.sec Comparisons with Danny Hillis' Ideas
	This section assumes the reader is familiar with
Danny Hillis' "Ten Things to do with a Better Computer".
.subsec The Truck Example
Danny's truck is basically very similar.
However packages might be better than unparsed commands
so instead of say,

BACK IS A BOX. TELL BACK "WIDTH 100 HEIGHT 100"

two things could happen with the Actor Prompter:

1) the user types it all in:
.begin indent 8,8

back is a box (width 100) (height 50)

Notice that I think this differs from Danny's in that
he constructs the back without dimensions and later tells
it it's dimensions, while here the notñþÍ,Fion is that back is
a box that is 100 by 50.
.end
2) The prompter is really utilized:
.begin indent 8,8
.nofill
user: back is a box
[fi];Since box expects a couple of packages
;(with the names "width" and "height") the system asks,[fr]
SYSTEM: WHAT IS THE WIDTH OF THE BOX?
user: 100
SYSTEM: AND THE HEIGHT?
user: 50
.end
	This may be a question of taste and style and perhaps
in either system the box can optionally require its dimensions.
.subsec The Grow a Garden Example
Lets compare the two using the "grow a garden example" of Danny's.
The flower definition would differ as above, and in addition
flower would have to know how to grow (perhaps) by passing the buck
to it's parts.  (Danny may be right that this should be behind the scenes.)

Danny's seed definition is:
.begin
.nofill
SEED DOES:
WAIT 10.
MAKE FLOWER.
TELL FLOWER "RIGHT 90 FORWARD 100 * RANDOM LEFT 90"
REPEAT 15, WAIT 2 TELL FLOWER "GROW 1"
MAKE SEED.
WAIT 100. UNMAKE FLOWER.

The Actor promptor dialog may look like this:

user: define seed
SYSTEM: WHAT SHOULD SEED DO?
user: first wait 10
SYSTEM: AND THEN?
user: make flower
SYSTEM: WHERE SHOULD IT BE?
user: right 90 forward 100 * random left 90
SYSTEM: IS THERE MORE?
user: yes, repeat
[fi];"yes" is a noise word that is ignored here by the system[fr]
SYSTEM: WHAT SHOULD BE REPEATED?
user: wait 2, tell flower [grow 1]
SYSTEM: HOW MANY TIMES SHOULD THAT BE REPEATED?
user: 15
SYSTEM: ANY THING ELSE IN SEED'S DEFINITION?
user: yes make seed
[fi];since seed has no inputs no questions asked[fr]
SYSTEM: IS THERE MORE?
user: yes wait 100 then unmake flower
SYSTEM: WHAT FLOWER ARE YOU TALKING ABOUT?
[fi];I think the flower needs a name to be sent a "unmake" message[fr]
user: the one that seed just made?
[fi];a smart system would understand that but...[fr]
SYSTEM: LOOK I AM CONFUSED TO UNMAKE
	SOMETHING IT NEEDS A NAME
	DO YOU WANT TO NAME ANYTHING?
user: yes
SYSTEM: WHICH MAKE DO YOU WANT TO NAME?
user: the one that made flower
SYSTEM: WHAT DO YOU WANT TO CALL IT?
user: a-flower
SYSTEM: OK "A-FLOWER IS A FLOWER", SO DO YOU WANT
	SEED TO UNMAKE IT?
user: yes
SYSTEM: IS THAT IT?
user: yes
.end
Appendeum:
	After talking with Danny about this he has a reasonable
justification for not bothering to name the flower in the case
when there is no ambiguity, i.e. when there is only one flower
being mentioned.  This does seem rather natural, but is a little
kludgey, so needs more thought.
	Many things are rather arbitrary in the above example.
One is after accepting the notion that it is a bug to unmake
and make things without giving them names.  It is not clear how
the system should handle it.  Clearly it should find out where
the thing being unmade was made.  But should (as it does in this
example) go on ask the user for a name rather than generate
one on it's own and not bother the poor user?  Many many decisions
need to be made at this level.
.sec Implementation?
	While from the examples it is clear that the prompter
needs to be pretty smart, the knowledge is very modular, and
where it belongs is very obvious, that being the actor being
"called".  A running Plasma is expected in a few months and
this prompter front-end could probably be done in a matter
of a few more months.  However, this like Danny's is intended for
better machines, or at least something like the PDP10 for
the kids.
	Another idea as to how to make Actors more accessible
for kids (and big folks too) is to have an optional visual trace.
As the code is being run on a display one sees actors, and
messages moving around being activated.  One way to do this
would be a sort of single stepper that was smart about different
levels of events. So the user can either have the tracer consider
say "factorial" as a primitive or go down a level or two and
see how it does it's thing.  The way I visualize this trace
is very much like the event diagrams Carl draws when explaining
Actor semantics.
,F".require "ken;pubset >" source
.turn off ""
.every heading([fb]An Actor System for Kids[fr],,[fb]Kenneth Kahn[fr])
.every footing([ft]DRAFT[fr],,[fb]{date} {time}[fr])
.insert contents
.count page from 1 to 99
.portion body
.if page = null then next page
.begin double space
.count section printing "I"
.begin double space indent 5,0,0
.require "ken;$actor >" source
.end
.tabbreak
.next page
.
.comment portion declaration for contents;
.portion contents
.count page from 1 to 99
.turn on ""
.once center
[ft]TABLE OF CONTENTS   An Actor System for Kids[fr]
.skip
.begin preface 0
.receive
.next page
.end

"&.sec Introduction
	This paper describes a prototype animation system,
originally written in 11 Logo.  Due to the limitations of the
Logo workspace, the system was transferred over to Lisp Logo.
This paper describes the later implementation, though the 11Logo
version is very similar.
The system enables one to define
new objects, new object types, and the kinds of messages
they can handle.  For example, one can&z easily create a
square named "George".  George can be told many kinds of
things like his size, speed of movement, or speed of rotation.
George can be asked to do many things, all the things that
turtles can do
(FORWARD, BACK, RIGHT, LEFT, HIDE, SHOW, PENUP etc.)
plus new
messages like grow, or change appearance.  George can also
be taught many new things, or can be told to behave differently
than the defaults.  George also has a memory, you can tell
George anything at all, his color, his friends, whatever.
	One very important thing that George knows (though
like everything in the system he can be told otherwise) is that
he is a square.  Presently, "Squares" know a few things, like
how to draw themselves, or that after rotating 90 degrees
that they look the same.  Squares in turn know that they
are objects. Objects know how to do the turtle-like things
mentioned above.  Objects, in turn, know that they are
"actors", things that can take in messages, can pattern match
those messages, and can perform memory functions.
Actors know how to learn new responses to new patterns.
This entire hierarchy is very flexible and modifiable by
the user.  The basic process is the message is sent to
some individual, "George", and if George has no patterns
that match the message, he sends it off to the actor that
he is a "Kind.of".  They in turn pass the buck, until either
someone can handle the message, or an error message is generated
.
This is also a very useful default mechanism, if George is
never told his size he can inherit it from "Square", or "Object".
	Another feature of this system is a simple scheduler
which knows about time.  This mechanism
allows apparent parallelism of processes.  George can race
against Sally.
Danny's garden can be grown.  A stick figure can simultaneously
move different limbs and change it's facial expression.
Movies produced by the scheduler can be run at any speed the
system is capable of, can be run backward, or single stepped.
	In addition, to being a very useful system for doing
animation, in a way that does not radically depart from Logo
syntax, the system provides an interesting environment for
modeling knowledge and thinking about inheritance of attributes
and defaults.  One can view each object in the system as an
actor, much like Carl Hewitt's actors, that receive and pass
messages around.  There is much flexibility as to how
the knowledge is distributed.
.sec An Example
	In Danny Hillis' paper, "Ten Things to do with a
Better Computer", he has an example of growing a garden.
He describes a garden in which seeds wait, grow into flowers,
create new seeds, and die.
Danny's seed definition is:
.begin
.nofill
[fl]SEED DOES:
WAIT 10.
MAKE FLOWER.
TELL FLOWER "RIGHT 90 FORWARD 100 * RANDOM LEFT 90"
REPEAT 15, WAIT 2 TELL FLOWER "GROW 1"
MAKE SEED.
WAIT 100. UNMAKE FLOWER.
.end
	In my animation system, his garden can be implemented,
however, the code is lengthier and more explicit.  In my
system it would be written:
.begin nofill indent 0

[fl]TO DEFINE.FLOWER
[fi];Logo syntax has been kept since this is built on top of Logo[fl]

10 OBJ.DECLARE 'FLOWER 'DRAW.FLOWER
[fi];DECLARE does 3 things:
;	1) updates the "Kind.of" tree with flower as a kind of OBJ
;	2) creates an actor named "Flower".
;	3) tells that flower that if asked to how to draw a flower
;	   then use "Draw.flower" with the default convention of
;	   passing it the size of the particular flower.[fl]
20 FLOWER [REMEMBER SIZE 0]
.begin fill
[fi];This tells flower to remember that the default size of flowers is 0
;"Remember" is the standard kind of message for telling any actor something
; to remember[fl]
.end
END

TO DEFINE.SEED
10 DECLARE "SEED [KIND.OF ACTOR]
[fi]; Seed is not an Obj, since it does not do turtle-like things.[f]
20 SEED [IF RECEIVE HANDLE ?SEED [START] THEN DO.SEED.THING :?SEED]
[fi];This simply lets seeds take a "start" message and calls the appropriate procedure.[fl]
END

TO DO.SEED.THING :SEED
10 LOCAL 'FLOWER.NAME
[fi];a thing to hold the name for the flower that seed will spawn is needed[fl]
20 MAKE 'FLOWER.NAME FLOWER [MAKE]
.begin fill
[fi];After the buck is passed to "Actor",
this results in the call "MAKE.INSTANCE 'FLOWER
which will create an instantiation of flower with a generated name that is
put in the variable "Flower.name".
.end
30 tell :flower.name [right 90]
;Flowers are objects so can take any turtle command
40 TELL :FLOWER.NAME [FORWARD 100*RANDOM]
50 TELL :FLOWER.NAME [LEFT 90]
60 TELL :FLOWER.NAME [DO SHOW IN 10 FRAMES]
[fi];This results in the following call:
;   SCHEDULER [DO :FLOWER.NAME SHOW IN 10 FRAMES]
;which schedules the call,
;   TELL :FLOWER.NAME [SHOW],
;ten frames after line 60 is executed.[fl]
70 REPEAT 15 [TELL :FLOWER.NAME DO GROW 1 AFTER 2 MORE FRAMES]
[fi];This schedules the call,
;	TELL :FLOWER.NAME [GROW 1],
; 15 times, each time 2 frames after the last.[fl]
80 SEED [DO TELL (SEED [MAKE]) [START] AT THAT TIME]
.begin fill
[fi]; This schedules the call,
zUz	SEED [MAKE] followed by that seed the message "Start"
at the same time as the last thing scheduled in line 80.
"At that time" is equivalent to "after 0 more frames".[fl]
.end
100 TELL :FLOWER.NAME [DO UN.MAKE AFTER 100 MORE FRAMES]
[fi];This schedules the suicide of the flower, 100 frames later.[fl]
END
.skip
SCHEDULER [MAKE.MOVIE GARDEN 1000]
.begin fill
[fi];This makes a movie named "Garden" 1000 frames long.
The schedule is run, and all display and turtle commands
are stored in the movie "Garden".[fl]
.end
GARDEN [REMEMBER SPEED 10]
GARDEN [SHOW]
.begin fill
[fi];These two calls result in the movie being shown at 10 frames
per second. [fr]
.end
.end
.sec Message Passing
	Message passing is communication between two
actors.  The transmission of the message is done by the standard
Logo calling mechanism.  Every actor is a procedure that expects
one input ":message". The interpretation of messages in more
complicated.  A pattern matching procedure called "Match"
is used to decode the incoming message. This is essentially
the "parser" described in my paper called "A Logo Natural
Language System".
	Match allows the binding of variables, in the pattern,
to what is in the corresponding position in the message.
There are two important types, one a "?" type that binds
only to one item, and a "$" type that binds to whatever
it can. For example, the pattern that accepts the "if receive ... then ..." pattern is:

[HANDLE ?ACTOR [$ RECEIVE $PATTERN THEN $ACTION]],
.ONCE INDENT 0
and the pattern for receiving "FORWARD" messages is:

[[OR FORWARD FORWARD] ?AMT].
.ONCE INDENT 0

	The first pattern means: look for the word "Handle", make
the next word into ?actor, and then look the next element should be
list which means:
"forget anything before
the word "receive", then make "$pattern into whatever is
between the words "receive" and "then", then make "$action
into all that follows the word "then".  In the case that
it is impossible to match the message, it aborts, and another
pattern can be tried. The second pattern means, "the first
word had better be either "FORWARD" or "FORWARD" and followed
by only one more word".  The first pattern has the following action
associated with it:
.begin nofill
[fl] RECEIVE :$PATTERN :$ACTION :?ACTOR
.end once indent 0
where "Receive is a normal Logo function".
	As an example of the use of the first pattern consider
when "Obj" is sent the message,
.begin nofill
[fl]OBJ [IF RECEIVE HANDLE ?OBJ [[OR FORWARD FORWARD] ?AMT]
	     THEN MOVE.OBJ "FORWARD :?OBJ :?AMT],
.end once indent 0
[fn]which will be sent up the tree as HANDLE OBJ [...], and
results in the call,
.begin nofill
[fl]RECEIVE [HANDLE ?OBJ [[OR FORWARD FORWARD] ?AMT]
	     [MOVE.OBJ "FORWARD :?OBJ :?AMT]
	     "OBJ
.end once indent 0
[fn]which in turn results in the following line being added to the definition of "Obj":
.begin nofill
[fl]IF TOLD [HANDLE ?OBJ [[OR FORWARD FORWARD] ?AMT]
	    MOVE.OBJ "FORWARD :?OBJ :?AMT
.end once indent 0
Now, if "Obj" gets the message "[HANDLE FOO [FORWARD 100]]", it
will call, MOVE.OBJ "FORWARD "FOO 100.
	Initially, these new statements were just tacked on to the
end of the actor being told how to receive something. (Actually
it was not really the end, the very end being reserved for the
call to the procedure that passes the buck up the kind.of tree.)
This resulted in many unnecessary calls to match, and so
the procedure, "Receive" was smartened up.  Based on the first
word in the message (not counting "handle"), each actor calls
the appropriate sub-function handling messages that begin with
that word.
.sec The Scheduler and Movies
	Using the system, one can write procedures that move
one object, then another and back again.  It will not, however,
look as if they are moving simultaneously
since the interpretation of the commands is slow.  The solution
is to have a scheduler run things at the appropriate time,
and then save away the display commands to be run later.
These saved-away commands, called a "movie", can be
run later
with the appearance of parallelism.
	When running, the scheduler has an integer
that increments, by one, each time through its loop. This
integer can be considered to be time or the frame number of the
movie.  When told to schedule a call at a time, it associates
with that time the call. When the scheduler runs, it runs
what should be run at that time, inserts a "new frame" marker
into the movie, and increments the clock and loops.  If nothing
more is scheduled at all it stops.  Movies can be made or just
the code run, depending
on the message to the scheduler.
.sec How does one talk about this?
	To teach how to use the animation system one should
have a consistent vocabulary and set of concepts, just as is
often said about teaching Logo.  Are the words "actor" and
"object" reasonable names for such general entities?  I think
not, but haven't thought up better ones. How should one talk
about the difference between "Square", the general square,
and any insz)tantiation of squares?  Will there often be a
confusion between the actor "George", his "script", and
his image on the screen?  Identity becomes very strange
to talk about, since anything about an actor can be changed,
what it looks like, how it acts, even what it is a kind of.
Only its name is permanent.
	Explaining the scheduler might bring up problems.
How should one talk about, or think about, many separate
processes going on in parallel?  I don't really know.
	This system lends itself very well to the "little man"
vocabulary.  Each actor is a little person,
who knows a few things,
can be told to do a few things, and can be taught new tricks.
This little person receives messages and sends them to others.
.sec Hassles or Logo is too small for this
	This animation system may have the rather strange honor
of being the largest program ever to "run" on Logo.  Possibly
the slowest, too.  The problem is that the system needs
at least 150 percent of the workspace just to run with one actor
instantiated.
Fortunately much of the code can be (and was) segmented.  The
code for rotating objects, for example,
need not be around when an actor is
being told to remember something.  The "solution" is to have much
of the code look like this:
.begin nofill
[fl]TO MOVE.OBJ :COMMAND :OBJ :AMOUNT
10 RUN
    LOAD "MOVE
	     [MOVE.OBJ1 :COMMAND :OBJ :AMOUNT]
	     [MOVE.OBJ1 FORWARD.OBJ]
END
.end ONCE INDENT 0
This means, if match is called then, run what load returns.
Load reads in the file "MOVE which contains, "MOVE.OBJ1",
the "real" definition of move.obj and "FORWARD.OBJ" a helping
function.  Then the "real" MOVE.OBJ is called and then
removed from the workspace.
	This works, but is too slow to be practical.  So
until Logo workspaces grow, only a parts of the system can be used
on the PDP11. These problem do not exist with the Lisp-Logo version
which realy runs.
.sec Extensions and Improvements
	One useful extension would be the addition of primitives
for joining and breaking apart "Object" actors.  For example,
one may want to join a triangle actor and a square actor to make
a house actor.  Or one may want to have a face accept messages
as well as any of it parts.  A person may get into a car, so that
temporarily any movement of the car should also change the state of
the person.
	Movies could be extended to including editting commands
for "splicing" film together.  Also a "camera" actor could be
useful for "zooms," "pans," and the like.
	Perhaps, in a few months, when Carl Hewitt's Actor
language PLASMA is in an advanced enough stage, this system
could be re-written correctly in it.  The semantics would be uniform,
there would be no such thing as Actor code and Logo code.
The syntax would have to be changed to make it accessible to
kids.  This is discussed in my paper, "An Actor System for Kids".
	Comments welcome!
.end

)".require "ken;pubset >" source
.turn off ""
.every heading([fb]An Actor-like Animation System in Logo[fr],,[fb]Kenneth Kahn[fr])
.every footing([ft]DRAFT[fr],,[fb][date] [time][fr])
.insert contents
.count page from 1 to 99
.portion body
.if page = null then next page
.begin double space
.count section printing "I"
.begin double space indent 5,0,0
.require "ken;$anima >" source
.end
.tabbreak
.next page
.
.comment portion declaration for contents;
.portion contents
.count page from 1 to 99
.turn on ""
.once center
[fb]TABLE OF CONTENTS   An Actor-like Animation System in Logo[fr]
.skip
.begin preface 0
.receive
.next page
.end

"N.sec Why is something else needed?
	Presently in LOGO there are only two
convenient mechanisms for putting knowledge into one's
program. Teaching (TO) and variables (MAKE)
are means of procedural and declarative imbedding
of knowledge, respectively.  This paper will attempt
to show that variables are inadequate, inflexible, or inconvenient
in many cases that a declarative representation seems
reasonable. An alternative that hopefully is not more
magical than variables is described in some detail.
	As an example only, let us consider some
problems in writing programs that play cards.  One very
important decision is how to represent the deck.  One
idea would be have an pointer array (or a list) of 52 elements.
Each element could be a sentence that contained the
owner, the suit, and the rank. To shuffle one would need to
randomly reorder the array (or list). To deal one would
replace some of the elements whose owner is the "deck" by
alternate players.  If in playing the game one wanted to
know things like who owns a particular card, or how many
spades a player has, for example, then one must go thru the
array (or list) checking each one and accumulating information.
While I will show an easier way of doing this the major
drawback of using arrays or lists is their inflexibility.
	What if one wanted to add some new characteristics
to a card? For example, in fish you might want to remember
that your opponent fished for a Jack a few turnNz s ago. One
might want two versions of the deck one representing "reality"
and the other "the computer player's point of view". Clearly
each change would require many changes and complications: an
unfortunate state of affairs.
.sec An Alternative Borrowed from AI
	One of the important innovations in
AI languages
such as Planner, QA4, and Conniver is a relational data base.
The idea is that much of the hair of finding the
right variable, of searching through lists (or arrays) is handled
by primitives provided by the language. Following example of playing
cards,
one could represent the deck as a series of "sentences"
to be remembered by the system. If one were to give the
command
.begin center skip
[fl] "REMEMBER [OWNER [JACK SPADES] PLAYER1]"
.end once indent 0
then the system would remember that in such a way that it could be recalled
in many different ways. For example, to get all the spades that
player2 owns one would give the command
.begin center skip
[fl]"FETCH [OWNER [? SPADES] PLAYER2]"
.end once indent 0
and the list of all sentences that fit the pattern would be
returned. To find out who owns all the Queens one would say:
.begin center
[fl]"FETCH [OWNER [QUEENS ?] ?].
.end once indent 0
Question marks are interpreted as anything can be here.
One should be able to see that such a system is very flexible.
	For example the program to deal cards could be written:
.BEGIN
.NOFILL
[fl]TO DEAL :NUMBER.OF.CARDS :PLAYERS
10 REPEAT [FOR.EACH :PLAYERS "DEAL.A.CARD] :NUMBER.OF.CARDS
END

TO DEAL.A.CARD :PLAYER
10 LOCAL "CARD
20 MAKE "CARD FIND [OWNER ? DECK] [fi] !This finds any one of the
				   ! cards in the deck.[fl]
30 FORGET :CARD 		  [fi] ! Take it out of the deck[fl]
40 REMEMBER (LIST "OWNER SECOND :CARD :PLAYER) 	[fi]!so :PLAYER owns it[fl]
END
.END
	FOR.EACH applies the function to each of the
list it is passed and REPEAT simply repeats its first argument
the second argument times.  SECOND is just a first of a butfirst,
in other words, the second item of a list.
.sec How does this all work?
	At the present, I have a rather complete version of such a
data base system implemented in 11LOGO.  There were many design decisions
to make, the hardest being how to make it run fast enough without
using up the entire workspace. The basic idea is really very
simple; what one should do is have "Remember" leave something
behind so that when fetch is called it has to do as little
searching as possible.  The first version of this system took
this idea to the extreme. When a sentence like "[A B C]" was
remember all possible combinations of question marks replacing
elements in the sentence were generated. These sentences
were then turned into words whose thing is all sentences that
are answers to that fetch. In this example,
.BEGIN skip
.NOFILL
[fl]PRINTOUT NAMES
:[A ? ?] IS [...[A B C] ...]
:[A B ?] IS [...[A B C] ...]
:[A ? C] IS [...[A B C] ...]
:[? ? C] IS [...[A B C] ...]
:[? B C] IS [...[A B C] ...]
:[? B ?] IS [...[A B C] ...]
:[A B C] IS [[A B C]]
:[? ? ?] IS [......[A B C] .....]
.END
	Notice that "Fetch" has to do in this case is
simply to take the argument (the question) and turn
it into a word and then output its thing. "Forget"
is just like remember except rather than adding to the
thing of the question it takes away. So under
this scheme we have very fast fetches but remembers
are slow and take up very much memory.
	Since so much memory was used
a compromise was devised. Only those
"questions" that consisted of only one non-question
mark item are "pre-answered".  In this case, only three sentences: [A ? ?] , [? B ?],
and [? ? C]. But what happens when the question asked is
"[A ? C]," for example? The solution is simple just
take the intersection of those answers to "[A ? ?]"
and "[? ? C]. This means some fetches are going to
be slower but that much memory is saved. (In the
previous case the number of nodes consumed was
proportional to 2 to the n, where n is the count of
the sentence. In this new scheme, it is only linear
with n.)
	However, what if the pattern "[A ? C]" is the
something that will be needed often and we are not
too short for workspace? It seems a shame to run slow
to save memory that might not be needed. To alleviate
this problem one can be in what I call "savemode". When
in this mode, "Fetch" after it answers a question like
"[A ? C]," will create the word "[A ? C]" and make it
into the answer that it returns.  Then on subsequent calls
to fetch it will have the answer sitting there.  Of course,
"Remember" has to be smart and update that pattern from then
on.  This way common questions are answered fast but rare
ones don't need to waste memory.
	What I said so far about "[A B C]" is accurate but,
what about the example above:
"FETCH [OWNER [QUEEN ?] ?]" ? It has
a list within a list.  To fetch a pattern like this one has
to fetch the pattern "[QUEEN ?]" first and then make
a fetch for each sentence returned by that as the second
item in the pattern "[OWNER <each returned by the firstzkz$ fetch> ?]".  When the structure of the sentence gets complicated
this can be quite slow so here especially "savemode" really
helps.  An observant reader may wonder how it is possible to
fetch "[QUEEN ?]" since it is not remembered by itself.  Well
it is. Remember recurses for every substructure.  But one may
wonder about the problem when one does:
.BEGIN skip center
[fl]REMEMBER [NOT [PLAYED [QUEEN SPADES]]]
.end once indent 0
and later
.begin skip center
[fl]FETCH [PLAYER ?]
.END once indent 0
What is probably intended would not be
what happens.  The solution
is a little crockish. The name of the word corresponding to the questions
differs slightly depending on whether the pattern is
from the top-level sentence or not.  The slight difference chosen
is a space before the word.
This means that [fi]only[fr] if you know what you are doing can you fetch
and have returned parts of sentences previously remembered.
.sec A lousy problem and a crockish solution
	Things seemed fine at one point and the three
major functions remember, fetch and forget worked perfectly.
Then I saved away the workspace including things that were
remembered and later when I read it in again all seemed to
work fine except my workspace had mysteriously been almost
filled.  The reason is that the things of all the pattern
words are lists of lists. And many of those sub lists are
the same between pattern words.  When created they are the
same identical lists (EQ in LISP) but when written and re-read
that is no longer the case (they are only EQUAL).
The crockish solution
was that instead of using lists I use the corresponding word
and fetch has to re-listify it.  Such is life.
.sec Extensions to the system
	One feature present in the AI languages is the
ability to put, in addition to the question marks, a word one
wants "made" to whatever is there in that position.  For example,
.begin skip center
[fl]FETCH [OWNER [QUEEN ?SUIT] PLAYER1]
.end once indent 0
would in addition to returning all the sentences that indicated
that player1 was the owner of queens of any suit, the name "?suit
would be a list of the suits that player1 has. This is clearer
than doing a first butfirst first butfirst of each sentence that
fetch returns and making a list of them.  This was tried but
it keep causing funny system bug errors so is pushed for now.
	One problem with the present implementation is that it
is so positionally oriented.  To do a fetch one must know what
position in the sentence words are.  So to fetch all sentences
that begin with the word "S, for example, one needs to do fetches
with the patterns "[S ?]," "[S ? ?]," "[S ? ? ?]" and
on up to the longest length sentence that could begin with an "S.
One very useful addition to kind of elements that can appear in
the pattern would be something that would match any number of
elements.  Using "*" as a symbol for that pattern element (it
clearly needs a good name) the above problem would be solved by
doing a fetch on the pattern, "[S *]".  Notice that this would
also pick up the unusual item "[S]" in the data base.  If one
wanted to exclude that possibility one could fetch on the
pattern "[S ? *]".
	This would clearly be a very useful
addition to the system but how could it be implement considering
the way things are stored? The answer lies in the chunking
mechanism discussed in the next section.  Basically a list of
all sentences in a "chunk" is available and one need only
butfirst down this list using a special match function.
(Incidentily this match function is quite useful in its own right.)  The price however that one must pay or
power, in this case, is time --- it will run slowly.
	In addition to "Fetch" I have written "Find" which
does a fetch then picks any one of list returned randomly. This
is very useful for say dealing cards.  It is also essential in
a simple sentence generator I wrote using this system.  But
at least one more variant of "Fetch" may be useful.  That is
something like "Try-next" in Conniver. The idea is that it is
like "Find" except it will never repeat until all of the
elements of the list have been output by previous "Try-nexts"
(instead of repeating it could return the empty list). A simple
way of implementing this would be to take advantage of "savemode".
The first call to "Try-next" would make sure that the pattern
is "saved". Then each call could return the first of the list
and then take that first element remove it and place it at the
end of the list.
	Another idea in Conniver and other AI languages is
methods that are triggered by the pattern of the sentence being
remembered, fetched, or forgotten. For example, if one was
using this system to model someone's family and we had remembered
[FATHER-OF TOM JOHN] and
[FATHER-OF SALLY JOHN].
And we wanted to "Fetch" [SIBLING-OF SALLY ?].
Then it would be nice if we could somehow say that if two people have the same father then consider them siblings. One way
would be to tell fetch about like so:
.BEGIN skip
.NOFILL indent 0
[fl]REMz/
EMBER [IF-ASKED [SIBLING ?A ?B] CHECK.IF.SAME.FATHER :?A :?B][fr]

where CHECK.IF.SAME.FATHER MIGHT be defined as:

[fl]TO CHECK.IF.SAME.FATHER :PERSON1 :PERSON2
10 LOCAL "?FATHER1 LOCAL "?FATHER2
20 IF BOTH FETCH  (SENTENCE "FATHER-OF :PERSON1 "?FATHER1)
           FETCH  (SENTENCE "FATHER-OF :PERSON2 "?FATHER2)
      THEN IF :?FATHER1 = :?FATHER2
	      THEN OUTPUT (SENTENCE "SIBLING-OF :PERSON1 :PERSON2)
30 OUTPUT []
END
.END
	Alternatively we could have done something like this
when the facts were remembered and call a similar function
to "Check.if.same.father" and "Remember" the sibling fact.  This
may be getting too complicated for elementary school kids,
we shall see, but for high school kids it doesn't seem all that
difficult to understand.
	One minor but useful extension to this would be the
ability to indicate that the following sub-sentence need not
be broken down, that no fetching will occur inside this sub-sentence.
For example, if one were using a context-free
grammar for generating only, then one would like to know what
a constituent can become but not what constituent starts with
say, a noun phrase.  One could say,
 REMEMBER [S CAN.BECOME "[NP VP]]
where the quote means don't break me down.  This can save
quite alot of scarce workspace.  One way to think of the
quote is that it says think of the following sentence as a
word (atomic).
.sec One more "improvement"
	Another idea current in AI is that chunking of
knowledge is important.  Yet as described this system
has one uniform data base without much structure.  One could
put structure into it by prefacing all sentences with a "context"
marker. Then only those sentences having to do with one point
of view or purpose are returned by fetch, not all that it has
been told about.
	A variation of the above idea that I favor is actually
only having those "chunks" that are relavent in the workspace
and the others living in files on disk.  If one does not switch
"chunks" too often this should run at reasonable speed.  A
big plus with this scheme is that one can put quite alot of
knowledge into a program and not be workspace limited.
.sec How do you talk about such things with kids
	One may be reading this thinking, "this stuff sounds
good for AI graduate students, but how are 5th graders going to
understand this stuff?".  I have a little experience trying it
and a fair number of ideas on this. The basic system which consists of
remember, find, fetch, and forget are rather intuitive.  Perhaps
if presented well they may be clearer than "make" and "thing".
One game that I played with a 5th grader to get her used to
the system was that we "Remembered" a few sentences about how
we and some people we knew in common looked. Then we would call
"Find" with the person position being a question mark.
We wondered who it would talk about next.  Then we could have
"remembered" some other attribute of the people such as their
size or clothes.  Then we could do four different things with
"Find". The previous thing still worked, of course, we could
also do Finds by this new attribute, or pick a person and see
what about that person would pop up, or do a Find on all it
knows.
	Another way to start is with a deck of cards
initiallized and then show how we can fetch cards by
owner, rank , suit or combinations of these.  Then dealing,
then....
	The usual Logo problem of vocabulary pops up here.
What should we call the sentences that are remembered or
forgotten? What about the patterns that are "Fetched"?
Are "remember," "find," "fetch," and "forget" good names?
If this system were extended as described above what do
you call if-asked, if-remembered, if-forgotten methods?
What word should be used for "chunks"?
.sec Other possible uses
	I don't really feel I need to write this section.
There are just so many things one could do.  Originally
I started this for an animation system in which the objects
in addition to being snaps had attributes like location, speed,
size, ... I have used the system described herein in a context-free sentence
generator that I will describe in another paper.  This last
example is interesting because for just doing context-free
generation this system is gross overkill.  But using this
system it becomes easier to later modify the system, for example, to do
context-sensitive generation.
The same grammar could be used by a simple parser, which would
not be the case if a simplier representation of the rules was
used.
	The idea of having children doing simple AI programs
seems quite a good one. But in order to model knowledge one
needs the proper primitives. These primitives are not part of
Logo but when presented to the child as primitives, new and
exciting things can be tried.

/
 j(.require "ken;pubset >" source
.turn off ""
.every heading([fb]A LOGO Data Base System[fr],,[fb]Kenneth Kahn[fr])
.every footing(DRAFT,{page},{date})
.insert contents
.count page from 1 to 99
.portion body
.if page = null then next page
.count section printing j!"I"
.begin double space indent 5,0,0
.require "ken;$ldb >" source
.end
.tabbreak
.next page
.
.comment portion declaration for contents;
.portion contents
.count page from 1 to 99
.turn on ""
.once center
[ft]TABLE OF CONTENTS   LOGO Data Base System[fr]
.skip
.begin preface 0
.receive
.next page
.end

!'dp,.sec Actors
	Recent trends in AI language development are tending towards languages
based on "actor" semantics.  Carl Hewitt's PLASMA foot< Hewitt, C...> and
Alan Kay's SmallTalk foot<Kay, ...>
are the best examples.  The basic idea is to consider each
entity within the system as something that is usually anthropomorphized as a
"little person."  Each "actor" can receive messages asking it to
do something, remember something, recall something, or send some messages to other
actors.  For animation this seems an ideal way to view objects on the screen.  Each
object is a process that can be arbitrarily smart.  Charlie Brown can be an "actor"
that can be told to walk, causing him to send the appropriate messages to his arms
and legs and moving the rest of his body.
	A system has been implemented in Lisp-Logo which
enables one to define
new objects, new object types, and the kinds of messages
they can handle.  For example, one can easily create a
square named "George."  George can be told many kinds of
things like his size, speed of movement, or speed of rotation.
George can be asked to do many things, all the things that
turtles can do
(FORWARD, BACK, RIGHT, LEFT, HIDE, SHOW, PENUP etc.)
plus new
messages like grow, or change appearance.  George can also
be taught many new things, or can be told to behave differently
than the defaults.  George also has a memory, you can tell
George anything at all, his color, his friends, whatever.
	One very important thing that George knows (though
like everything in the system he can be told otherwise) is that
he is a square.  Presently, "Squares" know a few things, like
how to draw themselves, or that after rotating 90 degrees
that they look the same.  Squares in turn know that they
are objects. Objects know how to do the turtle-like things
mentioned above.  Objects, in turn, know that they are
"actors", things that can take in messages, can pattern match
those messages, and can perform memory functions.
Actors know how to learn new responses to new patterns.
This entire hierarchy is very flexible and modifiable by
the user.  The basic process is the message is sent to
some individual, "George", and if George has no patterns
that match the message, he sends it off to the actor that
he is a kind of.  They in turn pass the buck, until either
someone can handle the message, or an error message is generated
.
This is also a very useful default mechanism, if George is
never told his size he can inherit it from "Square", or "Object".
	Another feature of this system is a simple scheduler
which deals with time.  This mechanism
allows apparent parallelism of processes.  George can race
against Sally.
Danny's garden of flowers can be grown.  A stick figure can simultaneously
move different limbs and change it's facial expression.
Movies produced by the scheduler can be run at any speed the computer
system is capable of, can be run backward, or single stepped.
	This system is intended to support the intelligent computer animator
discussed above and equally important to be used by children.  The system is
hopefully a more powerful and natural means for doing simple programs for animation.
The powerful ideas of instances, classes and the finding of the correct level of
generality and the little person model of computation are all imbedded into the system.
The hope is that through well-guided use of the system some of these ideas will become
more concrete to the children.  Of course,
all the usual reasons for teaching Logo to children remain in force since the actor-animation
is built upon Logo.
The use of the same actor-animation
system by children and by the computer animator is very important for making the
computer animator more accessible and understandable by the children using it.  The
idea is that if the children who programmed using the actor-animation system found it
natural and intuitive then its use in the intelligent system would also be clear.
	This view of programming as collections of actors, or
a community of "little people", that send and receive messages
from each other is very powerful.  It is conducive to a modular,
simple, natural representation of the knowledge needed for the
application.  With an actor system a model for intelligence can be
a community as easily as it can be an individual.
	Another AI aspect of this system is the explicit
"kind-of" hierarchy of actors.  Each object is told what class
it is a member of when it is created.  When any object receives
a message it cannot handle it passes the problem on to the class
of which it is a member.
The important concepts of instantiation, class
membership, placement of knowledge at the best levelpz0 of
generality, inheritance of properties, and exceptions hopefully
flow from the proper use of this aspect of the system.
.subsec A Simple Example of Its Use
.begin indent 0 turn off "[]" choose lisp
SQUARE [MAKE GEORGE]

[fi];This sends the message "make George" to square which creates
a square named George[fr]

GEORGE [REMEMBER SPEED 25]

[fi];This tells George his speed of movement[fr]

GEORGE [IF RECEIVE BLINK ?N THEN REPEAT :?N [GEORGE [HIDE] GEORGE [SHOW]]

.begin single space choose italic
George is told that if he receives the message "BLINK" followed
by some amount, then send the messages to hide and show, the
amount times.
.end


CIRCLE [MAKE SALLY]

[fi];A circle named "Sally" is created[fr]

SALLY [DO FORWARD 100 AT FRAME 2]

.begin single space choose italic
Sally is told to go forward 100 at movie frame number 2.  This
problem is passed on to the scheduler.
.end

GEORGE [DO BLINK 75 AT FRAME 2]

[fi];George is told to blink at frame 2[fr]

SCHEDULER [GO]

.begin single space choose italic
The scheduler is told to go.  After 2 frames the appropriate
messages are sent to Sally and George a cartoon is produced and
displayed on the computer display.
.skip 8
.end
.subsec An More Complex Example of its Use
	In a paper by Danny Hillis called, "Ten Things to do with a
Better Computer", he has an example of how to grow a garden in an actor-like
system.
He describes a garden in which seeds are born, wait, grow into flowers,
create new seeds, and die.
	In my animation system, his garden can be implemented in a fairly
straight-forward manner.
.begin nofill indent 0

[fl]TO DEFINE.FLOWER
[fi];Logo syntax has been kept since this is built on top of Logo[fl]

10 OBJ.DECLARE 'FLOWER 'DRAW.FLOWER
[fi];DECLARE does 3 things:
;	1) updates the "Kind.of" tree with flower as a kind of OBJ
;	2) creates an actor named "Flower".
