;	3) tells that flower that if asked to how to draw a flower
;	   then use "Draw.flower" with the default convention of
;	   passing it the size of the particular flower.[fl]
20 FLOWER [REMEMBER SIZE 10]
.begin fill
[fi];This tells flower to remember that the default size of flowers is 10
"Remember" is the standard kind of message for telling any actor
to remember something[fl]
.end
END

TO DEFINE.SEED
10 DECLARE "SEED [KIND.OF ACTOR]
[fi]; Seed is not an Obj, since it does not do turtle-like things.[fr]
20 SEED [IF RECEIVE ?SEED [START] THEN DO.SEED.THING :?SEED]
[fi];This simply lets seeds take a "start" message and calls the appropriate procedure.[fl]
END

TO DO.SEED.THING :SEED
10 LOCAL A.FLOWER
[fi];a thing to hold the name for the flower that seed will spawn is needed[fl]
20 MAKE 'A.FLOWER FLOWER [MAKE]
.begin fill preface 0 single space
[fi];After the message is forwarded to "Actor",
the call "MAKE.INSTANCE 'FLOWER" occurs,
which creates an instantiation of flower with a generated name that is
put in the variable "A.FLOWER".
.end
30 TELL :A.FLOWER [RIGHT 90]
;Flowers are "objects" and so can take any turtle command
40 TELL :A.FLOWER [FORWARD 100*RANDOM]
50 TELL :A.FLOWER [LEFT 90]
60 TELL :A.FLOWER [DO [SHOW] IN 10 FRAMES]
[fi];This results in the following call:
;   SCHEDULER [DO TELL :A.FLOWER [SHOW] IN 10 FRAMES]
;which schedules the call,
;   TELL :A.FLOWER [SHOW],
;ten frames after line 60 is executed.[fl]
70 REPEAT 15 [TELL :A.FLOWER DO [GROW 10] AFTER 2 MORE FRAMES]
[fi];This schedules the call,
;	TELL :A.FLOWER [GROW 10],
; 15 times, each time 2 frames after the last.[fl]
80 SEED [DO TELL (SEED [MAKE]) [START] AT THAT TIME]
.begin fill preface 0 single space
[fi]; This means at that time create another seed and tell it to start
at the same time as the last thing scheduled in line 80.
.end
90 TELL :A.FLOWER [DO [HIDE] AFTER 60 MORE FRAMES]
100 TELL :A.FLOWER [DO [UN.MAKE] AFTER 60 MORE FRAMES]
[fi];This schedules the suicide of the flower, 60 frames later.[fl]
END
.skip
TELL SEED [MAKE] [START]
;This starts the first seed off which will start the others off later
SCHEDULER [MAKE.MOVIE GARDEN 1000]
.begin fill preface 0 single space
The schedule is run, and all display and turtle commands
are stored in the movie "Garden".[fl]
.end
GARDEN [REMEMBER SPEED 10]
GARDEN [SHOW]
.begin fill
[fi];These two calls result in the movie being shown at 10 frames
per second. First, a flower appears and grows and and then another starts to grow and
after a while the first flower disappears and so on.[fn]
.end
.end end
.subsec Message Passing
	Message passing is mechanism of communication between two
actors.  A pattern matching procedure called "Match"
is used to decode the incoming message. This provides great flexibility in the
syntax of the messages sent and received, allowing English-like commands.
	The following is a list of some of the messages that any object in the system can
receive:
.begin nofill single space NARROW 5,5
(1) The "turtle" commands, i.e. "FORWARD," "BACK," "RIGHT," "LEFT," "HIDE," "SHOW," "PENUP,"
"PENDOWN,"

(2) The memory messages, "REñþ(E*`MEMBER," "TELL ME," "REPLACE," and "FORGET" which provide
a powerful relational memory to each actor.  These are also used to inspect the
state, speed, size and the like of an object and to update those quantities.

(3) "MAKE" and "UNMAKE" messages which create instances or destroy them respectively

(4) The "RECEIVE" message which enables one to add the ability to receive new kinds
of messages.

.subsec The Scheduler and Movies
	Using the system, one can write procedures that move
one object, then another and back again.  It will not, however,
look as if they are moving simultaneously
since the interpretation of the commands is slow.  The solution
is to have a scheduler run things at the appropriate time,
and then save away the display commands to be run later.
These saved-away commands, called a "movie", can be
run later
with the appearance of parallelism.
	When running, the scheduler has an integer
that increments, by one, each time through its loop. This
integer can be considered to be time or the frame number of the
movie.  When told to schedule a call at a time, it associates
with that time the call.  So each time contains a list of events
or messages to be passed at that time.  As earlier events occur
these later times may be told to add new events to their "to be
done" list.
When the scheduler runs, it runs
what should be run at that time, inserts a "new frame" marker
into the movie, and increments the clock and loops.  If nothing
more is scheduled at all it stops.  Movies can be made or just
the code run, depending
on the message to the scheduler.
The scheduler is the simplest kind possible.  Extensions so that
events can be scheduled not on the basis of the frame number but
in terms of other events are planned.  For example, go would be able
to say that someone should scream if someone else comes too close.
.subsec How does one talk about this?
	In teaching children to use the animation system one should
have a consistent vocabulary and set of concepts.
Are the words "actor" and
"object" reasonable names for such general entities?  I think
not, but haven't thought up better ones. How should one talk
about the difference between "Square", the general square,
and any instantiation of squares?  Will there often be a
confusion between the actor "George", his "script", and
his image on the screen?  Identity becomes very strange
to talk about, since anything about an actor can be changed,
what it looks like, how it acts, even what it is a kind of.
Only its name is permanent.
	Explaining the scheduler might bring up problems.
How should one talk about, or think about, many separate
processes going on in parallel?  I don't really know.
	This system lends itself very well to the "little man"
vocabulary.  Each actor is a little person,
who knows a few things,
can be told to do a few things, and can be taught new tricks.
This little person receives messages and sends them to others.
.subsec Extensions and Improvements Planned
	One useful extension would be the addition of primitives
for joining and breaking apart "Object" actors.  For example,
one may want to join a triangle actor and a square actor to make
a house actor.  Or one may want to have a face accept messages
as well as any of it parts.  A person may get into a car, so that
temporarily any movement of the car should also change the state of
the person.
*`,4.sec The Problem
	Computer animation is very limited compared with conventional animation.
At the present, it is well adapted only to producing abstract, as opposed to
representational, animation.  The few exceptions are not flexible enough to be of
general usefulness.foot<Refer to Utah system and sketch kind like Ron Baecker's>
  On the other hand, conventional animation is very time
consuming and requires expensive equipment.  As such it is available as a medium
to very few adults and even fewer children.  In both types of animation one must learn
many skills to transform a story board into a finished cartoon.
	The research I will propose attempts to alleviate this problem by
equiping a computer with many of the skills necessary for
going from an idea
to a finished cartoon.  This attempt diverges from the usual computer animation
and graphics research by facing the problem of putting the required knowledge into
a computer.  In order for the computer to be a useful apprentice in this endeavor
it must know much about animation and cartooning techniques such as in-betweening,
easing in and out, anticipation, exaggeration, flow of motion, composition,
and perspective.
It must also know many things about the real world; that if a person is described
as happy then probably that person is smiling, that if someone says "hi" there is
good chance that person raised his or her hand in greeting, that people walk by moving
their arms and legs in a particular pattern.  The computer needs to able to accept
statements about a character and take those facts into account while constructing
the animated scenes.  All of tz8hese facts about the world, the characters, and
the necessary animation techniques
must interact with the intended story in such a way that
a reasonable approximation of the user's idea is converted into a cartoon.
	Clearly, this is a huge problem. Therefore we are involved in exploring
only a limited portion of this problem.  The hope is that, if successful, a
more complete system could be considered.  The major limitations envisioned are:
.begin narrow 5,5 single space
.skip
(1) The only characters the system will know about are a few of those in Schultz's
Peanuts World,

(2) The computer will understand only simple activities.  For example, it will
understand walking, running, talking, turning and the like, but will [fi]not[fr]
understand more complex or unusual activities such as fighting, skipping rope, and
mountain climbing.

(3) The drawings will be simple line drawings,

(4) The English that the user uses to communicate with the system will need to
be simple and stylized.
.end
.sec Motivation for Creating such a System
	The reasons for building such a system are many and varied.
.begin narrow 5,5
(1) Artificial Intelligence (AI):  This research is being carried out at the MIT Artificial
Intelligence Laboratory where the problems of instilling intelligence in computers
and of understanding natural intelligence are being explored.  The animation system
should be a good domain within which to work on problems of representing real-world knowledge,
common sense, and interactions of many different sources of knowledge, such as
animation techniques, cartooning techniques, the Peanuts characters and their world,
human emotions and how they influence people's appearance, and the story
presented by the user.

(2) Educational: The system could provide a different and stimulating environment
for children to learn to express themselves well, to learn,
about animation and film in the role of a director,
to learn how to criticize their own work,
in order for it to be improved, and to get experience working on relatively long-term
projects.  These activities fit well with the activities of the
LOGO Laboratory,foot<This research is
being sponsored by the LOGO Lab.  The original idea of this project came from Prof. Ira
Goldstein of the Lab and was initially explored by a seminar led by him.>
 a part
of the Artificial Intelligence Laboratory,
which among other things attempts to involve children in learning by
programming within a specially designed computer language on well-chosen problems.
The teaching of programming to children and this intelligent animation project
can be more directly linked by encouraging the children to
modify and add new things to the animation system, in addition to simply telling
stories and criticizing resulting cartoons.  If the system is designed and
implemented properly then there is a good chance that the children will be able
to learn enough about how the system works to consider it a "white box."  That is
they might understand how the system "thinks", thereby providing them with an example to
help them in being more explicit about their own thinking.

(3) Computer Graphics and Animation: To our knowledge, this will be the first time
that a computer animation system knew what it doing, in addition, to doing it.
This knowledge enables the system to make reasonable guesses, rather than require
the user to explicitly describe each detail.  For example, the system will, if
not told otherwise, make assumptions as to whether a particular character is
smiling, where to place the characters and background, how fast to move things
and the like.  In addition, the system is to be built upon primitives that are very
well adapted to animation.  They are based
on the "actor" semantics of computation of Carl Hewitt foot<Hewitt, C. and Smith B.
Towards a Programming Apprentice ..> and Alan Kay foot <Kay, A. ...>
The relation of this research to advance programming language technology
is discussed in the appendix.

(4) Entertainment:  This may be the first time that animation would become an
[fi]active[fr] event in people's activities, as opposed to the usual passive viewing of
other's work.
.end
.sec The basic idea
	The system will go through the following steps in its normal mode of operation:
.begin narrow 5,5 single space
(1) translate the user's story or script into an internal representation;

(2) for each episode, generate symbolic descriptions of successive key-frames;foot<By the term, "key-frame", I mean "film" frames
that are interpolated between.  This is not to be confused with
Minksy frames which are closer to what I call "episodes" later.>

(3) find differences between the descriptions
of the key-frames thereby generating a description of the transformation between them;

(4) use the transformation or difference description to generate commands to
the appropriate "actors" which result in commands to the display;

(5) alter the appropriate
key-frames and transformations and repeat steps (3) through (5) iñþ2³z<f the user criticizes
the resulting cartoon.
.end
	If at any point in the performance of steps 1 through 3 the system is confused
or forced to make too many unfounded assumptions, then a dialog would be initiated with
the user and advice would be sought.
.sec An Example
	An imaginary scenario
is perhaps appropriate here.  Suppose the system was told the
following story (based on the cartoon in Figure 1):
.begin narrow 8,8
	Charlie Brown walks up to Lucy and greets her by saying, "Happy New Year,
Lucy!"
The background is a grass field.
[fi](The system assumes that CHarlie Brown's arm is extended as a sign of greeting.)[fr]
	Lucy asks, "Does that make it so?" [fi](The system assumes that Charlie
Brown has a look of puzzlement and interest.)[fr]
 Lucy continues, "Does your saying, 'Happy New Year'
MAKE it happy?"
[fi]( The system assumes that Lucy's arms are upraised as sign of emphasis.)[fr]
 Lucy goes on, "Just because you SAY it does that
mean it WILL be?"
[fi](The system assumes that Lucy's arms are outstretched as sign of question.
It also assumes that Charlie Brown has a facial expression of confusion.)[fr]
	Lucy shouts, "IS THIS A GUARANTEE?  IS THIS ..."
	Charlie Brown says, "Oh, good grief."
[fi](The system assumes that Charlie Brown's famous "good grief" expression is on
his face.)[fr]
.end
	This may be translated into the following internal representation:foot<Note
that the details of this example are [fi]ad hoc[fr] since the system is at such an early
stage of development.>
.begin nofill
.skip
[fl] (Background grass-field)
 (key-frame 1
     (Charlie Brown (walking (towards Lucy))
	      (says "Happy New Year, Lucy!")
	      (greeting Lucy)
	      (arm extended)) ; [fi]an assumption based on the greeting[fr]
     (Lucy (standing (facing Charlie Brown))))
 (key-frame 2
     (Charlie Brown (standing (facing Lucy)))
     (Lucy (asks "Does that make it so?")))
 (key-frame 3
     (Charlie Brown (arm hand-on-chin)
	      (expression (and interest puzzlement)))
     (Lucy (arms up-stretched)
	   (expression emphatic)
	   (asks "Does ... ?")))
 (key-frame 4
     (Charlie Brown (expression confusion))
     (Lucy (asks "Just because ...")
	   (arms out-stretched)
	   (expression questioning)))
 (key-frame 5
     (Charlie Brown ())
     (Lucy (shouts "IS THIS A GUARANTEE?")))
 (key-frame 6
     (Charlie Brown (expression good-grief)
	      (says "Oh, good grief"))
     (Lucy (continues "IS THIS ...")))
.end
	The next task of the system is to fill into these key frames
important details that are not explicitly given by the text.
This is accomplished by
the use of defaults and very simple common sense inferences.
The "complete" descriptions of the key-frames follows (the <<...>>
is used to identify the assumptions made):
.begin nofill
[fl] (key-frame 1
     (Charlie Brown (walking (towards Lucy))
	 (says "Happy New Year, Lucy!)
	 (greeting Lucy)
	 (arm extended)
	 <<expression mildly-friendly>>
		;[fi]since a greeting is going on[fr]
	 <<position screen-left>>
		;[fi] this choice is arbitrary [fr]
	 <<orientation full-right-profile>>
		 ;[fi]based on the above position and "(walking (towards Lucy))"[fr]
	 <<clothes default-Charlie Brown>>
	 ;[fi]a smarter version would know that "Happy New Year" indicates (clothes winter))[fr]
     (Lucy (standing (facing Charlie Brown))
	   <<expression mild>>
	;[fi]either default or for contrast with future frames[fr]
	   <<arms down>>; standard default
	   <<position screen-right>>
	;[fi] since there are only 2 people and Charlie Brown already is "screen-left"[fr]
	   <<orientation full-left-profile>>
	;[fi] since she is facing Charlie Brown[fr]
	   <<clothes default-lucy>>))
.skip 2
  (key-frame 2
     (Charlie Brown (standing (facing Lucy))
	 <<arms down>>)
	;[fi] people get tired of extending their arms, so if nothing to the contrary[fr]
	;[fi] is indicated then they are lowered. The rest is "no-change"[fr]
     (Lucy (asks "Does that make it so?")))
	;[fi] the rest is "no-change" and so need not be repeated[fn]
.once indent 0
The other key-frames would also be expanded.
.end
	Next the transformations are created based on the
differences between the key frames.  The transformation between the first
two key-frames follows:
.begin nofill
.skip
[fl] (Transformation key-frame1 key-frame2
 	(Charlie Brown (walking standing)
	    (arms (one extended) down)
	;[fi] note there is no position change except implicit in "walking"[fr]
	    (talking listening))
	(Lucy (listening talking)))
.end
	This results in calls to the processes or actors involved. For example,
the transformation "(Charlie Brown (arms (one extended) down)),"
(which means that Charlie
Brown's arm changes from being extended in the first key-frame to where
both arms are down) becomes a call to the "actor" Charlie Brown with the message
"(arms down)".  This actor or process interprets the message,
making assumptions as necessary such as the use of his right arm.  The movement
would take into account rz4z@easonable speeds for such movements, easing in and out,
and the like.
	After the "actors" are called, a movie consisting of a list of commands
to the computer's display is produced which is shown.
At that point, the user may enter criticism or advice. Some
examples are:
.begin nofill
.skip
 "No, have Charlie Brown come in from screen right"
 "Charlie Brown should walk in front of the tree not behind it"
 "Ok, but the eyes in the last part should open wider."
 "Lucy turned her head too fast."
 "We should zoom in on Lucy when she shouts"
 "Half way through they should turn towards the 'audience'."
 "Something is wrong in the last scene but I don't know what."
 "Instead of the first cut try a fast dissolve."
.end
	It should be noted that understanding
and determining what should be done when such comments are entered
are very difficult problems.
.sec Key-Frames and Transformations
	Key-frames are film frames that characterize a scene.  They are
symbolic descriptions of who and what is on the screen and in what form.
They are essentially static descriptions of the world at a point in time.
When enough things change a new key-frame is generated.  For example, when the
"camera" position changes significantly or when the characters change
position, orientation, or expression significantly then a new key-frame
describing the new situation is created.  Of course, the new
key-frame typically inherits much of its description from the previous
frames.
	Transformations are descriptions of the differences
between two successive key-frames.  Some elements will correspond to
differences such as "before his arm was up and now it is at his side," while
most will correspond to less static descriptions of the change such as "his arm is being
lowered until it comes to his side."  Both of these examples indicate essentially
the same action on the screen; however, there are cases where only one kind of
description is appropriate.  For example, "he ran very fast up to Lucy" is best
represented as a transformation.  On the other hand, a tv being turned on
may best be described as initially blank and then having some particular
picture on it.  It should be noted that transformations are only partially
derived from key-frame comparisons, sometimes they will be part of the
initial story, for example, the story may have included the phrase, "he
jumped up and down."
.subsec Semantic Interpolation or Symbolic Difference
	Usually computer animation systems that can interpolate between
two scenes do so without any knowledge of what the objects are that
are being "in-betweened."  Usually a point (or a line or a parameterized curve)
in one scene is
a associated with a point (line or curve) in the other.  Then the creation of
in-between positions for that point (or line) is simple.  There are at least two
problems with this method though.  One, is that points in the source
are easily associated with
the wrong target points.   Two, natural motion it seldom produced.  Take as an example
a stick figure walking.  A good speed and rate of easing in and out for the
arms may not be the same for the legs.  The rest of the body is also moving
and yet not a constant rate.  Meanwhile there may be a breeze going through a tree
in the background that requires another rhythm.
	The proposed knowledgable animation system solves these problems in
two ways.  The interpolation is done not on the points or lines in the scene
but on a [fi]symbolic[fr] description of the scene.  That this use of symbolic
descriptions greatly simplifies the problem can be seen in many of the AI
programs of the last five or ten years.  Rather than comparing the points or
lines of, say, Charlie Brown's face, we can compare simple descriptions of his
eyes, mouth and ears.  The description of the differences between the symbolic
descriptions of two key-frames is also more useful than a lower-level description
in terms of points or lines.  It may describe his mouth as first happy and then
angry.  In that case, the procedure that knows about mouths in general and
the procedure that knows about
any peculiarities of Charlie Brown's mouth would then be in a good position to
generate the in-betweens for his mouth.  The knowledge of how an effective
change of expression appears is then brought to bear, rather than relying on
some general interpolation mechanism.   It should be noted that if several
body parts interpolate in a similar manner then it is easy for them to share
the same knowledge.
.subsec The Vocabulary of Key-frames and Transformations
	After it is decided that symbolic descriptions of scenes and transformations
between scenes will be used, the question arises as to what
the vocabulary for the objects, relations
and actions will be.  To get a feel for the problems involved was studied the drawings
of Charlie Brown's face in a couple of Schultz's Peanuts books.  The outcome was
encouraging for it seems as if almost any expression found in the books can be
constructed selecting eyes, ears, mouths, etz8zDc. from a small "catalog" of parts of the
head.  For example, see Figure 2 < use old diagram that Karen redrew>.
	Relations between objects and actions have not been investigated very much
yet.  Hopefully, we will find that only a relatively small number of descriptors
will suffice for the simple animation we have in mind.  The need for a small size
vocabulary of actions and objects
is because knowledge must be attached to each
descriptor about how to interpolate, how to
make reasonable default judgements, and the like.
One may object that by classifying objects, actions and relations into coarse groupings
that subtleties that have important effects on the viewer are lost.  This is a
possibility, but it is premature to propose a computer AI system that produces
cartoons of anywhere near professional
quality.  Any lack of subtleties, while unfortunate, will not significantly affect
the success of the system as a learning environment and as a prototype for subsequent
more advanced systems.  We must learn to walk before we attempt to run.
.subsec The role of the Episode
	An episode is a coherent piece of the cartoon, usually separated from
other episodes by a cut or dissolve.  It is within each episode that key-frames
are generated.  In order for reasonable defaults to be chosen, the system needs
to identify with each part of the story a sterotyped or generalized episode.  These
episodes are similar to what Schank calls "scripts"foot<Schank ...>
and Minksy calls "frames."foot<Minsky, M. ...>  For
example, the system may have sterotypes of episodes such as someone greeting another,
or of someone arguing with another, or of people playing baseball.  These structures
contain much information, thereby lessening the demands on the user to describe the
story in greater detail.  For example, once a story fragment is recognized as
an instance of a greeting, then
(unless there is reason to assume otherwise) the character
will raise his or her hand while saying the greeting and perhaps the other character
will nod.  In addition, the sterotyped episode would contain such defaults
as the normal duration of a greeting, the normal facial expressions and the like.
	An episode provides much of the details necessary to produce the calls to
the lower-level drawing and animation primitives.  There are, however, other sources
of defaults.   Continuing with the greeting example, suppose the episode indicates
that Charlie Brown should raise his arm.  The episode knows nothing special about
Charlie Brown and so should not decide whether Charlie
Brown should use his left hand or right hand.  This knowledge
would exist elsewhere, for example, in the description of a typical person which might
indicate that if one hand is carrying something use the other, if both are busy then
suggest that perhaps no hand should be raised, and if both hands are free then pick the
right arm.  Now it might be that Charlie Brown is left-handed and so should
act differently in the last case.  This information would be associated with Charlie
Brown and would override more general information about people.  In other words,
I envision the use of a hierarchy of knowledge and appropriate inheritance operators.
.subsec The Role of Animation Knowledge in this Process
	It is obvious that an animator, in addition to knowing about the real world,
knows much about animation techniques, many of which apply equally well to
computer animation as to conventional animation.  For example, the animator knows
how to ease into and out of a motion, how to exaggeration an action and how to produce
a more dramatic effect by providing contrast to anticipated actions.  Also, the animator
knows how to produce film effects such as dissolves, zooms, pans and cuts.  Probably
most important an animator knows when to apply these techniques.
	Clearly, I need to imbed these techniques and advice about their application
into the computer animator.  The first task I face is grouping these techniques into
categories.  For instance, dissolves, pans, zooms and cuts might all be grouped into
"camera" techniques.  Easing in and out and in-betweening perhaps should be grouped
as "motion" techniques.  Anticipation, exaggeration, and focusing
attention on center of
interest perhaps should be called "dramatic" techniques.
	[fi]Camera techniques[fr]
could be procedures within the computer that are suggested
by the episode sterotypes.  Perhaps the greeting episode would suggest a
pan from the person greeting towards the other at a medium "camera" distance.  The
argument episode may suggest that quick cutting and close-ups on the faces of the
debaters would be best.  Cuts would, in general, be used to separate episodes and so on.
These would only be suggestions and a computerized "aesthetics" critic may notice that
too many, say, dissolves have been going on and either veto the suggestion or propose
a cut as an alternative.
	The [fi]motion techniques[fr] would be procedures that are suggested
by the object being affected.  In thatz<** way trees would know how to react to wind
in a way that is different from how an arm would move or an eye close.  As is discussed
later all these objects are imbedded in a hierarchy which describes what class
each object is a member of, and in turn what class each class is a member of and so on.
For example,
Charlie Brown's left arm is an instance of Charlie Brown's arms, which are instances
of arms (if adult arms were different, another class could be inserted), which are a
kind of limb, which is a kind of body part, which is an object.  Any advice as to
the natural flow of an object is placed at the highest level of generality that is
appropriate.  If there is something special about arms that does not apply to legs
then that information is associated with arms.  In moving, say, Charlie Brown's left arm
advice is searched for starting with the instance of the arm, then to Charlie's arms,
then arms in general, and so on.  Any advice that contradicts,
in a straight-forward manner, advice that came from
a more specific element of the hierarchy is rejected.
	The problems of deciding when the [fi]dramatic techniques[fr] are appropriate is
similar to the problem discussed in the next section on how the cartoonist's knowledge
could be imbeded into the computer animator.
.subsec The Artist's or Cartoonist's Knowledge
	A very important aspect of animation is the drawings themselves considered as stills.
The cartoonist's understanding of
composition, balance, point of attention, contrast, and the like is important for
producing aesthetically pleasing cartoons.  These techniques along with the animator's
dramatic techniques need to be incorporated into the system; however at this point,
it is not too clear where.  Some of the knowledge can go into the "aesthetics" critics
mentioned earlier that reject or modify
suggestions based on what preceded and
on what follows the current frames.
In the process of filling in the key-frames this knowledge is used in a more positive way.
Contrast and point of attention will be called upon to help place
the characters and elements of the background
whose position is under-specified.  The episode will also
have suggestions about the use of these techniques,
for example, the argument episode knows that, in general, the end
of an argument is the most dramatic part and perhaps should be emphasized by contrast,
exaggeration and anticipation.
.sec Summary
	This research while promising is at very early stages.  The actor-based animation
system described in the appendix works but is in need of many improvements.  The nature
of key-frames, episodes, critics, and the vocabulary needed are being explored and much
remains to be done.  The educational aspect of the project is the most exciting part,
yet other than keeping it in mind, little can be done now.  Children will be using the
actor-based animation system this fall, and hopefully improvements will become obvious
and problems discovered at an early enough stage to do something about it.  But as
to whether the system will really work, and, if so, whether it will provide an new kind
of environment to learn about animation, story telling, communication and thinking,
remains to be seen.

Appendix will be added later

"TpHAn Investigation of the Use of my Actor-like Animation System
written in both 11Logo and Lisp-Logo.

I hope to find bugs of many sorts in my system.  The conceptual ones, the
parts of the system that are hard to understand are those that I am most
interested.  Also, I am interested as to how intuitive this system is and
how it compares with more convential animation in Logo.  The child will in
addtion to the normal Logo benefits hopefully learn something of ideas such
as instantiantion, inheirtence of properties, class membership and a little
man model of computation.
The design of the system is such that one frequently has to deal these concepts.

It is really hard to decide what part of all this I will end up exploring.  The
primary aspect of actors as message passing "little people" will be the first
thing that I hope to put across.  Instantiation would come next.  The definition
of new classep#rs would follow, ending up with parallel processes via the scheduler.

I hope to work with one or two children at a time, each working on some
project involving animation, with me being there to provide guidance and help
with problems.

I will need an ITS terminal or else will have to work with a very small version
of the system on the 11.

Two children sounds about right, initially, and maybe two more after a month or so.
Perhaps a one-to-one relationship for the first
few sessions.
The first kids should be 6th or 7th graders and if after a month of experience
I decide to handle two more kids, then younger would be better.

45 to 60 minute sessions 2 or 3 times a week would be ideal, the number of
sessions would hopefully be large, maybe 20, at least til Christmas.

Nothing special with respect to supervision is needed
other than someone to talk to about problems and ideas, occasionally.

The system is developed enough now on Lisp-Logo, though I will be doing more.
If I end up working on the 11 then I would need a couple weeks to get a reasonable
sub-system together.

AI people is the kind of audience I imagine
and a daily log with editorializing included in a paper about the animation system.

.subsubsec Linking

Also the key-frames could perhaps be more actor-like#hIP.sec Sessions using the System
	The purpose of the system is to be used by
elementary school children, as a supplement to the
usual Logo sessions.  For various reasons, I only
worked with one child, Rod. Rod was a 7th grader at the King
public school in Cambridge.  He was 13 years old,
during these sessions.  He came on Wednesdays and
Thursdays for sessions of between 45 minutes and one hour.
I worked with him on Logo from May 7th to June 12th.
.subsec The Wednesday May 14 Session
	After two previous sessions of rather standard Logo turtle
work making squares, flowers, windows, etc., Rod was introduced
to my talk system.  On the previous Thursday, I attempted to
show him the system,
but system problems quickly aborted that attempt. I started off
talking only about generation.  I asked him for an example of a
sentence that we could generate.  He said, "Bob has a cat."
Then I told him how to remember that an "S" can be that, literally.
He typed "Talk,"
(and after problems with the tree-drawing routines due to workspace
limitations) it typed back "Bob has a cat," of course.
	I then asked how could we get it to say something
different (other than by remembering another "S" literally).
We talked about saying "REMEMBER [S NAME VERB DET NOUN]"
Rod providing much of the content.  We had a discussion about
the word "a" and what to call it. Then I told him that we can use
other words for the noun, verb, etc. than in the original sentence. So he told me to type (since he is a slow typer) things like
[NOUN CAT], [NOUN COW],
etc.. All together he came up with about 10 "dictionary"
entries. He typed "Talk," and we saw about 4 or 5 variations on the original.
I then asked him how we could get it to make different kinds of sentences.
He was confused, then I asked him for an example of a sentence
and he suggested
"I went fishing today."  He quickly did a parts of speech breakdown
on it, but was unsure about "today."
He asked me if it is a noun and I said yes but a special kind
and we don't want it to say, "I went fishing cow."  I suggested
calling it a "TIME.NOUN".
	Then he had me type in [PRONOUN I], [VERB WENT],
[VERB FISHING], and [TIME.NOUN TODAY].
He forget about
defining the sentence and when he ran "Talk" he got, "Bob went
a cat" and he said that was wrong and realized that one of
the new verbs was being used in the wrong place in the original
sentence.  After a few more talks I asked why we were not
getting anything like the fishing sentence.  He didn't know and
I asked him if we had "Remembered" what "S" can become and then
he told me to type, "[S PRONOUN I VERB WENT VERB FISHING TIME.NOUN TODAY]".
I tried to ask if he was sure that was right and he
thought it was.
	A few "Talks" later we got, "I I went went has fishing today today".  He wasn't clear why it was happening.  At this
point I thought at least the grind-showtree feature might help
but again workspace limitations did us in.foot<This was unexpected on my part
since I neglected to take into account the portion of his workspace devoted
to teaching aids.>
Anyway I asked him
for the "S" that made the sentence and explained how it caused
the funny sentence. So we "Forgot" it and "Remembered"
the "right" version.
	The next "Talk" was "I fishing went today," to which he
smiled and said "I went fishing today". I asked him what did
it say and he repeated it and then said "oh."  I asked him why
it happened and he said it picked the wrong verb.  So I suggested
making different kinds of verbs.  For "went" after some discussion
we picked "going.verb" and he suggested "doing.verb" for "fishing".
	The next talk produced "Bob fishing a cat" and then it
was time to leave.
.subsec The Thursday May 15 Session
	Today the grind-showtree feature was winning so I explained it and it seemed to help.
ThezT first talk returned "I going.verb doing.verb today" and
he remembered we hadn't finished yesterday.  We "Remembered"
"went" and "fishing".
	A few "Talks" later we got "Gil went a cat" and Rod
said to remove the [VERB WENT].
The next talk was "I went
fishing today" and then Rod came up with "running," "jumping"
and "swimming" as "doing.verbs".  He also added "he" and "she"
as pronouns.  Then the sentence "Bob has a cat," the one
without any
structure, poped up 3 times and we agreed to "Forget" it.
	After a few more successful "Talks" we went on to
matching. I suggested we try to find someone's name in
sentences.  We came up with the following list:
.begin
.nofill
	1) My name is Ken.
	2) I am called Ken.
	3) They call me Ken.
	4) Ken is my name.
	5) I am Ken
.end
I explained how to write a pattern for the first one which is
[$ MY $ NAME IS ?NAME $]. He caught on quickly to
the idea that a "$" means anything (including nothing) can be there.  Since he had little experience with "Make" and "Thing"
when doing turtle Logo, he was a little confused about "?name".  The second one we did together (me helping when he got stuck)
and Rod did the rest by himself (except for asking a few specific
questions).
We tested the first one at Logo top level all worked well so
I suggested we "Teach".  I had to explain "If" and "Output"
and I think Rod was confused but after using it a while they
became clearer.  We first taught only the first two patterns
and tested it out and it did return the name when it should.
When nothing matched we got a "NE didn't output" error
message and without much explanation I simply said we
need to output a name (I picked the empty word which perhaps
was a mistake) if no patterns matched.
	We tested out the procedure and all worked well.  I then
suggested we put it in a procedure that asked for your name then
called our procedure (NE) and then said "hi" to the name found
in the user's response.  We were debugging that when the session
ended.
.subsec The Wednesday May 21 and the Thursday Sessions
	We did slight cosmetic changes to talk
loop procedure (HK) and tested it out a little.  At one
point Rod expected the thing to be much smarter than it could
be.  After about 10 minutes, Rod asked if we could work with
the display turtle.  For the rest of the session and the next
one (May 23), we worked on a procedure for drawing
a house, and then parameterizing by size so that we could make
a city, (about 12 houses of different sizes distributed on
the screen).
.subsec The Wednesday May 28 Session
	I suggested we combine the talk stuff
and the turtle stuff, so that one can give English commands
like "Draw a big house in the center" and it would.  We talked
about it for a while and got to work. I asked Rod for a
sentence to make a pattern of, and he said, "Build a small house."  We worked out a pattern for it, Rod being  a little
rusty a first.  I explained to him the "OR" feature and we
came up with the following pattern:
.begin nofill skip
[fl][$ :DRAW.WORDS $ A ?SIZE :HOUSEWORDS]
[fn]Where we previously did [fl]
MAKE "DRAW.WORDS [OR DRAW BUILD MAKE CONSTRUCT] and
MAKE "HOUSEWORDS [OR HOME BUILDING HOUSE TOWER].
.end
	We wrote a procedure called "She2" for size of He2, the name
of the house drawing procedure. It did the makes, and then used
the above pattern in a match, outputed the ":?size" or else
printed "I don't understand you" and returned the empty word.
We tested it and it failed to match "Build a small house".
Both of us were stumped, so I told Rod about "match.trace"
which if true switches on comments from the matcher as to why
something is failing to match.  It's message was strange,
it looked as if it should have worked and yet didn't. So I
assumed it was a bug in my code, tried a slight change in the
pattern, and the message this time was, "The remaining pattern
[OR DRAW BULID MAKE CONSTRUCT]
 doesn't match the rest of the object BUILD".  I was sure their were bugs in my code and was about
to give up when I noticed "bulid" had been typed
instead of "build" and pointed it out
to Rod. We fixed it and it worked.
	Next we wrote "Builder," the main loop that asked for
instructions and then interpreted the input, and tried to do it and
then looped.  So Rod decided that "big" would mean "He2 100,"
"small" would be "He2 20" and "Large" would be "He2 120". We
tested it and it worked except, we forgot to print a message
asking for a command, and it did not clear the screen before
drawing a new house.
	During this session we talked a bit about the problem
of location, however I suggested we put that off until we got
the rest working.  We also planned to fix it so that if one
does not give a size, e.g. "Draw a house," it would have a
pattern
for that and draw a medium size house.
.subsec The Thursday May 29 Session
	We continued work on "Builder". First Rod
tested it out and all worked fine. Then I suggested the
sentence "Make me a house" and it said that it did not understand.
Rod was confused about this bñþL÷zXut knew it had something to do
with not having "?Size". He understood that we should make
a pattern without "?Size" but was confused what to do with this
pattern. . We put it in She2 and had it return "[NO SIZE GIVEN"].
Then he was confused as to how to change "Builder". He seemed
to think that the computer understood "[NO SIZE GIVEN]".
Eventually he had it make a house of size 60. We also changed
large and big to be smaller to fit on the screen.
	He tried it out and it worked fine.  I asked him
what would happen if we said something like, "make me a tiny
house". He answered that it would say we hadn't defined "tiny".
I think he was confused as to whether Logo or "Builder" interprets the
sentences typed in.  A few times later he would type "CS" (clearscreen) to
Builder, either forgetting the mode he was in or else not understanding it.
	He wanted to stop "Builder" but had to use the "break"
key.  I suggested we put in "She2" a pattern for stopping
and we came up with "[$ [OR STOP QUIT] $]"
and then it would
return to top level Logo.
	Next I suggested we have "Builder" ask where to put
the house, instead of the middle and clearing the screen each time.
I asked him to write some sentences that would be answers to the
question of where to put it. He wrote down:
.begin nofill
	Build it to the right
	Build it on the top of the screne (sic)
	Build it in the middle
	Could you please build it on the bottom
	You may make it on the bottom
.end
	I then asked what word are we trying to get out
of each of these sentences. I suggested "right" for the
first one and he came up with the others.  We decided on
"?Place" for a name for that word.  After some discussion
we came up with the pattern:

"[$ [OR TO ON IN] THE ?PLACE $]"
.once indent 0
which worked fine. We wrote "Fplace" that matched it's
input against that pattern and outputed ":?place" or
the empty word (in addition to printing "I don't understand").
We tested it on one sentence and it worked.
	We changed "Builder" to call "Tof" instead of "Clearscreen".
We then taught "Tof" which printed "Where should I put it?"
and then called "Fplace" with "Request". It then went
through many "Ifs," checking the place found against the words:
"middle," "left," "right," "top," and "bottom".  For each we
moved the turtle the appropriate way.  For "left" and "right"
it moved the turtle over 50 steps, for the others it set the
turtle state in the appropriate part of the screen.
	He then tested it out and it worked rather well. At
one point he wanted to clearscreen, typed "CS" and it responded
"I don't understand," "Where should I put it?". We agreed
that was wrong and I suggested that if it did not understand
it should start Builder over again and Rod agreed.  We
changed it.
	Just before the end of the session Rod
tested it out again (I think Rod enjoyed using his program).  At one point
he responded "on top" to the question, "Where should I put it?".
It typed "I don't understand" twice and then asked for a new
command. We agreed to fix that next time.
.subsec The Wednesday June 4 Session
	Rod asked about Doctor.  He said he wanted to do a thing like it.
I asked for an example and he wrote the following conversation:
.begin nofill
c- I am the Doctor. Can I help you?
m- I have a problem.
c- Can I help you in any way
m- Yes, that's why I came to you.
.end
	So we started working on generating the first sentence.
We did:
.begin nofill
[S WHO.I.AM HELP?]
[WHO.I.AM I AM DOCTOR A.NAME]
[WHO.I.AM I AM A.THE.YOUR DOCTOR]
[A.THE.YOUR A]
[A.THE.YOUR THE]
[A.THE.YOUR YOUR]
[A.NAME ROD]
[A.NAME KEN]
[HELP? MSMC I HELP YOU?]
[MSMC MAY]
[MSMC SHOULD]
[MSMC CAN]
[SMMC MIGHT]
.end
.begin turn off "{}"
	We had a hard time thinking of names for "the," "your" and
"a" and later for "may," "should," "can" and "might". I was thinking
that the syntactic ability to have a disjunction (usually denoted
by "{}" in linguistics) would be very useful here.
It was looking like a lot of work that
neither of us felt like doing. I asked where he heard about Doctor
and he said a friend talked to it.
.end
	So I started up Logo's
doctor and he had a long conversation with it. He seemed to
enjoy it very much.  Then I asked him if he was interested in
how it worked and he gave me a rather enthusiastic "yes".
	So we spent the rest of the session going over the
code, Rod seemingly understanding it rather well.  I gave
him a listing to look over, saying that  maybe next time we will try to
modify it in small ways.
.subsec	The Thursday June 5th Session
	Due to some confusion on Logo's part the session
was only 20 - 25 minutes.  When Rod came in a blocks world
program happened to be running on a console with no one there.
Rod asked about it, and we noticed (I had never seen it before),
that it was a mini-SHRDLU.foot<Winograd, Terry Procedures
as a Representation for Data in a Computer Program for
Understanding Natural Language, MAC TR-84, February 1971>
Rod spent the next 15 to 20 minutes
interacting with zP&6the program.  He told it to put blocks at
various places, and then asked it what it did earlier and why.
We stopped it and started looking at the code, getting the
general idea, but not being too successful.  With the remaining
five minutes Rod wanted to talk to doctor again and did.
.subsec The Thursday June 12th Session
	Rod came in while I was working on my
animation system and asked what I was doing.  I spent about
5 or 10 minutes showing him how it worked.
	Then I asked if he wanted to work on Builder some more.
Initially, he forget much of how it worked and what it does, but
soon was using some of the variation in commands that we had
built into the system.  At an early point, in response to the
question, "Where should I put it?," Rod typed, "in the center".  To which Builder replied, "I don't understand center" and
proceeded to build it in the center anyway.  I asked him if he
knew why it did that; he said, "no".
	We then started searching for the code that printed
that message.  We found that "Tof" understood "middle, right,
left, top and bottom," but not "center".  I then tried to
explain "memberp" to Rod and suggested we change the
':PLACE = "MIDDLE'
phrase to 'MEMBERP :PLACE [MIDDLE CENTER]'.
Rod only partially understood, I think, but we made the change
and he knew it would work now.  He typed, however, "Make me a
house in the center" and it responded "I don't understand you".
I asked him why and he was confused.  I gave an explanation
and suggested we fix it later. Rod then built some more houses
and when he used "right" I asked him if that was what he thought
it should do, and we decided it should be more to the right.
We then changed the code for "right" and "left".  Rod then
built some more houses and time ran out.  I gave him a listing
of the workspace and suggested he look at it and see if he
could figure out how to change it so that you can say the
location in the same sentence that you say to build it.
	There was no next time, Logo was down Wednesday
and Thursday.
&6T'Lt is not the typical input to a natural language
system it should not matter much.  The rules as written
assume the user immediately previous had been asked for
his or her name.  The rules do not distinguish between
sentences like "My sister's name is Karen" and "My name is
Ken" since the rule on line 30 will match both.  It could have
been written to check for "my" and allow no adjectives between
"my" and "name" or alternatively bind up anything in that
position using the "$<name>" type pattern and then look in that
cell for information.
	This parser is quite powerful, however, it remains to
be seen how convenient and understandable it is in use with
kids.  I found it easy to write patterns, to add new ones when
the system misunderstood me so there is hope.  This kind of
parser was chosen because of it's simplicity and ease of
implementation (most other sorts of parsers would be difficult
to implement in light of Logo's space and time limitations).
In addition, pattern matching is perhaps a powerful idea in it's
own right.
.require "ken;$rod >" source
.sec Summary and Evaluation
	Overall I was quite satisfied with the system while
working with Rod.  I feel it was a good experience for him,
however, I feel I was pushing him a little too hard.  Rod
did much of the programming but much of it he only partially
understood and I tried to explain.  Part of the problem
here is that I was interested in the language aspects of
programming.  I skimmed, or quickly passed over, much of
Logo, concerning control structure and variables. As a
result, Rod was confused about those aspects, but seemed
to pick up quite quickly the writting of generation rules
and patterns for parsing.  The remaining doubts I have
relate to the incidents where Rod expected much more
intelligence from his program than was possible.  The
simplest explanation is that he was not understanding
things fully, however at other times he gave me the directly
opposite impression.
	This document describes a system for writting
natural language programs. Both parsing and generative
capabilities are provided. Sessions with one child are
described, however,
it remains to be seen how
well it works with many children, and what they will get out
of interacting with it.
.skip 4
	I would like to thank Seymour Papert and Jon Allen for
expressing interest in this project and providing suggestions.

.subsubsec Special Features of the Generator
	In addition to providing procedures for defining,
using, finding and removing context-free rules the system
provides debugging aids.  The most important one draws the
derivation tree on the display as it runs.  This is useful
for seeing where the output came from and finding bugs in
the rules.  Another important use of the tree drawing routines
is that it makes expansion's control structure
visible and relatively easy to follow.  The hope is that the
rules are less abstract when a clear visual trace of their application is
available.
	Since for reasonable size sentences the parse trees become too
complex and require "Startdisplay 1" (which only one user at a time can have)
a different mechanism is sometimes needed.
This saves up the tree and if the proper switch is set will
print on the console a pretty printed version of the tree.  This
feature should always win and can be run after the fact (if the
tree is kept around).
	Another debugging aid prints out the contents of a chunk
in a reasonable format.  This is especially important if one
defines rules at top level (as opposed to within procedures)
though I would not recommend doing this often (since editing
can presently be done only by either forgeting the bad rule
and remembering the new one or, better, by erasing the chunk and editing
the procedure that defines that chunk and then re-running the
procedure).
.subsubsec Problems with the generator
	Some small problems that remain are questions of
representation.  Should there be any syntactic distinction
between terminal and non-terminal symbols? (Presently the
terminals are defined as those that have no rules about
how to expand them.) Should noise words such as "can.be"
be encouraged.
	One of the big problems that remain is how to explain
generation to children.  One procedure that I presently favor
is asking the child for a few sample sentences to generate that
mean roughly the same thing and then look for similarities
in the structure (see, for example, the greeting chunk above).
At some point the kid will realize that, for example,
one doesn't have to use the word "hello" but any word that
can be used in it's place such as "hi" or "good day."
This paraphrasing approach worked pretty well with the child
I worked with.
	Another issue that my generator brings up is whether
context free generation is a good paradigm for generation.
Maybe something that uses features extensively would be better.
Or perhaps a canonical internal representation should be
developed and then the generator could translate that to English,
the choices along the way providing the variety by paraphrasing
(similar to  Schank's MARGIE system foot<Schank R. and Colby K.
editors,
Computer Models of Thought and Language,
W.H. Freeman and Company (San Francisco 1973)>).
The major reason
I have for choosing context-free generation is that it is simple
and clean and hopefully easy to explain and learn.
.subsec The "parsing" portion
	The parser is a rather fancy pattern-matching routine
called "Match."  The first argument is the object which is matched
against the pattern (which is the second argument).
If the pattern matches the object then "True" is returned else "False."  In
addition certain side effects of the match can be obtained. The
pattern is a sentence where each element is any of the
following (the "<...>" are meta-linguistic):

$<name> -----which means 0 or more words

?<name> -----which means 1 word

<word>----which matches only that word

[or <word1> <word2> ...]----which matches only if one of the words do

:<name>--- do the above after replacing this element with it's thing

[<any list that does not begin with "or>]---recursively match this against the next item

	Where <name> is optional and it is a word that is bound
(make-d) into whatever was in that position of the object.
The following is a procedure that finds most of the ways one can
say one's name and returns the name found.  The input to
the procedure is presumably the sentence the user typed in.
.begin
.nofill skip
[fl]TO LOOK.FOR.NAME :ANS
10 IF MATCH :ANS [$ [OR NOT ISNT ISN'T] $] SAY "NOT.NOT.YOUR.NAME OUTPUT []
[fi]!this checks for the words "not," "isnt" or "isn't" preceded
!or followed by anything and if so says appropriate message[fr]
20 LOCAL "?NAME [fi]!AM ONLY LOOKING FOR THE FIRST NAME WORD
!If "$name" where used the list corresponding to the whole name would be found[fl]
30 IF MATCH :ANS [$ NAME IS ?NAME $] OUTPUT :?NAME
40 IF MATCH :ANS [$ I $ CALLED ?NAME $] OUTPUT :?NAME
50 IF MATCH :ANS [?NAME $ IS $ NAME $] OUTPUT :?NAME
60 IF MATCH :ANS [?NAME $ IS $ CALLED $] OUTPUT :?NAME
70 IF MATCH :ANS [I AM ?NAME $ ] OUTPUT :?NAME
80 IF MATCH :ANS [?NAME] OUTPUT :?NAME [fi]
!picks up any one word answer[fr]
90 OUTPUT [] [fi]!no name found[fr]
END
.END
	Notice that this parser will accept sentences that
are non-grammatical or non-sensical if they match the pattern.
Since thaz`P\problems than
other domains. It can be as simple or as complex as one
wants, at all levels interesting things can be done.
One of the goals of Logo is to make children's thinking
about thinking more explicit.  Clearly language use is an
important part of thinking.  Lastly it can be fun writing
programs that talk to people.
.sec Some Applications
	The list of applications is endless since language
is used in so many activities.  Some suggestions follow:

	1) A Family understander---a system that asks and
answers questions about the user's family.  The domain's
semantics are rather simple (e.g. if two males have the
same parents then infer that they are brothers).  Typically
the system would ask about the user's relatives, their names,
ages, etc. and be able to answer questions by looking up the
answers or making simple deductions.  This is a good place
for the language system to interact with my Logo data base
system described in another paper.
	2) A display command interpreter.  This is similar
to Ira Goldstein's ideas on envisioning systems.  Basically
the child would write a system that accepted commands like,
"Draw a circle in the center of the screen" or "Move the
square called 'ralph' into the circle in the middle" (or
simplier versions of the same).  Again this would interact
with the data base system for keeping track of what objects
exist and their attributes.  As described later, a simple
version of this was done by a 7th grader.
	3) A time reference understander.  This one I find
interesting because it flows directly out of my master's
thesis.  The idea is to write a question
answering system that accepts declaratives like "yesterday
I had a cold" and "my birthday is January 25" and then
answer questions and make simple inferences.  This is similar
in flavor to the first item, the family understander.
.sec What the system looks like and how to describe it
.subsec The generative portion
	The sub-system that generates sentences uses
user-supplied context free rules. The following is an
example of a set of rules for generating a greeting:
.begin
.nofill

[fl]TO REMEMBER.GREETING
10 SET.CHUNK "GREETING [fi]!chunks keep rules separate[fr]
20 REMEMBER [GREETING CAN.BE HELLO.EXP WHAT.NAME]
30 REMEMBER [GREETING CAN.BE WHAT.NAME]
40 REMEMBER [HELLO.EXP CAN.BE HELLO.WORD THERE]
[fi]!"there" is a terminal since there are
!no rules as to how to expand it[fr]
50 REMEMBER [HELLO.EXP CAN.BE HELLO.WORD]
60 REMEMBER [HELLO.WORD CAN.BE HI]
70 REMEMBER [HELLO.WORD CAN.BE HELLO]
80 REMEMBER [WHAT.NAME CAN.BE WHAT IS YOUR NAME?]
90 REMEMBER [WHAT.NAME CAN.BE WHAT ARE YOU CALLED?]
END
.END
	The command 'SAY "GREETING' will look
in the "greeting" chunk for rules that begin with the word
"greeting."  In this case it finds two rules and picks one
at random and then recursively expands each of the constituents
until no rules are found and that word is assumed to be a
terminal (i.e. what one prints).  For example, this grammar
will produce among others: "Hello there what is your name?"
and "Hi what are you called?"
The noise word "can.be" is
something I'm not too sure about.  It makes clearer what
one is saying but with most kids typing is already too much
of a problem to impose such niceties. Perhaps it should be
optional.
	Chunking of the rules into separate areas is very
handy here.  The rules for say, "greeting," should not, unless
intentionally specified otherwise, conflict with rules for say,
"glad.to.know."  This makes modularity the default.  Also
it serves the rather practical function of providing units that
can get swapped in and out of the workspace depending on recency
(so that "larñtbëp`.sec Why a Logo Natural Language System
	It is far from a new idea to apply Logo
to natural language understanding and generating.
I designed a system, however,  for doing both in way
that is hopefully adequate, clear, clean, modular,
convenient, and easy to use.  The sentence generation
is done by context-free grammars.  Sentence understanding
is done by pattern matching.  Using this
system for writing simple "doctor" programs should be relatively easy.
	Why do natural language at all?  Natural
language is a domain at least as rich as turtle geometry.
It more naturally leads to AI type p age System[fr]

ô[d.sec Abstract
	The application of the understanding of intelligence
that has arisen from AI research can be applied to education
to significantly enhance learning.  LOGO project, described
herein, is based on this premise.  Extensions of the LOGO
experience are proposed that are based on AI in interesting ways.
One extension is to encourage children to write simple AI
programs.  Tools to aid the child in this endeavor are discussed.
Another supplement to LOGO is the interaction of the child
with AI programs specially designed for this purpose.  These
programs, in addition to being easily used by children, should
be modifiable by children in interesting wayzhs.  The specifications
of such a program are described.
.sec Introduction
	This paper describes some ideas concerning three different
ways in which AI can play a role in education, which are
illustrated in Figure 1.  Children can both be encouraged to
write simple AI programs and to interact with specially
designed AI programs.  Both of these interactions with AI are
guided by the understanding of intelligence and learning that
has arisen from AI research.  These ideas are within the
framework of the LOGO project at MIT.  LOGO is
concerned with the more general problem of enhancing education
by applying the concepts that have come from AI research.
A description of how LOGO attempts to do this is described
in the next two sections.  Following that are some of
the author's ideas as to how AI can enhance education in
ways that supplement LOGO's.
.sec The LOGO Philosophy
	One of the main occupations of a child is to learn.
Yet, typically
no effort is made in schools to encourage the child to learn how
to learn, or even what this means.
The explicit teaching of general principles of
learning, at least without many examples, does not accomplish
this task.  The theory, to the child, is abstract and difficult
to understand----even more difficult to use.
	The child should, instead, acquire some good
"powerful ideas" foot<As they are called by Seymour Papert>
for
learning and problem solving.  The discovery of "powerful ideas"
is central to AI research and some have been developed.  For
instance, the notion of "linearization", or the strategy of
attacking the sub-problems of a problem independently, and then
dealing with the interactions is a powerful idea.  Another one
is to view "bugs" in a solution as things to correct and learn
from, not mistakes that lower a test score.  These
ideas are typified in Sussman's HACKER.foot<Sussman, G. J.,Sussman, G. J., A Computer Model of Skill Acquisition,
American Elsevier Publishing Company, Amsterdam, 1975.>
Some other powerful ideas are "naming of concepts", "planning",
and "being explicit about one's own thinking".  Most important
of all the powerful ideas is the notion of a powerful idea itself.
	If children could really learn these powerful ideas they
would presumably be better students and better problem solvers.
But how are they to learn these ideas? What is needed is an
environment where these notions surface frequently, where one
needs to be explicit about problem solving, an environment
where the problems are often found to be intrinsically interesting,
an environment
where the children constantly use powerful ideas to accomplish
their goals.  Programming is one such environment.  "Linearizing"
becomes "implementing subroutines independent of other subroutines". "Naming of concepts" becomes "naming subprocedures and
variables".  Debugging is an important component of programming.
	There are other beneficial effects of choosing programming
as an environment which if properly created can help the child become
a better student.  These are:
.begin indent 5,5
	1) The experience of working on a long-term project,
solving sub-problems and planning as one goes
	2) The experience of having explicit control over one's
environment
	3) The experience of learning about the domain to which
the child's programs apply
	4) The experience of finding that mathematics can be
useful.
.end once indent 0
Equally important is that children usually find it fun.
	The reader should note, however, that there is no claim
made here that programmers are any smarter, or any better learners, than other people.  It
is not the act of programming that encourages one to pick up
powerful ideas, rather it is programming well chosen problems,
within a language that encourages powerful concepts (such as
recursion), and with proper guidance by a teacher.
	Computers provide a rich environment for the child to
develop concepts of problem solving and learning.  Other
environments, however, are also useful towards this end.
The learning of physical skills, such as circus arts, constitute
such an environment.  The main thesis is that
the right verbal description of a physical skill does aid
greatly in learning it.
Also, interaction of learning in this domain and programming is
rich.  The child can learn that the powerful ideas learned while
programming are useful for more everyday activities.
.sec The LOGO Language
	The LOGO language was developed to provide the kind
of environment in which children can learn to learn.  It is a
programming language that is human engineered (or more accurately
"child engineered"). The structure of the language, the
primitives, and their names were designed to aid in the kind of
conceptual thinking described above.
	An important part of LOGO is a form of computational
geometry called "Turtle Geometry".  A turtle is a computational
entity with a state consisting of a position and a heading.
A turtle accepts commands, telling it move forward or backward,
that change its position.  Itñþcwzl also accepts commands to
turn left and right, thereby changing its heading.  Turtles are
usually realized as physical devices called "floor turtles" or
as "cursor" on a display.  Turtles also have a pen that can leave
a trail as they move.
	As an example, let us consider a turtle procedure for
drawing a polygon.  The procedure expects two inputs, the
size of each side and the amount the turtle should turn after
drawing each side. More details are shown in Figure 2.
	Children can prove Turtle Geometry theorems that
are useful, not only for proving more theorems, but for writing
programs.  A theorem called the "Total Turtle Trip Theorem" is an example.  It states that if a turtle takes a trip
and ends up in the same state (position and heading) in which
began, then the total amount turned right (minus that turned left)
is a multiple of 360 degrees.  Children usually understand
the intuition of the proof when asked to think about a broken
turtle that could not move, only turn.  This theorem is very useful
in writing state independent subprocedures.

	The previous sections describe the LOGO Laboratory
as background for what follows.  The rest of this paper deals
with ideas and research of the author that is consistent
with the
LOGO framework, but relies more heavily on AI research.
.sec AI for Children
	There are several good reasons why children should
write and interact with AI programs, a few of which follow:
.begin indent 5,5
	1) Children are encouraged to think explicitly about
how they solve problems.  Hopefully the children will thereby
improve their ability to describe and understand their own
thoughts.
	2) The problem domain to which the AI programs
are applied is learned, and in a
new and perhaps better way
	3) If children are to program, then AI can be an
interesting open ended problem domain for that programming
	4) The children will learn about AI which is a
subject, in the opinion of the author, that is as important
as spelling or history.
.end once indent 0
As an example, consider a child writing a simple natural
language understanding system. The child will hopefully
learn much about computational linguistics and something about
how he or she talks and listens.
	To facilitate AI programming of, for example, natural
language understanding, or common sense systems, the proper
primitives (from the child's point of view) need to be provided.
For this purpose the author has developed a set of programs
LOGO called "LAIL" for LOGO AI Language.  LAIL contains a set of operators useful for writing AI programs.
Currently LAIL consists of the following tools:
.begin indent 5,5
	1) A powerful pattern matcher for natural language understanding
	2) A context-free generator for sentence generation
to which the child provides rules.
	3) A relational data base handler facilitates
memory and inference
	4) An actor-like animation system which is described later for animation that is described later.
.end once indent 0
Most important of all, these
tools should be simple, powerful, natural, and encourage
the
right kind of conceptual thinking.
.sec An Actor-like Animation System
	Animation is a programming domain for children in which
I am particularly interested. My dissatisfaction with the
present animation primitives in LOGO led to the design and
implementation of an animation system written in LOGO.  Each
object in the system is very similar to an "actor" in Carl Hewitt's formalism.foot<Hewitt, C., et. al. "A Universal Modular ACTOR Formalism",
IJCAI-73, Stanford, August, 1973>  An actor is a computational
entity that can receive and transmit messages.  Each object in
my system can accept turtle-like commands (e.g. forward),
remember items told to it, and be taught how to handle new kinds
of messages.  Each object has a set of patterns which are matched
against the incoming message.  If a pattern matches then the
action associated with that pattern is invoked.  Parallel
movements of objects on the display screen are handled by
an actor called a "scheduler".  When the scheduler is sent
a message asking it to run, it passes those messages it has
associated with the time, redisplays the screen, increments
the time, and loops.  The scheduler can also produce
actors called "movies" that can show a cartoon at any
reasonable speed.
An example
of the use of this system is shown in Figure 3.
	This view of programming as collections of actors, or
a community of "little people", that send and receive messages
from each other is very powerful.  It is conducive to a modular,
simple, natural representation of the knowledge needed for the
application.  The model for intelligence can be
a community as easily as it can be an individual with an
actor system.
	Another AI aspect of this system is the explicit
"kind-of" hierarchy of actors.  Each object is told what class
it is a member of when it is created.  When any object receives
a message it cannot handle it passes the problem on to the class
of which it is a member.  An example of szdzpuch a tree is shown
in Figure 4.  The important concepts of instantiation, class
membership, placement of knowledge at the best level of
generality, inheritance of properties, and exceptions hopefully
flow from the proper use of this aspect of the system.
.sec An Intelligent Animation System
	Another environment for learning powerful ideas is one
in which the child interacts with specially designed AI programs.
I am in the process of designing an intelligent animation system
as a test of this hypothesis.  In the normal mode of interaction the
child will tell a story concerning the "Peanuts" characters,
and the system produces an animated cartoon based on the story.
The child can then describe the modifications he or she desires.
The system produces a new cartoon and the process iterates
until the child is satisfied (or quits).  The usual concepts of
linearization, debugging, and the like prevail here without the
need to learn a computer language.
Hopefully the
child will learn about animation, film making, and story telling
by writing stories and scripts to produce the desired cartoons.
An hypothetical example of a conversation with this system is
shown in Figure 5.
	Another more important way in which a child could interact with
this planned system is by understanding and modification of the
knowledge of the system.  The system's knowledge about
animation techniques, the real world, common sense facts about
people and things, and the "Peanuts" characters will be as
modular and simply represented as possible.  The child, with
the help of the system, will be able to define new characters
for the stories, change the system's vocabulary, add a reasonable
default course of action and so on.
	One hope is that in understanding and modifying the
knowledge of an intelligent system the children will actually
see the knowledge of something actually accomplish something
complex.  The idea that knowledge and intelligence can be
something formal should help the children to be more explicit
about their own thoughts and knowledge.  The use of the system
will also, hopefully,
help provide a vocabualary for talking and thinking about one's
thoughts.
If the design and implementation of this intelligent
animation system is done well, it
will provide a rich and exciting supplement to LOGO's
traditional environment.
.sec Summary
	In this paper three ways in which AI can interact
with education have been described.  After an introduction
to LOGO thinking and language, the benefits of children
writing simple AI programs using the proper tools were
described.  Finally, the ways in which an AI system
designed for education can interact with children were
discussed.  These ideas should be implemented and tested
with children.  Only then will the effects on education
be known.

User: Charlie Brown is walking and meets Lucy.  He says,
"Good morning".  She says, "What's so good about it? ...".
Charlie Brown frowns and says, "Good Grief!".

The system shows the cartoon after having made many simple
inferences and default choices.  For example, it decides where
to put Lucy and Charlie Brown, how fast they walk, how they
are oriented, their facial expressions, and so on.

System: How was that?

User: Ok, but Lucy should look crabby when she says, "What's
so good about ...".

The system changes the necessary parts of the cartoon and
shows the new one.

User: That's fine
.end

.ce
1The Peanuts Animation System0
.sp
	The goal of this research is a computer system which
in response to an english description of a story
creates an animated cartoon corresponding to that story.
Initially the characters of the story will be limited to
Schultz's Peanuts characters and rather simple activities.
The reasons for creating such a system are many and varied.
.sp
1) Educational---this could provide a different and
stimulating environment for children to learn to
express themselves well, to learn, in the role of a
director, about animation and film, to learn how
to criticize their own work in order to improve it,
and to get experience working on relatively long
term projects.
.sp
2) Artificial Intelligence---This system would be a
good domain to work on problems of real world knowledge,
common sense, and interactions of many different sources
of knowledge such as, animation techniques, cartooning
techniques, the Peanuts characters and thier world,
real world knowledge, human emotions and how they
appear, and the story given by the user.
.sp
3) Entertainment---This may be the first time that
animation would be an active event in people's
activities as opposed to rather passive viewing.
.sp
4) Computer graphics and animation---It will be the
first time that a computer animation system knew
what it was doing.  The proposed system would know
what all the characters look like, how they move around,
and what it was drawing at all times.  It would also know
a good deal about animation and cartooning
techniques and that why it was using any particular one.
It would know about in-betweening, composition, perspective
dramatic effects such as anticipation and exaggeration,
and flow of motion.  (By "know" I mean that it would
use the knowledge when appropriate, in this case for
defaulting when not told otherwise about a decision,
for explanation of what it is doing and why (which is very
important from the educational point of view) and for
criticism (in the form of warnings) when something isn't
the way it thought it should be (e.g. not much action,
the center of interest is too close to the corner, etc..))
.ce
1My Own Background0
	In conventional animation---none.  I have read about
4 or 5 books on the subject (and many more about cartooning)
and have always enjoyed viewing it. However I can not draw
very well but am willing to learn. In film and TV---I
studied cinema for a year at the University of Stockholm
about 3 years ago.  Besides reading and viewing, I had alot
of practice with video as camera person, director, editor,
actor, etc..
	In computer graphics and animation---Four or five
years ago I was very involved in "computer art" using a plotter
and taught a non-credit course on the subject.  More recently
I been involved with LOGO at MIT and have done some work
on computer graphics and animation with fifth graders.  I am
presently involved in a project to design animation primitives
to ease the task of teaching children how to write animation
programs.
.end
